unique_experiences: • Writing blogs for StayRunning and working with a group of volunteer writers • Writing informational guides on specific running topics, such as running apps • Creating and running targeted Google ads for promotional events • Organizing and executing themed events (StayRunning March Madness Challenge, StayRunning May 4th Challenge, etc.)
surprised: The conversion rate for Google ads is really low, so doing any marketing or promotional work for a non-profit is hard and requires heavy social media presence.
takeaways: Management is probably the biggest part of running and working in a non-profit because you have to plan out all of your projects and reach out to many people outside of the organization for opportunities.
mentor_name: Mrs. Mathewson (My situation was kind of unique because Mrs. Mathewson was my mentor and she challenged me to learn new skills related to the business side of running a non-profit.)

description: Helped create educational material for Mrs. Alvarez's class and put together an officer manual for Interlake HOSA.
unique_experiences: Doing research for and creating an LGBTQ+ resource guide.
surprised: The variety and amount of paperwork involved in running a club.
takeaways: The more personal care and creativity you put into an assignment, the better. There's always leeway to think of new ideas. Also, good communication is very, very important.

description: The position of a teaching assistant at Interlake High School for the HL Physics class, teaching students basic physics principals and grading activities. My time as a TA allowed for me to see another aspect to the education system, grading and providing feedback on student work. This forced me to have a deep understanding of the subject and also refresh the knowledge from the year prior.
unique_experiences: • Seeing different types of student responses. • Utilizing different grading software such as Excel, Teams and Turnitin. • Designing answer keys and activities for students.
surprised: What surprised me the most was the length of time and dedication that goes into grading that many activities and assignments.
takeaways: This was my first time working as a teaching assistant, so I learned a lot about what was expected of the position. In the end my greatest takeaway is how difficult a teachers job is, and how many different aspects there are to the job such as creating activities and also being diligent in grading an enormous amount of work.

description: This internship would be well-suited for students who like working with other students and enjoy computer science. Through this internship, I primarily worked with individual AP CS students to help solidify their understanding of CS concepts. Every week, I hosted tutorials for students to drop by with questions. I also helped with providing feedback on chapter projects.
unique_experiences: Assisted teacher with class organization through exploring new technology.
surprised: It was great to see the number of students who reached out and were proactive.
takeaways: As a student, we are often used to focusing on ourselves or our own growth/understanding. However, as a teaching assistant/helper, I learned to hear students out which allowed me to understand where they were struggling and guide them in a better way. I also learned how to engage students in relevant discussions to structure their problem-solving approach and think about the "why" or the "how" behind CS problems so that they could apply their learning to a wider range of problems.

description: Hopelink works to provide essential services to the community, such as housing assistance, food assistance, and transportation services. My time at Hopelink was almost entirely within the food bank section, although if you want to gain experience in other departments of Hopelink, Kassie is happy to connect you to those departments. My internship tasks included logging large donations from grocery stores, helping to create a COVID-19 handbook of processes and operations, and creating a system to track the hours of volunteers. For an in-person internship, additional tasks may include interacting with clients, preparing food orders, and sorting incoming food orders and donations.
unique_experiences: • Working with and around food bank resources helps give you a perspective on what those from different backgrounds and walks of life have to deal with and what resources they have. • Being able to pitch and complete your own projects helps develop professionalism as well as teaches you how to start and manage a project. • If in person, interacting with clients will also help give you a broader perspective on different cultures and groups in Bellevue.
surprised: I was surprised most at how much a food bank can do with so little resources. Hopelink manages to serve hundreds of families per week with surprisingly few resources, as well as the help of a lot of donations and volunteers.
takeaways: How much the work of volunteers matters. With around 500 total hours of volunteer work per month (across all volunteers), Hopelink is able to help feed upwards of 2000 families. Volunteer work is incredibly important to Hopelink as they have more limited resources than for-profit businesses such as grocery stores, and the work of a few volunteers can have a really large impact on the community.

description: I founded a non-profit organization called Baig Foundation, which currently focuses on automating the process of initiating and growing non-profit or community service projects. This free service in action provides experience-based guidance, technological resources like physical and digital labs, curated lists of potential partners and venues, as well as accessible funding to individuals interested in social welfare. This project consisted of three major tasks: one being the development of a platform and image to share the service to the public, two being expanding the capabilities of the service in funding amount, potential venues, and technological resources, and three being testing the service on real community service projects.
unique_experiences: • Website development and user interface design • Salesforce Administrator training on the trailhead, a free Salesforce learning platform, earning project certifications from Salesforce as an App Customization Specialist, and learning how to use Salesforce as a tool to amplify the impact of Baig Foundation's non-profit projects. • Initiated and led the North Star Project, a non-profit project focused on developing highly effective respirators and personal protective equipment in response to the COVID pandemic. Using Baig Foundation's network of partners, North Star was able to donate in-house 3D printed respirators tested to a P100 filtration efficacy to COVID testing centers from Seattle to Spokane. • Initiated and led the Rhythm & Sons Project, a non-profit project focused on providing free piano lessons to local low-income youth. During the pandemic, private piano classes were taught virtually. Successfully taught three students to play piano. Currently, Rhythm & Sons is pivoting by planning a virtual music performance for assisted living and elderly home residents. This project is based on the goal to enable people to use music for the greater good, as I believe music can unite people from all walks of life and bring joy to those in the dark. • Initiated the School.com project, a non-profit project focused on using Salesforce technology to create a COVID management system in a school setting. Developed a demo to test the idea. But after Marc Benioff, Salesforce's CEO, announced Salesforce would be developing the same service, I put School.com on the backburner. • Development of the Baig Foundation business plan, including the business description, business model and consumer facing service overview.
surprised: Government interaction, especially with the IRS, takes way too long. Receiving official documentation of incorporation took a month or so, and it has been nearly 4 months since I applied for 501c3 certification, and the IRS hasn't responded since. The pandemic is definitely not helping the Government process and authorize these applications, but the Government really needs to automate some of their systems.
takeaways: The workplace is always on fire, in my case I was the person lighting it on fire, meaning I had more work than I could handle, and I consistently made lofty goals that I reasonably couldn't achieve. But if you can work on something deeply fulfilling, all the struggle and work will be worth it.

description: Acres of diamonds works with homeless women and their children to help them get back on their feet. At my internship, I primarily worked with the children. Plan activities for them to do after school, then carry them out. There are volunteers and employees to help as well.
unique_experiences: • Working directly with kids in need • Creating a behavior tracking system to determine whether a child is improving over time • Tailoring activities specifically for certain kids.
surprised: How extensive Acres' programs were in helping the women get back on their feet
takeaways: How much more difficult it is to navigate things online. I volunteered there in person over the summer, and everything was far smoother. Communication was simple, and it was very easy to ask follow up questions. Working from home though made each of these things have huge delays, slowing work by hours, if not days.

description: Helping Mr. O'Byrne with various activity-making tasks, such as webassigns and shadow plots. This may vary from intern to intern.
unique_experiences: I discovered the processes of deconstructing a real-life event (shadow plots) into a set of equations via excel. I also learnt the coding behind Webassign pages. These may vary by person/task.
surprised: The amount of preparation that goes into everyday physics lessons and homework.
takeaways: A meaningful learning experience can come from a multitude of activities. I learned niche doing skills, as well as applied my previous learning onto something interesting. O'Byrne will be able to create an interesting internship experience.

description: Helping with physics work, converting previous exams of different types into use for future use in assignments and other such purposes. My time during this internship was focused on properly formatting questions using WebAssign and analyzing question to find the proper answer. Being an all-digital internship and having very flexible scheduling, the internship allowed me to properly review and push my skills forward while always having a balanced working schedule.
unique_experiences: • Applying physics knowledge to various exams from different nations to import into WebAssign. • Limited implementation of HTML
surprised: I am surprised that looking that a bunch of different physics exams and different ways to solve them were so interesting, mostly just looking at how different people and I process questions.
takeaways: Trying to derive other experience such as learning about how others think through issues is just as important as the literal internship experience you get.

description: Learning without Limitations is a student led organization which pairs students with volunteer tutors, helping students reach their educational goals through free tutoring, and volunteers gain service hours towards their graduation requirement. Responsibilities include pairing students with qualified volunteers, working with students to help them achieve their educational goals, communicating with teachers and administration to advertise the program, and constantly organizing more ways to improve the project.
unique_experiences: • Being a part of a student's educational journey and giving them the resources to achieve their goals • Developing strong organizational (e.g. Google Drive, Microsoft Outlook, etc.) and communication skills in a low-pressure environment • Meet other peers and teachers who share a passion for education
surprised: I was so surprised by how gratifying the work is! Both helping students firsthand by tutoring them and seeing more and more students sign up made me feel like I was making a real difference in my community. I was also surprised by how nuanced leading a project is. Unlike other internship opportunities, the freedom that comes with this internship meant that I developed a wide variety of skills.
takeaways: A large part of my work was working directly with underserviced students at Interlake. Although I feel like I learned many practical skills, I would say that my biggest takeaways from this internship are the social skills and knowledge which I developed.

location: Virtual/ Camp location changes every couple of months
description: Camp Unity Eastside is a nonprofit tent village that moves around eastside King County. It offers safe shelter, basic needs, and supportive resources to those working towards a more permanent housing solution. During my time there, I was tasked with researching policies and grants, contacting local businesses, collecting & organizing data, managing social media content, presenting my youth perspective etc. My meetings were very flexible, and my mentor was very willing to help set tasks that aligned with my goals. Projects varied in length and depended on what was most needed by the organization. The organization is not religiously affiliated although the Camp is hosted by churches on a rotating basis and receives much of their support from Christian church groups. I highly recommend this internship if you're interested in the intersection of business functions like marketing and management, and government policy and in making real change your community.
unique_experiences: • High degree of leadership over the tasks you take on and how you do them/ ability to pitch your own tasks you think would benefit the organization • Responsibilities are highly rewarding in that they benefit a community in need • Opportunities to get in touch with board members, people in marketing, city government staff, other nonprofit organizations
surprised: Every single person involved in the organization and partnering organizations is very willing to contribute to the organization's mission as well as support your personal success.
takeaways: • If you're passionate about the organization's goals, the tasks don't even feel like a "job." You will gain a good understanding of how an organization motivated by service rather than by profit operates. • You will be given many opportunities to advance your excel/word/technical skills as well as professionalism and communication skills. It's important to make the most out of every project.
mentor_name: Lori Peckol (my mentor is very interested in intern(s) for next year!)

description: Orion Education is a fantastic educational facility/tutoring facility which aims to expand young children's horizon of learning and quenching their thirst for knowledge by having interns and teachers that seek to simplify complex subtraction, addition, language, and science concepts in a creative and innovative way for children ages 3+
unique_experiences: One of my unique experiences at Orion Education was being able to take an entire class to plan out an entire curriculum where we color in math worksheets with the color of the students choices, reading a pdf book for them to learn and build their vocabulary and comprehension skills, and finally, being able to craft a googly eyed crab with red colored paper and pipe cleaners.
surprised: What surprised me the most is the way that online teaching did not hinder my ability to teach these young students. I was able to do math problems in a fun and interactive manner so that my personality imprints on these children. I did not realize how vibrant and brilliant all these children are, for they all have the capacity to quickly grasp material.
takeaways: The greatest take way from this internship experience is learning how to give and receive friendly contributions with a pool of students who are of a different age group than me. Children are very fun to work with and their personalities are easy to mold, thus making them the most versatile subjects to communicate with. I increased my patience by learning how to talk to children in the way they want to be treated. Additionally, my Internship mentor taught me the importance of formality, with curriculum, project books, and calls to effectively communicate with adults and other professionals, whether it's over text, email, or phone call.

description: The Statistical Center for HIV/AIDS Research and Prevention (SCHARP) is a subdivision of the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center that works with clinical trial networks all around the world to manage and analyze data from clinical trials on the topics of HIV and AIDS. In this internship, I worked in the Clinical Data Management team and got to work on individual trials, from the process of starting a trial to closing it out.
unique_experiences: • Valuable training in various clinical data management and trial related software • Opportunities to write and test forms in the clinical trial databases to make sure they are ready for each trial site to use • Checking and analyzing data after a study has ended to make sure the data is clean enough to go through statistical analysis for publishing
surprised: I was surprised at how many stages there are to building treatments and testing them in trials before they are allowed to be used at a consumer level.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship experience was the knowledge that there is so much more to working in the biological research field than just working directly in a lab developing treatments or in the field collecting samples to study. Before this internship, I was only really exposed to that side of biological research, but through working at SCHARP and learning about the different paths all my coworkers took, I was exposed to how broad the opportunities are in this field.

organization: Phillips Lab- UW Department of Otolaryngology
location: Virginia Merrill Bloedel Hearing Research Center, UW Seattle Campus
description: Learned about and applied research guidelines- human subject restrictions, IRB Reviews and ethical considerations, animal laws, OAW and HIPAA laws Attended monthly vestibular disorders group meetings to present research findings and discuss new studies Researched vestibular system and mechanisms of the inner ear through peer review with other researchers, pulled reference studies and cross-analyzed similar research Utilized MATLAB Software to process experimental data from vestibular implant study Received UW-issued monitor and software to take part in internship activities, all work conducted remotely
unique_experiences: I was able to learn about the various logistical steps required for conducting scientific studies and experience the iterative process of editing/modifying a currently published research study to include new breakthroughs. Witnessing this process was very useful since it taught me about the bureaucracy required to successfully undertake research within the biological field.
surprised: My mentor was very hands-off during the process, and it was mainly up to me to follow up about additional work that I could perform beyond training modules. Managing around his and my schedules was also surprisingly hard, since as a research professor he was constantly attending to other commitments and meetings were often scheduled and cancelled.
takeaways: That it is up to you to forge ahead and find work! Though my mentor was extremely supportive in the little time that I could spend with him, I had to be very thorough about emailing him and other members of his team. His assistant, Robin, was often very helpful in reaching him when he was not available or connecting me to other lab members.

organization: UW Henry M. Jackson School of International Studies
description: I worked as a research assistant to Prof. Sabine Lang at the UW School of International Studies – whose work is broadly centered around both civil society gender politics within the European Union. This year specifically, Prof. Lang worked on projects regarding the impact of federalism on state gender budgeting, EU gender civil society structures, and evolving stances on gender quotas from Merkel's CDU. For more information about the topics of Prof. Lang's work, you can visit http://www.sabinelang.org/. Most of my work fell into one of three categories: (1) compiling recent research relevant Prof. Lang's projects; (2) editing journal article/book chapter drafts for English language, conceptual clarity, and publishers' formatting guidelines; (3) summarizing literature relevant to Prof. Lang's ongoing work. I also did some work updating her website and helped with background research relevant to a grant application.
unique_experiences: • Attending lecture series featuring researchers and their current work in the fields of gender politics and democracy in the EU • A behind the scenes look at the academic literature production/publishing process • The opportunity to have conversations with and ask questions to someone whose has had decades of experiences with advocacy politics, feminist movements, and a variety of political ecosystems
surprised: Prof. Lang's general flexibility and willingness to let my interests guide some of my work, along with her genuine interest in my input, thoughts, and ideas were such pleasant surprises. This made my internship a great experience.
takeaways: The importance not being shy to offer my opinion when it's asked for and a better idea as to what academia looks like.

description: Jagran Foundation is a nonprofit that provides grants to many different local and global organizations such as schools, museums, and parks. They use Jagran's grants to execute projects which help the community in different ways. During my internship, I researched how to provide grants to different organizations and figuring out which of their projects would be the most valuable to the community, and where the money spent would be put to the best use. I also created a website for the organization by researching similar foundations' websites.
unique_experiences: • Applying research skills learned in school to real life for the first time • Designing a website with no prior experience • Gaining experience in business communication with other groups What surprised you the most? I am surprised at the speed at which other businesses and groups reply to communication. I would think that being such large organizations with so much on their plates, they would not have as much time to get back to small organizations such as Jagran Foundation, but they were much prompter than I expected. What is your biggest takeaway(s) from the internship experience? My biggest takeaway from the experience was the work environment of a real foundation. While my internship was all remote, I still got a sense of professionalism from working at a real job and by working with other businesses and organizations.
surprised: I was surprised by how kind and helpful everyone was. Even if I asked the most basic questions, everyone answered them thoroughly and always volunteered to help me. All the post grads even offered to help and give feedback on projects unrelated to their work.
takeaways: This was my first experience go through the process of conducting research, writing a paper, and publishing a paper. Even though we are still in the process of getting our paper published, it was really eye-opening to learn all the steps we took to finally get our work accepted into a journal.
mentor_name: This internship is not available Mentor Email: n/a

description: Artemis Connection is a strategic consulting firm that aims to create real-world solutions to current problems, especially for nonprofits, healthcare and the education sector but also taking on private equity, government and tech clients. My time at Artemis was split between several such projects, with correspondingly different tasks – conducting data analyses, literary reviews, and constructing slide decks, to name several. Artemis also offered sessions for interns/new employees to learn research and networking skills, which greatly contributed to learning and performing my internship roles.
unique_experiences: • Worked on projects with tangible impacts o Engaged in statewide task force to produce a report on the digital divide in Washington that became House Bill 1365 o Developed presentation youth mental health in relation to screen time and social media for a media client • Gained experience in both data-driven analysis and team-based collaborative problem-solving
surprised: Before beginning the internship, I imagined everything would be serious, data-oriented and ruthlessly efficient. In practice, there is much more conversation, critical thinking and collective consideration that I anticipated. Perhaps the greatest surprise was the degree to which more experienced employees were eager to help me develop the skills I needed to thrive. And while some discussions were serious, high-level and technical (especially on the task force), the majority of meetings felt like normal conversations – albeit tackling difficult questions of how we could problem solve. The data analysis and research was done outside of meetings and far from the focal point – numbers were used to support ideas, not vice versa.
takeaways: Although the research skills I gained from the internship at Artemis might have the most tangible benefits in the near future, the experience participating – and eventually, sometimes leading – high-level problem-solving discussions will be the best benefit overall, applicable at every difficult juncture in life to come.

organization: N-Body Shop research group, UW Astronomy Department
description: I interned at the N-Body Shop, a research group in the UW Astronomy Department that uses computational techniques to model astronomical behavior. Coding was done primarily in C++ and Python was used for data visualization. I implemented features to the simulation code ChaNGa that model particle shear in rotating reference frames.
unique_experiences: • Learning practical coding skills and OS/Linux command line stuff applied to physics research; I got a lot of experience implementing research papers and equations into code. • Attending my research group's weekly meeting to share what we've been working on – it was a great opportunity to learn about graduate level astrophysics • Getting to sit in on a bunch of thesis defenses and seminars • Going canoeing with the research group at the UW waterfront :D
surprised: How incredibly generous my professor was with giving me one-on-one time and energy supporting my work. I knew almost nothing about computational physics research going in, and he was exceptionally patient and supportive as I slowly learned the ropes of research.
takeaways: Research is an extremely gritty process and progress is often slow. There were many points where I genuinely had no idea how to solve the problem that was blocking me; I learned to be willing to bother people with questions and to solve issues one at a time. You will eventually make a breakthrough and be awarded with a nice plot that will make you feel all warm and fuzzy inside.

description: Professor Hohn's work involves the revitalization of Southern Lushootseed, the language spoken by many Salish tribes in the Puget Sound region. In addition to spending a lot of time learning this language and compiling a portfolio, I also was able to practice extracting language from a document and transcribing the orthography of the original author to the writing system used today.
unique_experiences: • Valuable insight into languages revitalization and linguistic processes • Practice working with and extracting language from historical documents
surprised: This was the first time I was able to do any work in a field related to linguistics and I was surprised by the difficulty of converting earlier orthographies into the modern-day equivalent but also how much fun it was.
takeaways: I learned a lot about how to approach this type of work more like a puzzle than a chore, and I was also able to learn about how this kind of work has a real impact on the community, even if I wasn't interacting with the community during my internship.

description: My internship as a research assistant under Dr. Serena Cosgrove allowed me to work closely with her through this period of the research process. The focus of the research led by Dr. Cosgrove and her co-principal investigator is based on the way minoritized women are organizing in conflict and post conflict countries (primarily the Democratic Republic of Congo and Guatemala) against gender-based violence, with a focus on intersectionality within these settings. As a research assistant, I primarily worked on the literature review part of this project (reading and summarizing key points of academic articles) but was also given the opportunity to edit another publication that Dr. Cosgrove was working on, and to create a written contribution of my own.
unique_experiences: • Flexibility to find and focus on my own interests within the greater sphere of our research • Independent work with frequent support and guidance from my mentor and our small research group • Small writing project on a topic of my choice to be published in the final product
surprised: I was surprised at the sheer effort it takes to produce this type of research and the magnitude of how it differs from our regular high school level experiences. Not only has this project been almost a decade in the making, but it has taken the collaborative work and long-term dedication of dozens of academics, NGO leaders, and locals in the Democratic Republic of Congo to create.
takeaways: This internship experience was very influential in choosing my course at university. I had only applied to a single school for International Development instead of International Relations, but throughout the year, the flexibility of this internship helped me to gain a deep appreciation for social sciences research and an interest in new topics that ultimately led me to choose this area of study.

description: The FDA Office of Generic Drugs is mainly involved with crafting and implementing regulations for generic drugs. These regulations ensure that the safety and efficacy of generic drugs are at the same standard as brand name products that are already on the market. I primarily compared scales used to assess irritation and sensitization reactions to create a more accurate scale for assessing the safety of generic products.
unique_experiences: • Presentation of Immunology of the Skin saved as instructional material • Research machine learning application to dermatology
surprised: I was surprised by how different it is working in a real world academic setting since tasks are more specialized and require new knowledge that branch off of what we learn at school.
takeaways: Research works at its own pace, especially when collaborating with others and working remotely. Thus, it is important to stay focused on contributing your best effort in completing work in a timely manner and with high accuracy.

description: • Astrophysics/Machine Learning research projects • Mostly working with Python and Overleaf • Read papers on intermediate black hole mergers and pulsating star/supernova events • Compiled data from multiple Hertzberg Russel Diagram studies to model star behavior after collapse, clustering data using SKLearn and other Python clustering programs (Machine Learning) • Very self-driven, expect Prof to be very hands off – if it is remote, you might have 2-3 calls throughout the year, but expect most communication and follow-up to be through email
unique_experiences: Astrophysics encompasses so many different subject areas, so a lot of my work included exploring data science, machine learning, Python, etc. A lot of my work was self-driven – self-study from literary texts (e.g. Spacetime Physics and Exploring Black Holes) + watching lecture and documentaries. What surprised you the most? There is so much we don't know about the universe, and every topic has so many subtopics you could dive into. Even though it seems rather techy, astronomy requires so much knowledge about all areas of math and science (and sometimes even social sciences). It's something everyone can (and should) learn a bit about because it makes you look at the world through a unique lens.
takeaways: What I mentioned above but also how important it is to build connections within any industry. At times I was unable to find open-source data since the topics I was focusing on were so recent, but my mentor was able to reach out to some of his friends who had access to data that had not yet been published. On the same note, there's so much value in cold emailing people – I needed access to some papers for my research, and the professors were not only more than willing to send me their papers but they also helped explain some sections that I found a bit confusing at first glance.

description: I was a general research assistant for Bioethics faculty, focusing on clinical research in behavioral sciences. I helped with writing surveys, reviewing prior scientific literature, constructing bibliographies, writing grant proposals, applying detailed formatting requirements for scientific papers, and pretty much whatever else they needed help with. I also sat in on research-planning meetings and helped manage logistics like contacting participants.
unique_experiences: Single-handedly drafting a survey that was later used in a clinical study and using an NIH tool to verify grant finances were probably the highlights of my internship experience!
surprised: The sheer amount of bookkeeping/paperwork/logistical work was startling, and I'm glad I could help take some of that burden off the actual researchers.
takeaways: The research field is highly self-directed. Faculty must come up with new ideas to research, plan out the entire study down to the tiniest details, successfully apply for funding/permissions, recruit participants, pass stringent ethics standards, and more before they can even start the main body of data collection.

organization: Kaeberlein Lab, University of Washington Dpt. Of Pathology
description: Primary research goal of screening natural/synthetic compounds in C. elegans to identify drugs that have the potential to delay the onset of/alleviate symptoms associated with neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases. Interns do data annotation on images of c. elegans for developing a neural network that will allow the ability to investigate aging and aging interventions. Working on neural network to identify age of onset of Alzheimer's disease through analysis face images.
unique_experiences: Getting to learn a lot about microbiology and the science behind how cells and different organisms age. Ben is extremely patient and did an amazing job walking through different research papers on PubMed so that we could learn something new each week.
surprised: How dedicated everyone in the lab is to their work. My mentor was very flexible and easily set up new projects and experiments to test out new ideas. There was a lot of creativity involved in these systems, and we used advanced equipment such as neural networks and detailed automatic cameras for each petri dish that was observed. There is a good crossover between computer science with biology, especially as a virtual intern.
takeaways: I got to learn a lot about how different members of a lab work together to critique each other's procedures and analysis of data, as well as the different roles of the members in the same lab. It's also a really great feeling to know you made an impact on a study or experiment that might actually help real people one day.

description: Fairhall Laboratory is a primarily computational neuroscience lab that studies Hydra, a microorganism with simple behaviors. Most of my time working in the lab was spent doing video analysis using software such as Python, Matlab, ImageJ, and Icy. I worked on a variety of different videos from calcium wave videos to 30- minute full body behavioral videos. This internship offered close mentorship with graduate students as well as professors who were very supportive. Furthermore, it was a great way to learn about neuroscience and how research works in the real world.
unique_experiences: • Learning how to use computer science to process behavior in different microscope videos. • Weekly lab meetings featuring in depth discussion and planning for future research. • Drafting research papers and presentations and practicing scientific communication.
surprised: I was very surprised to see how collaborative research is. Before, I thought that research was done by individuals and had conclusive endpoint, rather than a continuing process involving many people
takeaways: This internship solidified my interest in scientific research. I now know that I scientific research is something that I enjoy and will happily pursue career wise. It is important that I had this experience before committing to pursuing academia with no idea what it was really like.

description: Working under Dr. Salene Jones and Dr. Harris, I was able to complete clinical practice and social/behavioral research trainings to be certified to work at Fred Hutch. I analyzed and organized data for studies related to endometriosis and pain measurement, which was a combination of their work. In the second half of my internship, I was able to research clinical trials that tested endometriosis specific drugs and their efficacy to mitigate pain.
unique_experiences: Certifications in Good Clinical Practice and Social Behavioral Research Understanding how to organize data from publications and clinical studies Got the opportunity to begin writing an intro to a research protocol
surprised: In researching endometriosis, I was shocked to find such a sparse sert of clinical trials for a condition that affects hundreds of thousands of women annually. Not more than 20 clinical drug tests for endometriosis specifically have been conducted.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is understanding how published research work can be greatly beneficial to current/other researchers. Published trials and data are extremely useful in determining what research needs to be expanded and in which areas, for example, drugs that are specific to endometriosis, instead of general drugs such as oral contraceptives, are in crucial need of testing. I was able to do all of this completely virtually, including the trainings!

organization: Luscombe Research Lab - Molecular Engineering and Sciences Institute
description: The research of the Luscombe Lab is focused on the development of controlled polymerizations to synthesize precise polymeric structures. The role of the intern is to understand the development of machine learning tools to optimize processes. As an intern, I gained significant experience in the data science aspect of the internship, mainly due to being remote. My responsibilities included using python to scrape data from scientific published papers, use machine learning to recognize patterns in published papers, and understand the importance of polymerizations.
unique_experiences: • Learned how to code python and web-scrape • Gained valuable experience with machine learning algorithms to understand published research papers • Exposed to a real-world data science field
surprised: The amount of technical work involved was definitely a surprise. Going into the internship, I was prepared to learn mainly about different chemical reactions involving polymers and conduct independent research. However, I was also able to work with other interns on "labelling" published papers in order to help us code an algorithm that presented us with patterns in the research papers we were studying.
takeaways: I learned the importance of having technological experience. As someone that was mainly interested in health science and research, this internship gave me a chance to explore data science which bridged the gap between both research and engineering.

organization: Fred Hutchinson, Public Health Sciences Division
description: The Jones Lab at Fred Hutch researches the financial toxicity in the treatment of cancer and other diseases (relevant topics and ideas include medication adherence, financial questionnaires, and repetitive negative thinking). The lab focuses on how to measure this financial toxicity as well as how it affects quality of life and treatment.
unique_experiences: • I was responsible for putting together and organizing a database of academic papers about different veins and complexities of financial toxicity using excel. • In the later stages of my internship, I transitioned into completing literature searches, researching the relationship between credit score data and cancer treatment.
surprised: One surprise was the diversity in skillsets and specializations in cancer research (and research in general). Specifically, my internship centered on the social/financial side of a medical issue, which I found to be a different approach than expected.
takeaways: This was my first experience working in a large organization, and I learned about the inner workings of how different professionals with different specializations research and collaborate. I found it valuable to familiarize myself with the less glamorized aspects of the field of medical/cancer research.

organization: Luscombe Research Lab: Molecular Engineering and Sciences Institute
description: I was able to work with this organization by using technology to conduct and analyze polymers (plastic) that is used for packaging. They use electronically active materials to make new-semi conductive polymers and to understand their properties. To do so, I frequently used python and machine learning. I was also able to work on inputting given data and ensuring that received data is in the correct format. Throughout this internship, I used various coding websites and tutorials like Anaconda and PsiKit.
unique_experiences: • Working with graduate and undergraduate students; gave me more perspective and prepared me for working with others in college • Learning machine learning and incorporating it into our research
surprised: • Adjusting to working fully remote was much easier than anticipated, since most of the work was online • This was my first time working with python, so I had self-learn the code using many outside influences and it was more difficult than I expected.
takeaways: • It's really influencing and worthwhile to work with age groups that you are not used to working with. • I learned to ask for help a lot more, since the content and projects were a little outside my comfort zone. • I learned to adapt with digital work, which was starkly different than learning the content in person.

description: As a National Park, Carlsbad Caverns is dedicated to preserving the natural features of Carlsbad and educating the public. During my internship, I was a part of the cave monitoring team and contributed to the ongoing Visitor Use Management (VUM) study. The objective of the VUM study is to understand how park visitors can affect the health of other visitors and the cave system. Members of the team based in New Mexico collected visitation, carbon dioxide, and temperature data inside the cave, which I analyzed to better understand the patterns that emerged between those variables and external weather events.
unique_experiences: • Experienced a government work environment and gained insights into park management • Data analysis methods: worked in R and Python, learned and applied various statistical tests and methods • Research communication: practiced both written and verbal research communication by writing mini papers and giving presentations at team meetings
surprised: I was most surprised by the incredible thought and effort put into designing visitor protocols and how much goes on behind the scenes of maintaining and running a national park.
takeaways: I now have a much greater appreciation and understanding of various data analysis methods. Throughout my internship, I have learned and applied many powerful analysis tools to determine relationships in data, and directly saw the importance of such research in influencing park policy.

description: The UW Autism Center is devoted to supporting individuals and families affected by autism spectrum disorders through exceptional clinical services, innovative scientific research and high-quality training. My time at the UWAC provided me with hands-on experience with a handful of large-scale research projects. It also helped expose me to the different fields inside of the UWAC, as I was able to virtually shadow different health care providers who had the same goal of helping neurodivergent adolescents.
unique_experiences: • Experience navigating research websites (REDCap, LORIS, UWAC profiles, etc.) as well as how to score various behavioral assessments. • Observe behavioral visits and how researchers collect information on adolescent's development (cognitive, verbal, social, play, and academic). • Shadowing SLPs, Behavioral Technicians, etc. • Many one-on-one calls with research coordinators in the UWAC. I got the opportunity to ask them about their work and various other questions, ranging from developmental disorders to research techniques.
surprised: How much everyone in the UWAC cared about the interns' experience and how responsive they were to any issues or suggestions I had. In addition, if I ever had any issues with timing or not being able to get an assignment done, they were more than understanding.
takeaways: People who enjoy their work want to share what they love about it with you! Don't be afraid to set up meetings with people if you're interested in their work. You can learn a lot not only about the field, but also what potential jobs in it would be. At least in the UWAC, there were many new types of job openings since research on developmental disorders is fairly new and I found that to be very valuable information.

location: Home due to COVID restrictions (401 Terry Ave N, Seattle, WA 98109)
description: ISB is a research nonprofit with the primary goal of improving global health and realizing the future of medicine, which will be P4 (personalized, participatory, predictive and preventive) medicine. My time there gave me hands on experience with the collaborative research process and bioinformatics analyses for different genes linked to Alzheimer's. Having its hands in so many research and health science projects, ISB allowed me to find a project on a disease I was interested in and find ways to visualize the network of its causes through creating boxplots in R, Python and other applications.
unique_experiences: • Access to a wide variety of experts in different fields • Being able to access and analyze large cloud-based data from partner organizations • The availability of so many projects makes it easy to find something you're interested in
surprised: ISB implements a multidisciplinary approach to treating disease, where they analyze it on many fronts: genetic, proteomic, metabolomic, etc. I've realized how far stretching their interdisciplinary methods are, being applicable to healthy aging, pregnancy health, and Alzheimer's (what I'm working on) in addition to many other areas.
takeaways: This was my first experience into bioinformatics research, and through it I realized that when tackling a complex disease, it's important to look at it from many different angles. As my research was dry lab, I didn't have to have knowledge of typical lab procedures which shows that if you're interested in research but aren't a hands-on person, there is still plenty you can in terms of data analysis.

location: Department of Ophthalmology at the University of Washington
description: Help with database management of OCT images, learn about the eye and different inflammatory eye diseases, assist with different research projects the lab is working on, help construct PowerPoints for OCT scoring
unique_experiences: • Published research abstract at ARVO (Research Conference) as second author • Participated in developing an AI model for OCT scoring in rat eyes by managing OCT database • Led my own journal club and lab meetings
surprised: I was surprised at how my research mentor was so eager to involve me in as many projects as possible. Because I didn't have much experience, I thought I would not be involved in much, but she was very willing to teach me and allow me to participate in many aspects of the lab.
takeaways: I was really amazed at how research at even a small level like this can influence many other labs and research projects. Research is truly a transformative and important field and it was a great learning experience. I also realized how much I would like to continue doing it in college to find innovative solutions to issues in the world.

location: University of Washington Internship description Kaeberlein Lab, part of the UW Laboratory Medicine and Pathology Department, focuses on aging and aging- related diseases. The lab focuses on many model organisms to study diseases that range from Parkinson's to metabolic dysfunction. My group focused on Parkinson's and Alzheimer's and their respective models in C. elegans. Starting at Kaeberlein did require basic knowledge in biology, but will allow interns to grow in a specialty, in my case being neurodegenerative diseases. Unique experiences • Planning, executing, and presenting original scientific designs • Data analysis of drug tests on model organisms • Growing skills in conducting scientific literary research
description: Kaeberlein Lab, part of the UW Laboratory Medicine and Pathology Department, focuses on aging and aging- related diseases. The lab focuses on many model organisms to study diseases that range from Parkinson's to metabolic dysfunction. My group focused on Parkinson's and Alzheimer's and their respective models in C. elegans. Starting at Kaeberlein did require basic knowledge in biology, but will allow interns to grow in a specialty, in my case being neurodegenerative diseases.
unique_experiences: • Planning, executing, and presenting original scientific designs • Data analysis of drug tests on model organisms • Growing skills in conducting scientific literary research
surprised: A huge part of making sure an experiment can work is testing and retesting preliminary designs, which can require a lot of guess-and-check in addition to prior research/knowledge. What is/are your greatest takeaway(s) from the internship experience? There is a lot of planning and retesting into making sure a drug does have an effect. It is a rewarding experience to find a chemical that has an effect and apply it to different models to confirm and corroborate to new findings.
mentor_name: Dr. Elena Vayndorf (possibly won't accept for Fall 2021 but can redirect to someone else)

organization: Kaeberlein Lab, University of Washington Department of Pathology
description: The Kaeberlein Lab studies the process of aging and aging-related diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's in model organisms such as C elegans worms. Since C elegans have short lifespans (2-3 days) and are relatively simple organisms (with only approximately 1000 cells), they are useful for testing the effects of anti-aging drugs or knocking out specific genes to study their role in aging. Through this internship, I have learned many practical research skills and I have developed a better understanding of how scientific research is done in the real world.
unique_experiences: • I score Wormbot images of C elegans and creating graphs to communicate the data. • I help create a general database of the efficacy of various natural products in treating different symptoms. For instance, if I find a journal article that describes a controlled trial testing the effects of ashwagandha on reducing anxiety, I summarize the information and add it to the spreadsheet. • Based on the research articles we read, we picked the most promising natural products to test on the C. elegans in the lab. I will be testing the effect of ashwagandha and dietary nitrate on muscle strength in C. elegans.
surprised: I was surprised by how interesting it is to process data and interpret statistics. Creating graphs of the C. elegans lifespan data and drawing conclusions was very satisfying. When conducting background research, I was also surprised by just how many bad studies are out there. You have to carefully look at the research design, sample size, and statistics; you cannot just assume that if it is published in a journal it must be good.
takeaways: My main takeaway is that scientific research involves a lot of collaboration. I work with my internship partners to check each other's data, give feedback, and discuss results. Although I am not a naturally outgoing person, through this internship I have learned to volunteer my opinions, and not preoccupy myself with worries about being wrong.

location: Kaeberlein Lab, Health Sciences Building D-514 (although it was online this year)
description: • The Kaeberlein Lab studies mechanisms of aging by using many model organisms, with this internship focusing on the model organism C. Elegans. • Typically (if it wasn't remote this year), wet lab skills would be applied to prep the worms. But since this year was all done remotely, dry lab skills were primarily used. Dry lab skills include scoring the worms for both lifespan and paralysis as well as graphing and interpreting the data. • If you are not scoring worms, you would be doing research for other potential drugs to treat different mechanisms of aging or reading other interesting research papers. • Once a week, you will have a meeting (online or possibly in person) discussing research papers or you will present your graphs and data for the scoring of the worms. • Typically, there will be a project that you will be in charge of that you will have to find evidence for and make a presentation on it.
unique_experiences: • Valuable experience in dry lab work: scoring, research, developing an experiment, etc. • Working with a group to analyze data and understanding how to read and make graphs. • Learning about mechanisms of aging and preventive drugs.
surprised: What surprised me the most was seeing how difficult research could be and how invaluable working with your peers can be.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is to take your time and make sure you are doing the best job you can and to not be afraid to reach out for help if you need it.

organization: Kaeberlein Lab at the UW Department of Pathology
description: My mentor's research focuses on pre-clinical screening of compounds on C. elegans. They are interested in identifying drugs that have a potential to delay the onset or alleviate symptoms for neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's. I helped my mentor by scoring experiments that he has completed in the lab and gather data on people who have had Alzheimer's. We also read research papers related to C. elegans research and aging.
unique_experiences: • Attending lab meetings and learning what research looks like in the professional scene • Analyzing lab data and finding new compounds that extend lifespan
surprised: I was surprised at elaborate the data collection process is and how many experiments must be completed to test one drug compound.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship experience is learning how many different aspects there are to scientific research. For example, a lot of the data collection process involves manual work and computer engineering. The wide range of ways to get involved in research has made me more interested in research for my future.

description: Brunton Lab at UW-Seattle researches applications of machine learning for dynamics and control, namely fluid dynamics. The role of the research intern is primarily self-driven. The intern is expected to self-study from textbooks, read papers, and learn new technologies as fit for the completion of their projects.
unique_experiences: • Machine learning workflow: gathering data, choosing appropriate feature encoding techniques, choosing appropriate models, training the models, dealing with overfitting/underfitting/overflow/underflow/etc. • Bibliometric analysis: understanding metrics for measuring research impact, interpreting citation graphs, frameworks for bibliometric analysis • Applied math: statistical methods, linear algebra, and a bit of Matlab
surprised: Implementing the models led me to realize machine learning was a lot more empirical than I thought. There isn't always a clean reason for why representing my data a certain way or changing the activation function of a layer gives better results, and there are a lot of caveats in the real world that get abstracted away in textbooks. In this way, machine learning is a little like cooking: I can read about the theory, but I don't truly understand it until I get my hands dirty.
takeaways: As this was my first time experiencing a research environment, I'm grateful for the opportunity to learn more about research as a career path. My mentor's most memorable advice was that to succeed in research, you must be able to explain the significance of your work to all sorts of audiences. This advice is also very applicable outside of research.

description: Over the course of my internship, I had the opportunity to work on a couple different projects, primarily coding in Matlab. The lab and mentor I worked with were interested in the role of certain brain pathways in drug addiction using mouse and rat models. One of my major projects was refining and writing a program to analyze RNAscope (microscope images) to identify overlap between markers for brain activity. I also worked on a project that reconstructed microglia image stacks in 3D.
unique_experiences: One of the most interesting projects I got to contribute to was a Matlab program called DeepSqueak. My mentor created Deepsqueak, a program that denoised and classified rat calls for use by other scientists in their experiments. I spent some time training the networks in the program to improve their automatic classification. I thought it was a really unique experience to be able to work on an actively developing program that could be used by researchers everywhere.
surprised: I was most surprised by how much effort and detail it requires to run a lab. Part of my internship was attending weekly lab meetings where I realized the role each person plays in keeping the lab functioning.
takeaways: Research is such a collaborative and welcoming space! Everyone in my lab was always willing to help each other out and interested in seeing each other succeed. I could definitely see myself pursuing a career in the research field and potentially this discipline.

description: This bioinformatics research project created a dataset of human proteins and attributes of those proteins pulled from several different sources in order to help answer questions such as: which are the as-yet undetected human proteins? What is the most likely reason that each is undetected? Which proteins are the most promising targets for detection with current technologies? The goals were to create and continually refine the human protein dataset to better answer the above questions, write a Python build process to allow frequent updates to the dataset as new information becomes available, and write a manuscript that describes the process and results of creating this summary of human proteins
unique_experiences: • Experience writing scientific manuscripts • Diverse uses of Python • Interacting and receiving feedback from professionals in the field • Getting to attend scientific conferences and other events
surprised: The level of helpfulness and collaboration throughout the internship
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from my internship was seeing a glimpse of what professional scientific research and the process is like

description: The Smith Lab at Fred Hutch has current focus on recombination genetics in Escherichia coli and Schizosaccharomyces pombe. As a researcher, I've focused my efforts on the RecBCD enzyme in E. coli, producing data and compiling a manuscript to disprove commonly held, yet incorrect, assumptions about the critical recombination enzyme. As a diligent worker, my mentor was extremely understanding regarding my schedule provided I communicate my responsibilities ahead of time.
unique_experiences: • Developing prospective research questions and planning out lab work • Using industry standard programs and services such as ClusPro, SnapGene, PyMol, NCBI, GeneBank, etc. • Computational genetic comparisons and analysis with python and Java
surprised: How welcoming and supportive my mentor and labmates were. Though most of the time I found myself working alone due to COVID-19, whenever I reached out my coworkers were more than willing and able to help.
takeaways: I've been able to produce and write literature that will soon be published, making a real-world, quantifiable difference. Not only has this process helped me deepen my understanding in the field, it has also been greatly confidence-inspiring.

organization: Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning Team (EPE-ML)
description: The EPE-ML team applies machine learning techniques to known problems in particle physics, using data from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN. After initial training, I was able to take ownership of two research projects, along with a team of a few other undergraduates. We had set times for biweekly status reports to Professor Hsu and our supervising postdoc, but other times for collaborative efforts between team members were highly flexible.
unique_experiences: • Experience in machine learning: TensorFlow; complex architectures like CNN; complex paradigms like GAN; many physics-motivated architectures like LBN, LGN, CWoLa, SALAD, DijetGAN • Crafting and executing a research project about a novel anomaly detection method, with the intent to publish research findings at the ML4Jets conference • Collaborating on code, using remote computing resources, and communicating results using tools like GitHub, Jupyter Notebook, Cori at NERSC, Google Cloud
surprised: I was surprised by how open the field of EPE ML research is. My team was able to choose an unsolved problem and contribute to its solution.
takeaways: This was my first time doing scientific research, which I have no doubt I will be doing more of later in my career. I learned a lot about the entire research process, especially in the collaborative aspects. I also learned about the power of machine learning methods to solve a wide range of problems, often surpassing hand-engineered algorithms. ML is a rapidly evolving field that has such a wide range of applications! Were you able to continue your internship remotely? Do you think this location can offer a remote, or hybrid model for future internships? Yes, and yes. The nature of the internship was all digital anyways. The team communicated over a variety of platforms, including Discord, Skype, Slack, and email.

description: I worked on building sequence prediction models using TensorFlow and Keras to aid in Professor Brunton's research in normal numbers and chaotic systems. Most of my time was spent testing different models, collecting data, and doing analysis of the data. I also spent a good amount of time reading articles related to machine learning and dynamical systems, as well as studying mathematical statistics.
unique_experiences: • Used TensorFlow and Keras frameworks to build machine learning models • Applied-math research • Self-study from textbooks and research articles (Professor Brunton provided me with many good resources) • Mostly independent work
surprised: I was a little daunted at first since I did not have much experience with machine learning, but Professor Brunton was very helpful in providing me learning resources and I was able to self-learn many concepts quite quickly.
takeaways: Overall, I learned a lot not only in machine learning/data analysis, but also how to self-learn and present my findings. It was a bit challenging to work independently and virtually this year, but I definitely gained valuable research experience.

description: Vogt Lab focuses on environmental sciences. As part of my internship, I worked in a group focusing on better understanding the ways we educate others regarding environmental science/issues. While not research in a traditional sense, it provided me with first-hand experience with the ways professors approach their work and offered me opportunities to emulate that. Despite working as part of a larger team, I was very well connected with the professor.
unique_experiences: • Creating a board game used to teach K-12 students environmental principles. • Designing, preparing and running an experiment comparing teaching methods. • Just being able to hang out with and talk to a professor. Professors are very interesting people, and they like sharing.
surprised: Operating in a college environment was a lot more different from high school than I had anticipated. It's very self-driven. Especially since research is so open ended. At the beginning of the year, although we had a goal in mind, we had no idea how to go about getting there. Everyone on the team had a say in the matter, which was certainly new, given how railroaded assignments may feel in high school.
takeaways: Finding an internship is not easy, but it is worth doing. I'm not saying that you'll learn to enjoy sending emails to complete strangers, but I learned to not be embarrassed about it. Don't be put off by a couple no-responses.

description: Read and critically analyze peer-reviewed biomedical articles from databases such as PubMed/NCBI, Biomedcentral, and other others. Distill information related to healthy aging and age-related diseases into an Excel sheet with information retrieved from these articles.
unique_experiences: Primary project involves preparing a presentation on three health/aging-related symptoms and natural products. Individual group members will design and carry out their own experiments centered around their assigned symptoms and natural products. The secondary project involves using the 'WormBot,' a Kaeberlein lab developed robotic technology to re- screen drugs (that were previously screened by HIS students over the summer) to look for confirmation that they delay the onset of Alzheimer's-related phenotypes in an invertebrate model.
surprised: I'm surprised at how complex the preclinical trial phase of drug creations are.
takeaways: Don't procrastinate and keep a flexible schedule. Whenever you procrastinate, you're not only creating unnecessary stress for yourself, but you're also imposing unreasonable time. You need a flexible schedule because your teammates might procrastinate, have emergencies, not do their work, etc.

description: The Koch Lab at Fred Hutch researches maternal-fetal interactions and how they affect offspring immunity. My mentor focuses on how maternally transmitted antibodies can influence the development of various elements of the immune system, including the gut microbiota. In my virtual internship, I spent time reading scientific articles on various topics related to the lab's research, attending lab meetings, and organizing lab catalogs. I also watched my mentor do experiments through Zoom, which gave me a better idea of what an in- person internship would be like.
unique_experiences: • Watching prep for flow cytometry/ELISAs • Learning about lab routines such as autoclaving and making media • Attending lab meetings and learning about other topics such as cancer research
surprised: The lab culture surprised me the most. Everyone was incredibly friendly and welcoming, which made meetings feel like a genuine conversation with a tight-knit community rather than just work.
takeaways: This internship changed my idea of what a career in research would be like. I've learned a lot about the scientific process and how planning for experiments, especially those involving new procedures, involve more trial and error than I thought. I also really like the lab environment, which was a pleasant surprise and is one of the biggest reasons I'm planning to continue my internship through the summer!

organization: Texas Heart Institute – Center for Clinical Research
description: I researched guidelines and application processes of new treatment operations for a patient with an immediately life-threatening condition or serious disease or condition to gain access to an investigational medical product (drug, biologic, or medical device) for treatment outside of clinical trials when there is no comparable. Specifically, I worked under the Texas Heart Institute's Regulatory Affairs Manager in cardiovascular clinical research regarding FDA approval for Expanded Access Use (Compassionate Use, Emergency Use and Treatment IDE) and Humanitarian Use.
unique_experiences: • Gained valuable knowledge regarding various IRB's (institutional review boards) application review processes for expanded and humanitarian use • Learned how to set up ESG (Electronic Submissions Gateway) account for FDA submission
surprised: I was shocked to learn about how complex regulatory affairs management is within cardiovascular clinical research, especially the unique protocols for various clinical testing submissions processes. Despite many of the obstacles that I learned often occur during the process, each new step proved vitally beneficial to ensuring that the new treatment would be thoroughly reviewed.
takeaways: In speaking and working with my mentor on the extensive processes for the new cardiovascular treatment options the THI team tries to provide using stem cell research for patients who are terminally ill, I have a far greater understanding and sense of gratitude for the professionals in this field.

description: The Vera Project is a non-profit music venue and center for arts and community events. In my time at the Vera as a Marketing and Community Engagement Intern, I was mainly responsible for marketing our upcoming shows using our socials, postering, newsletter, and advertisement so we could get as many ticket sales as possible. For some shows, I was also responsible for allotting our budget to different methods of marketing.
unique_experiences: • Sat in on meetings and learned how music venues are run • Created and helped execute marketing plans for concerts • Attended shows and events to get footage and content for socials • Managed account with >25K followers • Learned about how show deals are made
surprised: I was surprised by how many moving parts have to work together to keep the place running efficiently. It seemed like one of the hardest but most important things to do is keeping everything organized – there are so many tasks that need to be done and without organization, none of it happens. This applies to marketing too – there is a constant flow of things that need to be checked off the list, and without a streamlined process and organization, it's super easy to lose track of it all.
takeaways: Marketing is less about just convincing people that they need something that you are selling, and more about getting the right information to people that would already be interested. It's hard to convince people and easy to just target different people.

description: At the World Affairs Council as a Community Programs intern, I help with event organization for our company's membership events which host various speakers to come and discuss global issues and how they affect us here in Seattle. I also help with some of the social media marketing via creating posts for LinkedIn and Instagram and promoting our community events.
unique_experiences: Helped host events and networked with various speakers including the Mayor of Seattle, NATO Ambassadors, and various other professionals
surprised: I didn't really know what to expect to expect having an office job at 17, but I was really surprised at how welcoming and friendly the office environment was. I was expecting a much more serious environment, but at my office people are always talking and moving around, and overall, it's a really lively space.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from my internship is how important it is to go up to people and introduce yourself. It can definitely be intimidating being in an environment surrounded by adults but putting yourself out there and being confident and outgoing will get you a long way. Take every opportunity to grow your network!

description: Worked with my mentor's clients that were looking to buy homes off-market in King County.
unique_experiences: Worked on my communication with clients and other realtors - Finding off-market properties for clients
surprised: I was surprised how much time I spent calling/texting homeowners, and I was able to learn a lot about how people best respond to someone they don't know reaching out to them
takeaways: I was able to learn how important connections are within the world of Real Estate, although that can apply to other business endeavors as well. My mentor was constantly on the phone talking to different people and worked 24/7 to find deals. This has taught me how important it is to work hard since that is what leads to results. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: Jenna Kamran is a real estate team that focuses on residential properties in California, Arizona and Washington. As part of the team, I contributed to various marketing initiatives and conducted market research to support property evaluations, as well as educating clients on their purchasing. My responsibilities largely centered around marketing projects for various properties. Natalie (my mentor) and the rest of the team were supportive throughout my time there and inspired me to think creatively through each project.
unique_experiences: • Developed a full-scale marketing campaign for $4.3 mil worth of properties • Created CMAs and presented them to clients to inform pricing strategy • Assisted in social media content planning and email and mail outreach
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of behind-the-scenes work that goes into marketing and eventually selling a property. While the product may seem simple at first glance, the strategy behind targeting the right audience and standing out in an already saturated market takes effort and time.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship is that office life, especially in real estate, can often be monotonous, requiring consistent effort for a long period of time. However, by staying disciplined and pushing through, it makes the result much more worthwhile.

description: The City of Bellevue's Transportation Department oversees the development, construction, and maintenance of Bellevue's roads, sidewalks, smart traffic lights, and everything in between. During my time at the City, I worked on Neighborhood Sidewalk Program planning and moved onto designing document formats for current and future sidewalk proposals. My internship mentor Kristi was very kind and accommodating, where internship work was almost completely self- driven.
unique_experiences: Using GIS tools such as Mapshot to view all current and planned sidewalks in Bellevue. - Surrounded by and interacting with city employees, police officers, and firefighters. - Use of Microsoft Publisher, Word, and Excel to complete work. - Witnessing various City-led programs (school fundraisers, projects, etc.) being developed and finished.
surprised: I was surprised that many of the tools used by city government organizations, being the Microsoft Office Suite, were already familiar to me due to already being used for schoolwork. This significantly reduced the learning curve for my internship work and streamlined my productivity.
takeaways: Real-life, no matter if it's work or break, is entirely self-driven and your productivity is completely up to you. I was given almost too much free reign over when and how I completed my work. This tempted me to slack off and do nothing, but I quickly learned to be more responsible and motivated.

description: Worked as a site manager, working closely with architects, engineers, city hall members, and site contractors.
unique_experiences: Bonding with contractors and experiencing soft skills of the job - Understanding litigation and rules that change between cities, ie. Bellevue regulations vs Redmond
surprised: The internship was never truly focused on honing my technical skillset, but maintaining both technical and soft skills that allowed me to communicate efficiently into finishing a project.
takeaways: Building a lot of land, to a house, and then to a home, is far different than what I though went into the job. It is not about the core focus of technical skills, but truly being able to communicate with city hall members, and bonding with contractors, and developing those relationships that truly make the building of house efficient.ss IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: Sensoria Health is a small company headquartered in Redmond that aims to give the healthcare industry tools that help monitor patients remotely, identify issues early and deliver better outcomes. Their vision is to make everyday sensor-driven data and patient self-reported data a trusted part of every patient rehab journey.
unique_experiences: As an intern, I mainly worked with the Smart Garment Engineer, and we wired and tested thousands of products. However, because the company was so small (only 4 people in the building I worked in), I was able to jump around to learn what everyone was working on. For example, I spent a week with the Electrical Engineer who taught me how to sauter and program each of the chips. I also worked with the Program Manager and the CEO.
surprised: I was most surprised by the independence I had in my work. Once I was taught how to do something, they let me continue doing it and it was up to me to give them updates or let them know if I ran into any problems or had any questions.
takeaways: It's nice to go into an internship with an open-mind and A LOT of questions so you can learn the most possible. This internship was a lot of busy work, but I learned so much about the people working there and about small businesses.

description: Professor Das guided me through the process of completing a machine learning project. I proposed several topic ideas related to my interests, and he gave advice on how to approach them. Then, I explored strategies for cleaning and processing a dataset in Excel. Finally, I tested various classification algorithms and parameter tuning techniques to try and maximize my model's accuracy. Along the way, I learned about some of the math and statistics behind these algorithms.
unique_experiences: Solving a machine learning problem through classification algorithms like KNN, Naïve Bayes, XGBoost, and more - Applying data processing techniques to a real dataset - Learning about tools to identify image features
surprised: I was surprised how much freedom and agency I received in working on my project. I was able to center my topic around my interests, which made it a lot more enjoyable. Moreover, my mentor's relatively hands-off approach allowed me to experiment and learn a lot more.
