takeaways: My major takeaway from this internship experience is to not be afraid to seek help and ask your mentor questions. Additionally, I learned how helpful it can be to take advantage of online resources and tutorials.

description: Worked on multiple open source projects and implemented features on company product as requested by mentor.
unique_experiences: Worked on my test-driven development skills, I had never written a unit test prior to this internship so it was a different experience to write tests first then write code. - Worked on my documentation skills, writing pr-faqs and white papers for my work was a core aspect of it.
surprised: I was surprised by how much slower development in a professional environment was, with a whole suite of testing, it took hours to even check if your code was sound, but this also meant that you would have a lot less bugs in production.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience was learning to think from a customer perspective. Something that is very important from a product design perspective is thinking for and about the customer. It doesn't matter how the product is built, all that matters is the customer experience. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: Kingdom Coding is a small non-profit organization that teaches basic programming and robotics to children in Washington, Arizona, and more. With the goal of making STEM as accessible as possible, I worked with Kingdom Coding to teach programming to K-12 students and developed a website to expand its reach.
unique_experiences: Developing a website from start to finish - Teaching students in Washington and Arizona how to code
surprised: I was surprised by how important good communication is even on a team of a few people. Failing to properly note finished tasks or progress would often lead to overlapping efforts.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience is to always stay organized and keep an accurate log of the things you work on. Having to constantly check if yours or a colleague's task is properly finished wastes time that could otherwise be saved if done and documented the first time.

description: XCMeet.com is a free cross country timing system based in Bellevue. It is designed to be used for medium sized races, especially focused on the high school level. Primarily, I worked as a full stack developer, with my work mostly revolving around cleaning up and redesigning old features, as well as working on the splits system, which I implemented from the ground up.
unique_experiences: I designed and implemented the entire splits feature at XCMeet.com, and got feedback and testing from cross country coaches at an official league meet during the season.
surprised: I was surprised at how self-paced my learning was. While our boss, Coach John, did guide us with larger features and code reviews, a lot of the implementation and programming (I didn't know SQL or JavaScript starting the internship) was learned independently.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience was being able to account for the large number of situations and uses (and misuses) the system would go through. Being able to recognize all these situations and test them was a key part of my work at this internship.

description: Robodub is a Seattle-based aerospace startup focused on the development of innovative drone systems equipped with multi-axis, morphing arms designed to optimize power efficiency across a range of flight conditions. As an intern at Robodub, I had the opportunity to engage in physics-based modeling of various drone configurations. Using simulation software, I derived equations governing the motion and dynamics of specific arm formations and contributed to the ongoing exploration of optimal structural arrangements to enhance torque generation and overall performance.
unique_experiences: I worked on an engineering project using SciLab to model various drone dynamics. While I didn't work directly with hardware, I simulated key properties such as angular acceleration, velocity, torque, and moment of inertia, gaining practical experience in aerospace modeling.
surprised: The entire engineering experience during this internship was deeply rooted in physics, with a strong emphasis on applying core principles such as circular motion.
takeaways: • Modeled drone dynamics using SciLab and physics principles • Deepened understanding of torque and angular motion • Saw how simulation informs drone design and innovation

description: OpenEXA is a financial tech startup company focused on providing a variety of financial services tied to cryptocurrency, from providing traders with access to liquid credit from the OpenEXA token in exchange for other non-liquid assets as collateral, to having AI agents that utilize predictive models to make profitable trades on the market, advise clients of potential beneficial trades identified, and make commission off other bots who follow these trades. As a software development intern, I helped create some of these predictive models and built a custom API to integrate models into the flow for the AI agents. Team meetings take place every school day and stand-up meetings occur with the mentor once a week to ensure work is being completed.
unique_experiences: Working with other interns and members from different places and ages - Learning how to build, update, and fine-tune machine learning models - Learning how to upload models and apps onto the cloud
surprised: I was surprised by the flexibility of the internship and how rewarding it felt to make meaningful progress and complete the tasks delegated to me, especially as this internship requires you to learn as you go.
takeaways: Everyone plays a part in making a product work - Things work best when everyone has delegated work yet help each other out - Showing your value early and working hard will give you the best experience

description: OpenExa works on predicting stock and crypto movements using pattern recognition and news articles. The company uses AI to interpret news articles and stock movements. As an intern I coded an algorithm to interpret news articles and email the user back whether to buy or sell a certain stock. While I was initially lost with the help of my mentor, I was able to learn.
unique_experiences: • Learning how to code stock visualizers and uploading them to clouds • Researching and deciding which APIs and AI tools to use for different tasks. • Coding with AI tools to interpret and manage results
surprised: I was surprised how much could be changed when it comes to stock prediction algorithms. I expected stock prediction algorithms to be very precise and tuned however with the constant introduction of new tools in recent times there are many ways to enhance stock prediction algorithms.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is to always find ways to enhance my work. If I were to attempt my project 1 year ago it would be incredibly inaccurate compared to today due to the lack of tools and their development. Using that logic in the following year I expect there to be many more advances and new tools and if I use them to enhance my code it will be significantly more consistent.

description: Inclusive Technology of Washington (ITW) is a non-profit dedicated to teaching technology life skills to underserved communities, like disabled and rurally-located students. Many of these students rely on Washington DSHS services like Respite and Specialized Habilitation to pay for the classes they take with ITW. My job was to bill these students through the DSHS portal and program a software that automates the billing process.
unique_experiences: • Coordinated with DSHS Case Managers and HCA service providers • Learned the ins and outs of government disability services, like Respite • Independently led and oversaw a programming project
surprised: I was surprised to see how outdated many of the tools ITW relied on for their billing were. At a small non-profit scale, there's many systems that may easily be streamlined but aren't due to a lack of proximity to people with knowledge and experience doing so. Filling in these gaps was hard work.
takeaways: • Lead communications when you need to, and push for recurring systems (like Internship meetings, sending weekly updates, etc.) when possible • Researching and learning skills on-the-job (like different coding languages) is crucial when working on a constantly adapting coding project

description: OpenEXA is an AI -driven company that allows investors to enhance their portfolio returns with minimal risks by using traditional assets to borrow and trade cryptocurrencies. By providing services such as data visualization and analysis of ETF sentiments, OpenEXA caters to asset owners, fund managers, and traders seeking to making profitable investment strategies.
unique_experiences: • Developed an ETF sentiment analysis workflow using n8n, automating emails that provide users with actionable insights – such as buy, hold, or sell recommendations – based on online sentiments. • Gained hands-on experience with Docker, Google Cloud, and Azure ML company subscriptions to deploy applications from real-time ticker visualizations to local n8n workflows.
surprised: • Having to adapt quickly to a fast-paced environment where flexibility and rapid execution were essential, often shifting priorities to deliver results within weekly deadlines. • Gaining portfolio-worthy project experience and was positively surprised by the CEO's direct involvement with interns, which significantly boosted team morale and engagement.
takeaways: • Progress isn't just individual; it's also about moving forward together as a team in an aligned manner. • Maintain open communication by asking thoughtful questions and welcoming feedback on your work. • Stay eager to learn new tools, interfaces, and platforms to maximize your contribution to projects and experience.

description: OpenEXA is a fintech startup aiming to connect traditional Wall Street assets with cryptocurrency markets by offering on-chain credit—an essential step to expanding participation in the crypto ecosystem. The platform holds custody of real-world assets like bonds and issues corresponding credit in the form of a credit token (OpenEXA Stable Credit Token) or a collateral token (Asset Unique Token). By doing so, OpenEXA delivers the credit framework, entry point, and infrastructure needed for traditional asset managers to access and operate within the crypto space.
unique_experiences: Worked with multiple APIs, graphing the stock market. Additionally, I worked with Docker, Azure, and google cloud.
surprised: I was surprised with the degrees of freedom given to every intern. This gave me the space to grow my own interest, and the confidence to tackle new subjects.
takeaways: Even in individually-oriented experiences, it's really important to ask question and collaborate. Your internship is only as meaningful as you make of it.

description: OpenEXA is a fintech company that aims to make investing easier for the average person. It utilizes AI models and cryptocurrencies to aid the average person in making quality investments. Projects ranged from creating website to AI chatbot web flows
unique_experiences: Learning web development and building webpages that chart financial data of various companies - Understanding and utilizing AI sentient models - Build flows that allow AI to analyze financial news and make a decision using n8n
surprised: I was surprised at the level of research and self-education on new technologies that is needed to. Crypto and AI are a complex topic and I had to exert full dedication and interest to learn them quickly.
takeaways: This was my first experience working in a company and was exposed to the quick nature of tasks. I felt my research and technical abilities improving while being satisfied that my work was meaningful.

description: OpenEXA is a technology-driven company focused on increasing the accessibility and liquidity of digital assets. We began with training on foundational topics such as GitHub, and JavaScript, ensuring all participants had the necessary technical skills. Afterward, interns were divided into teams to work on a variety of real-world projects, including AI chatbots and website development, allowing for both creative freedom and collaboration.
unique_experiences: • Lots of Project Choices: We got to pick from a bunch of cool projects—like AI, websites, and crypto resaercg. • Tried More Than One Thing: Most of us ended up working on more than one project, which kept things interesting.
surprised: I was most surprised by how effectively different teams' independent projects could be integrated to create larger, cohesive systems. It was impressive to see how modular development and team collaboration came together to solve complex problems.
takeaways: One of the biggest things I learned was how important teamwork is in tech projects. Even if you know your part well, communicating and syncing with your team makes everything smoother and more fun.

description: Stumbling Cat is an indie game studio based in Seattle, known for creating the game Potions: A Curious Tale. As an intern for Stumbling Cat, I was able to explore the game development process by creating new content for Potions: A Curious Tale. I worked with my internship mentor to learn about both design and coding within Unity.
unique_experiences: • Designing and implementation of puzzles, bosses, and quests • Participation in a gaming convention
surprised: I was surprised by how much freedom I was given during this internship. I was able to choose what projects I wanted to work on throughout the year, and my mentor helped guided me through the process.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was my experience with the game design process, as well as the conventions and strategies associated with it. I learned about how important aspects of games are implemented, such as dialog, monsters, and more.

description: As an intern at Stumbling Cat, you will be creating new content for the game Potions: A Curious Tale using the Unity game engine. This internship will allow you to gain the skills and experience working on an indie game and become familiar with Unity,
unique_experiences: • Implementing quests, puzzles, and bosses • Designing quests, puzzles, and bosses • Attending a gaming convention
surprised: What surprised me the most was how much creative freedom I had when designing my own quests, puzzles, and bosses.
takeaways: Gaining hands-on experience in a professional game development pipeline and developing practical skills in Unity (scripting, asset integration, etc.).

description: Support the law firm by providing legal research, drafting documents, managing case files, nonprofit law etc… If you want to do business, there is a lot of nonprofit management and marketing stuff to do. Also, it is in a bilingual environment, so Chinese is preferred but not needed. A lot of the internship will be helping to manage the international law society and doing community outreach.
unique_experiences: Helping manage/review/contribute to actual legal case files - Interacting with others in a law firm like an employee - Starting up the International Law Society, a global nonprofit - Working with interns from other schools - More
surprised: What surprised me the most was how intensive yet laid back a law firm can be. Some days there will be no work, yet other days everybody is screaming and arguing
takeaways: You probably shouldn't go into law for the money, you should go if you want to help people

description: During my internship, I gained valuable experience with SLG's client management system (Clio). I was often responsible for drafting and sending closing letters as well as ensuring their file management system was kept up to date as cases were closed and re- opened. I was also responsible for keeping company social media up to date by editing videos and blog posts providing information about legal concepts and civil rights.
unique_experiences: Reading about potential clients to summarize their intake notes for attorneys - Talking to clients on the phone to understand their case
surprised: It surprised me how limited the scope of most law firms is, as SLG often referred potential clients to other firms since they were outside of their scope
takeaways: I learned a lot about the importance of keeping thorough records of all business activities, so everyone is on the same page

description: During this unpaid internship, I worked eight hours per week with King County Councilmember Claudia Balducci's team. I went into the office 1-2 days per week and did the rest of the internship remotely. My main roles included conducting research, writing communications, analyzing constituent feedback, and attending educational community events.
unique_experiences: Research to inform policy development - Research for constituent response - Community events - Communications (weekly newsletter writing, proclamations, speeches, etc.) - Analysis of survey results - Evaluation of funding requests
surprised: When reading constituent letters or open-ended survey responses, it always surprised me how many different opposing views and potential solutions those in our area have. I am impressed that Balducci is able to represent such a wide range of people.
takeaways: I feel that my writing skills greatly improved over the course of this internship. This is because I did lots of week on Balducci's weekly newsletters, wrote letters back to constituents, and made writeups about programs or initiatives after researching them. (The team is not explicitly looking for an intern next year but may accept one if interest is shown)

description: The City of Bellevue Stream Team is a volunteer organization that is managed by Laurie Devereaux. I interned under her, soI had a much broader scope than just Stream Team. With this internship, I leaned how to plan, enact, and evaluate a marketing program. We piloted the "Check for Drips, Avoid Costly Trips!" campaign. Beyond that, I also attended many public outreach events, professional development seminars, and helped with the education involved in stream team.
unique_experiences: • Designed and founded a marketing campaign • Organized Salmon Watchers and Peamouth Patrol meeting • Attended the Pacific Northwest Social Marketing Seminar
surprised: I was most surprised about how important it was to have a good relationship with your mentor. I had volunteered at Stream Team for about a year prior to starting this internship, and so I already knew my mentor well. This allowed me to get this internship relatively easily. Additionally, I was surprised about how many opportunities there are to connect with other people in this field. I got to go to so many seminars and networking events.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was about how enjoyable this experience was. I went in, just wanting to not have to take many classes my senior year, but I got really invested in the work I was doing and learned to care about and have fun with it.

description: Seattle Litigation Group offers a hands-on internship supporting their legal team on active cases involving labor law, discrimination, and civil litigation. Interns assist with client interaction, case development, and legal document drafting while gaining real-world legal experience in a professional setting.
unique_experiences: Collaborating with legal professionals on active litigation projects - Enhancing professional communication skills to improve clarity and team efficiency - Interacting directly with clients seeking legal support - Learning how legal documents function in the context of ongoing cases
surprised: What surprised me most was how approachable and communicative the team was. I initially felt intimidated by having to work with seasoned legal professionals, but their openness and authenticity made it easy to ask questions. Everyone is willing to support you.
takeaways: Don't be afraid to communicate. Every question you have is valid. If you have skills from outside the immediate scope of your role, it's worth speaking up. People appreciate initiative and creativity, and offering your skills can lead to new opportunities within the project. Being open, curious, and willing to contribute in unexpected ways can make your experience even more fulfilling.

description: Admon Law Firm provides legal counsel to various civil disputes in the Pacific Northwest Area. Admon Law Firm has a strong emphasis on clear communication with clients and quality of work. During my time at Admon Law Firm, I researched landlord/tenant law, contract law, medical law, employment law, and collision law. I drafted many demand letters and documents submitted to courts. Mr. Admon was very nice. However, recently work has gotten incredibly complex and there are less opportunities allowed for learning/work.
unique_experiences: Calling clients - Writing demand letters - Negotiating with opposing counsel
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of work that lawyers do. I was also surprised by the cutthroat nature of law.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience is to pay attention to detail. Law is an area where details matter a lot, so I have gotten into the habit of rereading my work many times. In addition, I have gotten incredibly better at communicating with many different people.

description: As an intern at UW Medicine's Shoreline Clinic, I was responsible for shadowing and assisting my mentor, Nick Dunlap, who is a medical assistant. I was responsible for greeting and rooming patients from the waiting room, taking their vitals (pulse, temperature, blood pressure), taking patient history and entering that data into the patients electronic health records via Epic, assisting my mentor with calling patients who were late or needed to reschedule their appointment, presenting the patient history to the physician, and bringing in an interpreter when needed.
unique_experiences: There were a couple times when a patient had an interpreter in the room and we weren't sure how accurately the interpreters were relaying the questions we were asking the patients (based on the patient's answers). Interpreters sometimes don't have a medical background, so I learned how important it is to cross-check with family members or caregivers if they are around and maybe invite them into the room or call them if needed. We often had kids in the clinic who needed to get vaccines and they wouldn't stop crying because they were scared. Sometimes, my job was to help the parents calm them down by holding their hand, giving them a book or toy to play with, or giving them stickers.
surprised: I learned a lot about the behind-the-scenes work medical assistants to do get the room ready. It's a lot of tasks to juggle, which we often don't hear about. They are the first people typically that patients talk to when entering the clinic, so they are the ones that really set the stage for the patient's experience.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship experience was being able to meet and interact with so many different kinds of people. At the clinic, we saw people who were feeling angry, scared, happy, calm, and everything in between, and it was our job to help not only treat the disease or symptoms they were experiencing but to understand and get to know them as a person. I also learned the importance of treating people well, and the difference even a smile can make. As a clinic, we're expected to be a source of strength and constancy for people, even during turbulent times like the November windstorm. Just showing up and doing our job is so powerful and means a lot to people.

organization: Bleakley Lab @ Fred Hutch Cancer Center
description: The Bleakley Lab focuses on Graft-vs.-Host Disease prevention through developing and experimenting strategies to engineer hematopoietic stem cell grafts with clinical trials and lab work. The lab is also searching for novel leukemia-associated minor histocompatibility antigens for future immunotherapy and graft development. My clinical trial responsibilities included data entry and organization and writing clinical trial result summaries for ClinicalTrials.gov. My wet lab responsibilities included SNP-typing sequencing and tissue culture for different donor cell types (PBMCs, LCLs, etc.).
unique_experiences: Attending seminars by well-known researchers, such as Jennifer Doudna, who presented on her invention CRISPR - Draft clinical trial result summaries for CTGov (official website for federally funded clinical trials) - Sequence patient samples and contribute to larger mHA discovery project
surprised: There are many intersections between clinical trials and laboratory work. It fascinated me that the clinical trials' patient data and blood samples were both used for larger projects of the lab in different ways.
takeaways: When working in a large team, it is very important to write down everything you are doing, such as procedures in the lab, what the results mean and where to find them, since others may use your results in their future projects.

description: The UW Department of Surgical Pathology receives patient samples in the Gross Lab and processes them into slides that pathologists review to diagnose or confirm a patient's condition. My experience was mainly focused on reviewing patient cases with Dr. Reiter, who specializes in OB/GYN pathology. We looked at various histology slides and signed out cases related to cancers and tumors of the breast, female reproductive system, and fetal membranes. I also worked with some of the fellows, Michael and Rachel, to go over more complex cases and got to learn about specimen dissection.
unique_experiences: • Saw rare conditions under the microscope • Touched and grossed fascinating specimens • Listened in on interviewing/hiring process • Diagnosed cases from China and Ethiopia through consult
surprised: I was surprised by how many conditions—both benign and cancerous—exist just within OB/GYN pathology. I didn't expect that a small sample could create so many slides that hold millions of cells, each with clues that help the pathologist diagnose the patient.
takeaways: One of my biggest takeaways was realizing how much patience and dedication is needed in diagnostic medicine. When I first started, the slides looked like inkblots—but now I can recognize patterns and understand how different conditions present under the microscope.

description: This clinic specializes in providing neuropsychological evaluations for adults, both in a medical and legal context. From a medical standpoint, the patients can develop their treatment plan and attend psychotherapy at the clinic. In legal cases, the clinic performs cognitive health/ability assessments of defendants to aid as an expert witness. My role as an intern was primarily to help with development of patient resources, paperwork such as prepping and scanning patient charts, and scoring tests and making diagnostic tables.
unique_experiences: Experiencing how cognitive tests work firsthand as a test subject - Learning software for scoring diagnostic tests for patients - Gaining exposure to issues dealing with medical corporate system as a small independent clinic
surprised: I was surprised to learn about the frequency of neurocognitive issues that arise after accidents like workplace injuries or in most cases, car crashes. Even a seemingly minor incident could lead to a patient facing a traumatic brain injury, leading them to seek out treatment. I had initially assumed the clinic would mostly deal with patients with dementia, so learning about the various types of issues that patients come in for was quite interesting.
takeaways: One of my greatest takeaways was that running a small clinic requires a tight-knit community. I hadn't expected to work on such different tasks with multiple people, but it was fun to find out how everyone was flexible in sharing tasks to optimize efficiency. I enjoyed working in a small team very much, and am grateful for the experience!

description: As a dental intern, my primary task was to help turn over patients by assisting in running sterile and preparing exam rooms, with the secondary goal of gaining insight into the dental and medical field by shadowing and researching outside of the clinic.
unique_experiences: • Observed dental procedures (cleanings, sealants, extractions, etc.) • Shadowed conveniently located next-door orthodontic and oral surgery clinics • Learning and assisting daily operations of a private pediatric dentistry • Required certification in bloodborne pathogens
surprised: The importance of communication and interpersonal skills. The social- emotional side of patient care—making kids comfortable, asking questions, assuring parents—often overshadows technical skill and knowledge.
takeaways: Being part of a healthcare operation for the first time reshaped my perception of dentistry and healthcare. I had once thought of medicine as a field of treatment, but now I understand that its largest impact something else - A truly meaningful clinic is based upon prevention, arming patients with the knowledge to treat themselves for their lifetimes. Whether in dentistry, medicine, or any other sector, I now see the importance of fostering long-term, positive change through education.

description: This internship was focused on utilizing fundamental molecular biology techniques related to T-cell immunology at Fred Hutch Cancer Center, a research facility that strives to prevent and eliminate cancer/infectious diseases. In this internship, you'll learn about the basic principles of immunology before working your way through the process of designing synthetic gene constructs.
unique_experiences: • Learned college-level immunology concepts (and then applied them in the lab!) • Edited the DNA of target vectors via restriction enzymes and ligation process • Altered native receptors of T-cells using real DNA sequences
surprised: Besides doing research work in a lab environment, my mentor also takes care of patients in the UW Medical Center! The immunology concepts that he learns about while researching go hand in hand with his treatment of patients who are recovering from bone marrow transplants. It surprised me to see that my mentor's work schedule was flexible enough to accommodate two different roles in the medical field.
takeaways: Overall, I learned to enjoy the process of achieving my goal. There were months dedicated to planning out and preparing steps in my T-cell project, but once the process of testing my experiment came, the results only took a few days. Most of your time in this internship will be spent preparing solutions instead of testing on cells, so it's best to embrace every step, no matter how trivial it may seem.

location: 15446 NE Bel Red Rd #300, Redmond, WA 98052
description: Bel Red Pediatric Dentistry is a pediatric dental practice in Bel-Red, Washington. Throughout the internship, I shadowed Dr. Chen in his check-up appointments, as well as working as a scribe and taking dental notes. Dr. Chen also made time to teach me how to recognize dental notation, how to examine x-rays and how to recognize different oral conditions.
unique_experiences: Shadowing Dr. Chen during check-ups and surgeries Working as a scribe and changing notes during check-ups Learning about dentistry with Dr. Chen
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of set-up and preparation work needed to do for one check-up or one surgery. Each person in the office worked on one specific task, and it was clear how teamwork made everything run smoothly.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience is how much knowledge I can learn if I learn slowly one day at a time. I entered this internship without any knowledge about dentistry, but I was able to grasp so much knowledge by staying curious.

description: Lake Hills Orthodontics provides orthodontic care to patients of all ages. They provide consultations for a variety of orthodontic treatments, provide financial planning for treatment options, and remote monitoring systems to track treatment progression.
unique_experiences: • Scanned teeth and used modelling software to print resin models with 3D printer • Shadow appointments and treatment consultations • Made and packaged retainers for patients
surprised: What surprised me most was the level of precision that is required in every aspect of orthodontic care. From making individualized retainers to scanning teeth for digital modeling, every step was tailored to each patient's unique dental structure. I also was surprised at how much digital technology was integrated into orthodontics.
takeaways: This internship helped me understand the intersection between patient care and hands-on technical work in the dental field. I gained a better understanding about the behind-the-scenes work that goes into orthodontics.

description: Vision Care Center is an optometry clinic in Bellevue that provides eye care. I worked on organizing around the office, taking inventory of glasses, helping with decorations, and scanning in patient documents. By staying at the clinic, I was also able to get a feel for the typical workday of an optometrist and see the work that went into running their business.
unique_experiences: learn what what goes into running a private practice as an eye doctor -opportunities for shadowing eye exams -help with work around an eye clinic -manage and upload patient records to system
surprised: I was surprised at how much advanced eye technology there is now, like a machine that automatically reads glasses prescriptions and a device that takes comprehensive pictures of the eye instead of using traditional dilation.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is not to be discouraged by tasks that seem large. Even if there is a lot of material to organize and sort through, you can make steady progress and get it done.

description: Working on a project within the Blanco-Melo lab investigating the functional difference between immune antagonizing proteins C and V from a 1912 MeV isolate to modern circulating MeV genotypes to overall further our understanding of host-pathogen evolution. Also learning various methods of computational biology, such as R, which allows us to make complex coverage plots of various viruses.
unique_experiences: Through this internship, I had a couple of unique experiences, the first being the ability to work with the first known measles virus hands-on (1912 MeV isolate). Additionally, I was also able to attend and participate in a variety of scientific talks about the development of viruses over time, which correlated with my interest in history too. Finally, I was able to independently perform experiments to understand if we were able to sequence both 1912 and 1954 MeV isolates, looking for genomic differences.
surprised: I was most surprised by how my internship differed from my experience in school, at my internship, asking questions was the foundation of learning, not being taught material.
takeaways: Through this internship, I learned various skills that I can apply to my higher education moving forward, such as a newfound interest in virology/history, a mastery of various lab procedures such as PCR's, inoculation, sequencing, gel electrophoresis, etc.

category: Art, Design, Architecture and Technical Productions
description: Seattle Opera is a non-profit arts organization known for its world-class productions of classic and contemporary operas. During my time at Seattle Opera, I worked primarily with the P+P Department and the Development Department.
unique_experiences: Assisted with youth programs such as Youth Opera Project and Teen Vocal Studio (Making shadow puppets, dyeing t-shirts, creating stage sets, etc.) - Prepared scores, librettos, costumes and props for the School Opera Tour - Learned from professional opera performances and competitions - Analyzed and organized audience data in Tessitura
surprised: I found it incredible that Seattle Opera could be both a renowned professional opera company and remain deeply committed to promoting arts accessibility. They offer so many ways for the community to engage opera, such as Youth Opera Project, Teen Vocal Studio, Black Artists Market, etc.
takeaways: It was really inspiring to witness our diverse community engaging closely with opera—an art form often seen as costly or exclusive—in such an inclusive and approachable manner.

description: Eat Happy Now is a food rescue non-profit that partners with donors, volunteers, and recipients, seamlessly connecting businesses with surplus, edible food to deliverers who bring these food items to people and communities in need. I researched potential donors (usually corner stores, cafes, and bakeries), constructed a schedule for Eat Happy's goals, and created social media posts. Eat Happy has a flexible, accommodating schedule that interns take the responsibility of setting themselves.
unique_experiences: • Delivering food to often unnoticed but valuable parts of our community • Networking with other students and corporate higher-ups at volunteer meets • Conducting research about the corporate role in fighting food waste
surprised: I was surprised by the level of detail and coordination required to effectively run a non-profit. Eat Happy is a coordinating non-profit, meaning there are many moving parts and items to track, including across county and state lines.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from my internship is to ensure the work you do is well-planned and well-thought out. Hours are flexible and you meet them according to the volume of work that you do, rather than by time spent in an office, so you must make sure you know what jobs there are to do and when and how to do them.

description: Loki's Loop is a project of the University of Washington Center for an Informed Public. They created their first escape room game, Euphorigen, in 2020 and have continued to make escape rooms and other games aimed at helping people navigate misinformation. I helped manage their website and create materials for new games and localizations, working with groups across Europe and Africa.
unique_experiences: • Creating online and printable localization puzzle materials for games. • Helping run game play-testing sessions and Misinfo Day with graduate students. • Creating, re-designing, and updating website pages.
surprised: I was surprised about the variety of work and details I needed to consider when designing materials for a multitude of audiences across the world from
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is how important it is to know your audience and how to be aware of the assumptions we subconsciously make. Design, especially for a non-profit, needs to be centered around the humans that are going to use them.

description: The Holocaust Center for Humanity is the only museum in Washington State that educates on the history of Holocaust and other genocides globally. As such, my time here consisted of considerable work in curricula development, teacher trainings, and public outreach events. Paul, my internship mentor, is extremely kind and dedicated to this field, and I am sure he would help any future student passionate about these subjects.
unique_experiences: Doing research that exposed artwork in the Bellevue Public Library as made by a former, ardent Nazi. I made this call upon request from the library. - Facilitating educator training weekends/workshops that even had me meeting with a survivor of the Japanese internment camps from Bainbridge Island. - Conducting historical research to develop new/updated lesson plans.
surprised: I was most surprised by how critical state and large-actor funding is to smaller non-profits like the Holocaust center. Almost every other task was related to raising funds so the center could extend its resources and mission across the whole state.
takeaways: Do not be afraid to engage with difficult topics, even those as painful as genocide studies. Hard conversations are a necessary step towards healing past memories, and the center has taught me to approach these spaces with professionalism, care, and attentiveness.

location: Benjamin Hall Interdisciplinary Research Building/UW Medical Center
description: Our team at UW's Department of Radiation Oncology focuses on developing novel therapies to specifically target various cancers with minimal collateral damage. At Benjamin Hall, I helped distill and filter various chemicals and antibodies for other researchers' experiments. At the Medical Center, I ran thermal simulations and collected experimental data on cyclotron targets in order to ensure the targets' design was enough to withstand the heat of the cyclotron, allowing us to produce astatine-211, a key component in molecular radiotherapy.
unique_experiences: • Hands-on experience with lab techniques like distillation and isoelectric focusing • Operating physics simulations and learning how to match simulation outcomes with experimental data • Troubleshooting both experimental and simulation designs and data collection
surprised: The flexibility of most researchers' schedules. Everyone in our team had certain goals and rough deadlines to meet, but no one really knows how long it will take to achieve said goals, resulting in very unstructured schedules and lots of flexibility.
takeaways: When it comes to research, be patient. Oftentimes, the slower and more carefully you go about your research, the higher quality it will be. Rushing through research is thus generally not recommended, especially when you have to present your research to your higher-ups and try to convince them that you're not going down a rabbit hole.

description: The Geng Lab is currently screening environmental toxins from the ToxCast library - a chemical collection curated by the EPA - to discover environmental chemicals that can negatively impact the development of social behavior and potentially lead to increased autism risk, using zebrafish as an experimental model. During my time at the lab, I assisted with lab work as well as compiling and analyzing data in Excel.
unique_experiences: • Learning how to use various lab equipment • Observing gene editing using Crispr-cas9 methods • Attending a lab member's PhD dissertation defense
surprised: I was surprised at how much time and effort it takes to write a research paper. Every word has to be confirmed and double-checked, and every detail that could possibly affect the results is included.
takeaways: Talk to people. Even if they're working on a different project, you can learn a lot just from asking a couple questions. Communication and collaboration are key.

description: Fred Hutch is a research organization seeking to understand and address the impact of cancer and infectious disease. The Blanco-Melo lab studies the immune response to viral infection and seeks to enable the design of therapeutics for current and emerging viral threats.
unique_experiences: Performing techniques like PCR and bacterial transformation - Learning and observing others in the lab to learn new procedures - Reading papers on relevant scientific topics
surprised: I was surprised by how much time scientific research takes. For example, if I started an experiment on Monday and continued working on it throughout the week, I wouldn't know the result of the experiment until the next week.
takeaways: Things often don't work on the first try. Especially in research, being prepared for failures and being able to rebound is really important for success.

location: Seattle (307 Westlake Ave and 1916 Boren Ave)
description: The Vaughan lab is part of the Center for Global Infectious Disease at Seattle Children's Research Institute. My mentors, Lucia Pazzagli and Ashley Vaughan, are using genetic recombination to create a Pan-African strain of malaria, which will make future vaccine research more accurate and efficient. As an intern, I organize the lab and do hands-on work to collect and record data for experiments.
unique_experiences: Attending weekly meetings and hearing about global conferences and current developments in vaccine research -Learning wet lab skills like PCR, IFA, and gel electrophoresis -Using the micro slicer to collect samples of humanized mice liver -Learning to use fluorescent microscopy to find and measure the size of parasites in slices of infected liver
surprised: I was most surprised by my conversations with the scientists about how they chose vaccine research. Almost every single person in the lab moved from a different country solely to work at Seattle Children's, and each with different reason for why they love the work. Hearing the experiences of scientists from all over the world has helped me develop a more realistic and nuanced view of scientific research as a career path.
takeaways: With high-level research, there is a lot to be confused about. My main takeaway would be to make up for inexperience with curiosity and genuine engagement. The main purpose of being an intern is to learn, so never be afraid to ask questions!

description: The Dey Lab at Fred Hutch focuses on research into colorectal cancer (CRC) and how gut microbiota affects health. My role as an intern at the lab was primarily focused on applying machine learning models to analyze data, but I also had the opportunity to contribute directly to the manuscript and spent much longer than I'd like to admit just reading papers.
unique_experiences: • On my first dey (haha), my de facto mentor actually tested me on what I knew about machine learning, which everyone told me would NOT happen • Worked with real data on CRC presence among Alaskan native populations and created diagnostic ML models • Applied SVM, random forest, and clustering models to categorize participants but also read a lot of relevant literature
surprised: To be honest, when I first started my internship, I felt like I was totally thrown off the deep end. I was surprised by the difficulty of the tasks that my mentor assigned to me, but it was also a bit of a wake-up call. There is quite a discrepancy between classroom learning and applied research (no matter what field) and reconciling that is hard!
takeaways: "Growth for the sake of growth is the ideology of a cancer cell. " There is a sort of culture in research that publishing is like somehow the main objective, and it's important to realize that there are real people your work affects, and it's ultimately the ability of a paper to bring positive impact that will get it recognized.
mentor_name: Neelendu Dey (technically, but I worked mostly with Dr. Maysam Orouskhani, who's unfortunately informed me that he does not plan to stay at the lab)

description: Dr. Ebi at the UW department of Global Health needs interns to conduct literature reviews for an upcoming study on heat waves and their impacts on global health. Interns will choose and examine online literature, removing papers not fit for the scope of the study. Interns will then compile remaining studies into a comprehensive literature review document.
unique_experiences: Working as part of a large group of researchers on a comprehensive worldwide study - Reading and compile research papers, considering gaps in research and evaluating merits of studies - Seeing research evolve as time goes on
surprised: I was most surprised at how flexible my schedule was with this internship, as I was able to complete work mostly on my own time, as long as it got finished, while at the same time learning loads of knowledge on a subject that interested me.
takeaways: Even if you feel working through the minutia can be repetitive and difficult, working towards a final result that you can be proud of is incredibly rewarding. And if you're ever confused, ask questions. Your mentor is there to help.

description: Conducted research under Principal Investigator Dr. Jonathan Tang to create a physical interface of consciousness by using language models to understand psychological patterns. I researched the varying roles of brain parts to isolate the system/area responsible for generating consciousness and interpretations of subjective experiences.
unique_experiences: I was able to develop my own research paper/project on a topic that I was interested in under the guidance of my mentor - Shadowing meetings with other researchers and learning about the exchange of data and information - Observing lab procedures like mice neurosurgery!!
surprised: I was surprised at how researchers networked and reached out to gather more information and data that is not available for public access. This experience highlighted the importance of communication in research as a much more necessary factor than I had previously considered.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship experience is expecting failure and doubt when conducting research. I've learned that progress doesn't come from rehashing over already-answered questions – instead, it comes from challenging and willing to take on new topics that don't have the results predetermined.

description: The Strong lab at Fred Hutch mainly focuses on using protein crystallography to study the functions and binding properties of the immunoprotein siderocalin. During my time here, I learned to do protein sequencing, molecular cloning, and protein crystallization, as well as extract protein crystals for X-Ray diffraction. I also got to learn basic lab procedures like purifying DNA, performing size-exclusion chromatography, and setting up crystallization plates.
unique_experiences: Design and make a protein through protein sequencing and molecular cloning - Learned protein crystallization and used crystal structures to study the function and binding properties of proteins
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of math that I had to do at my internship. Whether it was calculating the correct molar ratio for a solution or finding the absorbance of a sample, I had to do plenty of math for my experiments.
takeaways: There is a lot more collaboration in science than most people think. Though people are working on different projects, they are often related and it's important to discuss results and consult coworkers when you're unsure.

organization: Smith Lab, Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center
description: The Fred Hutch Cancer Center is a biomedical research institute that aims to develop treatments for cancer and infectious disease. The Smith Lab is part of the Basic Sciences Division of the Fred Hutch, which focuses on understanding the relevant biological systems (i.e., DNA replication) to the treatment of disease. One phenomenon that the Smith Lab investigates is crossover interference which informally speaking, is when the probability of two traits being both passed on by a parent to their offspring is less than (or greater than!) what is expected from independence. As part of my internship, I got to learn more about proposed models for crossover interference in S. pombe, a yeast strain, and set up experiments to detect/calculate crossover interference for different genetic intervals in S. pombe.
unique_experiences: • Learning and applying different techniques used by S. pombe geneticists • Reading and discussing papers with my mentor • Enjoying donuts with lab scientists!
surprised: The amount of freedom I had in the lab was surprising as high school students typically aren't given much access to hands-on research. I was largely responsible for deciding what I did in the lab every day and maintaining a lab notebook to keep record of experiments.
takeaways: Don't be afraid to ask questions and take initiative in learning the biological background and techniques used in the lab. And of course, keep a tidy lab notebook! If you don't have something recorded, it never happened — that's what my mentor always reminds me.

category: Machine Learning / Biomedical Informatics / Predictive Analytics
description: Professor Hu's team at the University of Washington Tacoma is leading a graduate-level project focused on rare event prediction in clinical time series. The current focus is on early sepsis detection in Level 1 trauma patients.
unique_experiences: Led data collection and preprocessing for large-scale EHR datasets (MIMIC, UW) using SQL and Python - Collaborated with clinicians to determine clinically relevant features and refine cohort selection criteria and data inconsistencies - Developed and implemented a custom masked autoencoder in PyTorch for time series data to impute missing clinical values
surprised: What surprised me the most was seeing how closely data science and patient care are connected. Our research was able to directly guide clinical decisions in a data-driven manner.
takeaways: During the internship I was able to visit UW Harborview's Trauma 1 ICU patients and observe patient care at the bedside. My greatest takeaway is that while the data can seem like only numbers at times, every data point represents a future at stake.

description: Fred Hutch Cancer Center unites innovative research and compassionate care to prevent and eliminate cancer and infectious disease. I worked with patient samples, generally documenting where they would be in the viral and infectious disease repository. However, I eventually branched out into designing a system to simplify the inputs required for the entire lab.
unique_experiences: • Working in a biorepository, pulling samples from freezers and documenting them (~800+ samples) • Utilizing Microsoft VBA to build a user form for inputting samples into a greater collection • Attending a lab meeting and symposium reviewing real research being done by PHD's in the field
surprised: I was surprised by the style of work being done at the biorepository. A lot of manual work is needed to process samples from years in the past and I found it rewarding to be able to provide help in potentially helping to streamline the processes.
takeaways: Take every opportunity and take initiative - my internship started as simply sorting through boxes of samples. However, after working through some VBA courses I was able to volunteer my knowledge of how sorting works and produce a tool to simplify manual inputs and reduce error. Reach out to your mentor and figure out what resources you have available – I got free certifications in VBA, Handling Biological Substances, etc. from Fred Hutch.

description: At the UW Chemistry Gelb Lab, we focused on the human genome with real- world applications in newborn-related research. The Gelb Lab has a history of winning awards in the field, with lab technicians ranging from undergraduates to PHD research. I served as an assistant to these researchers, and my responsibilities included compiling research data, reading up on papers posted by our lab and identifying potential impurities in samples.
unique_experiences: Experience with wet-lab spectrometry & preparation of chemicals - Attended courses and conferences with other undergraduates and PHDs - Processed and organized data to be used for future research
surprised: I was surprised by how many different projects were being worked on at the same time in a single lab, often by the same people. Rather than being focused on one project at a time, several smaller projects were always taking place.
takeaways: This internship allowed me to learn how to conduct research that has real- world impacts, and the ability to take initiative on projects that had been put aside or ignored. I also learned how research is conducted and how that research is processed before being sent to publications and peer review.

description: AIMI Center was established with the primary mission of developing medical AI applications and conducting biomedical imaging research to advance patient health. My mentor specialized in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and from her, I learned how MRI functions and how ML is used to create high- quality images, even in scenarios of under-sampling.
unique_experiences: • Learning about current research around using AI to improve and save lives • Utilizing MATLAB to create quantitative graphs for research papers • Analyzing research papers and conducting thorough literature reviews
surprised: I was surprised by the diversity of the people involved. My mentor collaborated with radiologists, PhD students, and research scientists in medical imaging and ML, not just from the AIMI Center but also through partnerships with UC San Diego.
takeaways: The internship opened my eyes to the possibilities of machine learning in a field that directly impacts humans where faster and more accurate scans can lead to earlier diagnoses and improved outcomes.

location: Magnuson Health Sciences Center in Seattle
description: As a student researcher in the Vásquez Lab, I've investigated how developing organs take shape by combining experimental and computational biology. Our lab uses the Drosophila melanogaster renal system as a model to understand how molecular signals and mechanical forces guide organogenesis. During my time here, I've taken on both independent and collaborative projects: designing and conducting experiments, analyzing confocal imaging data, contributing to shared lab resources/projects, and working on a computational analysis tool. This experience has helped me navigate the culture of academic science while contributing meaningfully to hypothesis-driven research.
unique_experiences: • Gaining hands-on experience in immunohistochemistry, confocal microscopy, and image analysis of Drosophila embryos • Leading a multi-year investigation into the role of cell-cell junctions in sculpting the final organ form • Participating in weekly lab meetings and attending dissertation defenses • Collaborating with a PhD student to train a deep learning model for unsupervised cell segmentation of tubular tissues in confocal images
surprised: I was surprised by how open scientific spaces can be. I had expected research to feel hierarchical, but instead found myself in an environment where undergrads, graduate students, and principal investigators regularly bounced ideas off each other during lab meetings. Questions were both encouraged and expected.
takeaways: This internship taught me that failure is part of the process. I learned that research is inherently iterative: most experiments don't lead to an immediate answer or conclusion, but each one taught me something new. Embracing uncertainty and learning how to collaborate with my mentor and colleagues helped me grow not just as a scientist but as a problem-solver.

description: The Lampe laboratory performs studies to find blood tests or protein biomarkers that can indicate if and where a person has cancer and if it is present, how to best treat it. Specifically, they are working to find early detection, recurrence or response biomarkers of colon, breast, and lung cancer. Once the biomarker is defined, they make drugs based on that biomarker so the tumors can be directly attacked.
unique_experiences: Perform antibody cloning, transfections, minipreps - Conduct tissue culture protocols - Participate in lab meetings - Process and analyze data from ELISAs - Run and scan western blots
surprised: I was surprised by how much creativity and problem-solving were involved. I expected the work to be mostly technical, but performing the experiments required thinking outside the box. I was also surprised by how collaborative the lab environment was. Everyone was constantly sharing ideas and learning from each other. Even as an intern, I felt like my contributions mattered.
takeaways: My main takeaway is that research requires a lot of patience. There is a lot of waiting involved in the protocols and sometimes you need to repeat experiments if something doesn't work, which is often the case. It is basically just trial and error, seeing what works and what doesn't, and then continuing off of that.

description: Fred Hutch is one of the leading Cancer Research Centers in the US, with a variety of research and clinical focuses. I worked as a Research Tech, performing lab duties, bench work, and data analysis. Along with fellow lab members, I researched Colon and Esophageal Cancer, designing early detection biomarkers and investigating the Senescent Microenvironment of tumors. I was given immense freedom to pursue my own research interests and directions.
unique_experiences: • Performing cutting edge research • Connecting with renowned scientists • Presenting data at meetings and conferences • Using liquid nitrogen and dry ice
surprised: I was very surprised by how personable and exciting my fellow lab members were. I always thought scientists would be scary and socially awkward, falling into the mad scientist stereotype. Interestingly, everyone in the lab is extremely friendly and not crazy whatsoever.
takeaways: It's ok to make mistakes, as long as you keep track of what mistakes you made. Not accounting for the mistakes is the biggest mistake you can make.

description: The Quantum Devices Lab was involved with small devices which can be used to provide information about this hidden world which we cannot see. During my time at the lab, I worked on research regarding devices which can be inserted into the human body, called nano biodevices, and gaining an understanding for the research process. Professor Anantram was very accommodating and willing to accept interns, and help them explore their interests in engineering through a new lens.
unique_experiences: • Getting to write my own research paper • Meeting with Professor Anantram at UW • Creating presentations for Professor Anantram and his team to see
surprised: I was most surprised how involved the process for properly writing a full research paper was. My work for the better part of last year was almost exclusively compiling past research to build my understanding. The amount of training and dedication behind a singular research paper was challenging yet rewarding once I was able to become proficient.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is paying attention to details. Research papers are quite formal, and technical documents which require the utmost accuracy to not create any confusion. I would have to write and rewrite my research paper, with support from Professor Anantram, many times to properly convey ideas. It was a long learning process, but I have an appreciation for details now.

description: The Greenberg Lab is an immunology lab focused on the research of various cell markers of primarily CD4 and CD8 T-cells in combatting cancer cells. Various processes including flow cytometry, and DNA isolation and extraction are used to target and identify the cells of interest, allowing for cell modification and design to be tested on mice with cancerous tumors.
unique_experiences: Cell passaging - Learning flow cytometry - Cell counting - Lab chores - Gel electrophoresis - Making agar plates - Bacterial transformation - Overnight cell cultures - Mice spleen processing
surprised: What surprised me the most was the amount of time a round of experiments takes in the lab. The process of designing and identifying the markers in the t-cells to test and the length it takes to culture, isolation and grow the target cells along with the trial period where the t-cells are placed inside the mice with a tumor can take several months.
takeaways: Collaboration is necessary to progress in science. My lab undergoes weekly lab meetings to discuss over project progress, or to help others with their project presentations and there is always someone in the lab working together with someone else to accomplish a task. Science is slow. Over 50% of my time spent at the lab was waiting for things like my bacteria or cells to grow or for something to spin down in the centrifuge. However, it wasn't boring as there's almost always things to be cleaned or experiments being conducted in the lab that can be observed.

description: This internship primarily involves setting up and implementing the EPA's environmental education initiative. The EPA calls it the "EcoLearn Program" . Throughout the internship we communicated with Bellevue School District heads and school principals, designed our own environmental lessons, and created detailed plans on how to expand climate-related education across Bellevue School District. Aside from that, we also had to each write a research paper on a climate-change-related topic of our choosing, with the goal of our papers being shared to government officials in DC to advise policymaking.
unique_experiences: During our internship, we got to talk with lots of EPA employees who were able to share their journey in government work. We met our mentor's boss who used to work at NOAA, an EPA researcher that specialized in toxic waste, and an EPA employee who helped write regulations for environmental waste in Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and Alaska.
surprised: During our internship, the Trump administration gutted lots of funding for the EPA and heavily restricted what we could or couldn't do as part of our internship. Not only that, but locally, BSD suffered lots of major budget cuts, meaning we weren't ever able to implement our environmental education initiatives locally. It was shocking to see these issues I only ever saw on the news materialize and affect the work that I did personally.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway from this internship was about communicating with others professionally. I got a lot better at making proposals/presentations and writing professional emails to school principals and BSD executives. For example, I had to make a proposal for expanding field trips in middle school education, and while making the presentation I got a lot better at communicating the information while still making it exciting and enticing for teachers/school principals. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: Our main tasks were to expand the EcoLearn program to several elementary and middle schools across the district, write a research paper on any environmental related topic of our choosing, and create proposals for climate change education for high schoolers.
unique_experiences: Our main tasks were to expand upon the previously existing environmental education program (EcoLearn) to more schools across the district. To do this, we partnered with the district superintendent and joined the Bellevue School District Sustainability Team. We also learned a lot about graphic design and social media as we ran the Instagram account and designed the website and flyers.
surprised: Our team all chose very different topics and research paper styles for our research papers (I chose to study how bat populations are affected by deforestation in Southeast Asia, and my peers chose point source pollution and sustainable energy). This, along with the opportunity to talk to a wide variety of professionals throughout the EPA about their work and college experiences and the different tasks they do, helped me realize how vast the field of environment studies is, and how many different topics it spans.
takeaways: I learned better communication skills (especially when engaging with audiences of all ages and backgrounds), improved my scientific writing skills, and developed greater confidence in presenting topics clearly and effectively. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: The Environmental Protection Agency works to protect public health and the environment. During my time as an intern, half of my experience was to write and present a research paper on my own topic of choosing, and the other half of the experience was spent creating education proposals for the Bellevue School District via the EcoLearn program. Our mentor Viccy was extremely helpful in guiding us through this whole experience.
unique_experiences: Meeting and questioning current EPA employees that had worked for NOAA, the White House, and other national organizations - Research paper on own topic of choosing - Creating education proposals for Bellevue School District
surprised: I was surprised by how many different backgrounds people working for the EPA came from, and how many different tasks they did. Some work in pure science research, others focus on the ethics and law-writing, etc.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience is learning time management skills along with working with a group on multiple different projects. Being assigned to write a research paper over the course of a year brought a lot of flexibility for me as the author to pick and choose when I wanted to work on what steps, and I learned a lot of time management skills from this. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

organization: Seattle University/Dept. of Civil and Environmental Engineering
description: I worked under Dr. Thompson at SeattleU's Environmental Engineering Department. I joined a team of undergraduate engineers who were dedicating to advancing sustainability in environmental health. As an intern this year, I helped run the experiments researching the decay of chlorine under sunlight as well as helped the undergraduates prepare solutions, brainstorm ideas, and organize the data.
unique_experiences: Learned how to use new equipment such as Ion Chromatography, HPLC (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography) and solar simulators - Researched chlorine as an alternative to current "PNA Decay" method of wastewater management, determined chlorine concentrations in water with DPD (color indicator) - Compiled and wrote a research paper on use of compost tea in hydroponics
surprised: I was most surprised at how flexible my schedule was with this internship, and how helpful Dr. T and the undergraduates were if I was confused or wanted to learn more. Everyone was nice but also helped teach me the important of working efficiently and together.
takeaways: Do not be afraid to ask questions, even if they don't seem significant. I also learned the importance of collaboration and attention to detail, as college research requires much higher expectations.

organization: Seattle University, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering
description: I worked in a chem lab under Dr. T with a team of undergraduates working to advance sustainability and awareness of environmental health. I helped run experiments researching the decay of chlorine under sunlight and as an alternative in methods of wastewater treatment.
unique_experiences: • Learning how to operate lab equipment and facilities, such as HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography), IC (Ion Chromatography), solar simulators, and spectrophotometers • Worked on a project researching chlorine as an alternative to current "PNA decay" methods of wastewater management, used DPD (color indicator) and spectrophotometers to determine chlorine concentrations in water • Practicing lab protocol and use of PPE at all times in the lab
surprised: How repetitive and monotonous work in the lab can get, as you oftentimes are repeating and running the same experiment over and over again. However, I found the lab team very helpful and supportive when it came to things like understanding how to operate equipment.
takeaways: I learned the importance of preciseness and detail when working in a professional lab. However once you pick up the habit and routine, it becomes more natural and you can enjoy the experience of working in college-level research.

category: Nuclear Physics, Computer Science, Applied Mathematics, Signal Processing
description: The Center for Experimental Nuclear Physics and Astrophysics (CENPA) is a DOE-funded Center of Excellence laboratory (Grant #DE-FG02-97ER41020) and Service Center located at the University of Washington Seattle campus. CENPA is located at the North Physics Lab: a 53,500-sq. ft. stand-alone facility that supports up to 100 faculty, staff, students, and visitors. CENPA supports a broad program of experimental physics research in fundamental symmetries, neutrinos, muons, axions, and non-Newtonian gravitation. CENPA is home to the Axion Dark Matter eXperiment (ADMX), the Eöt-Wash suite of precision "tabletop" experiments, the unique Project-8 measurement of the neutrino mass, and a precision measurement of the He-6 beta decay spectrum using our 9-MV Tandem Van de Graaff Accelerator. The latter two experiments are exploiting a unique and new technique called cyclotron resonance emission spectroscopy (CRES).
unique_experiences: • Development of Axolotl GUI used for He-6 energy analysis • Modeling of signals and other phenomena. • Problem solving for concepts related to He-6. • Short presentation of work at CENPA conference.
surprised: Less surprising but more enlightening was the level of collaboration and discipline that both professors, doctoral researchers, and grad/undergraduate students have when working on subprojects. Everybody has a role in some form, playing off their strengths and contributing to something.
takeaways: Anything can be useful in the discipline of research. When it comes to physics, almost any form of background knowledge somehow became integral to the functionality of CRES-9 where things I learned prior such as the essentials of Monty Carlo became one of the focuses of my work. That is to say, I learned to appreciate the STEM I learned and see how it can actually be used firsthand so cohesively.

organization: University of Washington Department of Atmospheric Sciences and Geophysics
description: This internship is centered around doing research on your own on a chosen topic of atmospheric physics. I was introduced to the basics and given the fundamental tools needed to run a forecast. Then, it was up to me conduct the tests. I researched the manipulation of artificial intelligence and hybrid model forecasts by changing random perturbations in the initial condition. I compared the error propagation of the different models, and how their results changed over time.
unique_experiences: Getting to use machine learning and hybrid forecast models - Analyzing atmospheric data from weather anomalies - Working with cutting edge backpropagation systems run on supercomputers
surprised: I was surprised by how much I learned by problem-solving on my own. While troubleshooting to get the forecast models to work, I learned about artificial intelligence coding, different python tools, and data display systems.
takeaways: My greatest takeaways is that very few things you try will work on the first try, and the simplest solution might end up being the best solution. None of my forecasts gave the desired results on my first run-through, and often enough the most basic fix would end up resolving the most frustrating errors.

description: The EPE ML Group, led by Professor Shih-Chieh Hsu in the University of Washington Physics Department, looks at the application of machine learning and artificial intelligence in the context of advanced physics. For the first half of the year, interns complete a sequence of labs to build a foundation in Python and programming for machine learning, before taking on a research project with a partner to apply learning. Meetings take place once a week with the mentor in-person at UW and virtually, as well as additional meetings with partners/groups if needed.
unique_experiences: I was involved with the classification of finger flexion decoding from electrocorticographic signals from the brain.
surprised: What surprised me most was the wide application of machine learning within physics, ranging from medical applications such as EEG and ECoG signals in neuroscience, to more particle physics applications such as Higgs search and b-tagging. I was able to explore the foundations of machine learning and then understand how to apply it to several different fields.
takeaways: Never show up underprepared – always make sure you know the material so fluently that you would be able to explain it without even looking at your code. If you have any questions, never hesitate to ask your group members or the mentor, because it will save you so much confusion and stress. Communicate with your mentor!

organization: Institute for Nuclear Theory, UW Department of Physics
description: At my internship, I worked directly with Professor Cirigliano exploring machine learning in physics. The first part of the year was dedicated to gaining a solid understanding of quantum mechanics and its underlying math through discussions to get up to speed with Professor Cirigliano's work. After that, we pivoted into learning a lecture series on machine learning together as it aligned with both of our interests. As an intern, I learned about mathematical concepts in machine learning and quantum physics and created basic neural networks and quantum physics simulations in python.
unique_experiences: Coding wave function simulations in Python - One-on-one discussions on various physics topics with Professors
surprised: I was surprised by how all of my prior math experiences came together with both physics and computer science applications. Drawing from that past experience was key to my success in keeping up.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was the value of asking questions, especially in an unfamiliar environment. It's easy to fall behind and asking questions isn't something to be afraid of, it can help everyone gain a better understanding.

organization: UW Bothell Gravitational Wave Astronomy Research Group
description: In this research group, UWB faculty and students work across the spectrum of gravitational wave astronomy including work in data analysis, astrophysics, and parameter estimation. Ongoing research projects include work with the Pulsar Search Collaboratory (PSC), Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA), and the LIGO Hanford Observatory. I worked with the PSC, where I was able to access radio data from the Green Bank telescope after a 6-week training period and analyze the data for new pulsars. I was also involved a research project with a PhD student, which focused on analyzing the periodic Giant Pulses observed from the Crab Pulsar.
unique_experiences: Conducting real radio astronomy by analyzing radio data - Learning about PhD and faculty research project through weekly meetings - Gain exposure to research networks
surprised: It surprised me how accessible research projects are, and how many PhD students and faculty are willing to allow interested students to assist in their projects.
takeaways: Make sure to communicate! Be willing to send emails to PhD students and Professors if you're interested in their work – they may actually respond! - Ask questions – researchers are often very willing and excited to talk about their research

description: At the University of Washington's Electrical and Computer Engineering department, I researched metal combinations to enhance DNA conductivity in microchip applications. I reviewed scientific literature, transcribed analytical reports, and aided in creating diagrams to be used in scientific literature.
unique_experiences: One of the most unique aspects of this internship was contributing to foundational work in nanoscale electronics with real-world implications. I had the opportunity to present my findings to senior researchers and propose strategies for experimental replication. This experience strengthened both my scientific communication and independent problem-solving skills
surprised: I was most surprised by the varying experience levels of all the people I worked with. I was working with graduate students and other professors at the university during my internship and that was a really unique experience and helped me learn more about networking and formal communication.
