takeaways: Some of my greatest takeaways from my experience working at the university were the skills I gained. This internship taught me how to approach research with both curiosity and structure. I learned how to analyze scientific literature critically, design systematic methodologies, and communicate complex findings clearly. Not all parts of research is groundbreaking, a lot of the time it is a lot of work and data collection but its really cool to be a part of a team working on building something new.

location: 1 weekly meeting in person at UW, rest remote
description: This group introduces and teaches Machine learning concepts through lab instruction in the PHYS417 curriculum and then the second part of the year is focused on application of that learning. Usually, a group of mostly Interlake high school students will meet weekly with Professor Hsu to go over progress in the lab assignments as they develop a comprehensive understanding of coding in Python and Machine learning. Then, after the lab assignments have been completed, interns are given the option to select from an array of projects (among the choices are elementary particle physics experiments) and interact with UW Postgrad and undergraduate mentors as they complete them.
unique_experiences: • Familiarizing yourself with AI/ML concepts through case studies (stock market prediction, handwriting detection, etc.) • Unique collaborative environment with Interlake students you've known for years as well as people you've never talked to before • Get to experience a research symposium at the end of the year and make your own poster to present
surprised: The most surprising realization was that this is really a choose your own adventure internship. If you're more interested in computer science, do the labs in depth and pick a project to test out those skills. If Physics aligns more with your future plans, then learn the basics and start doing projects with the UW Physics department.
takeaways: Make sure you have a good team and get your communication and collaboration set up early because it's a lot worse to go at it alone.

description: Prof. Anantram's lab focuses on developing nano materials and devices. There are different projects that students can choose to work on. My project was to learn about the field of DNA sequencing via graphene nanopores by reading recent papers and novel developments in the subject, then summarizing the information into a review paper. This involves searching for obscure and novel papers and doing research to understand them. I met with Prof. Anantram every week to discuss my work on the review, and occasionally with other members of the lab.
unique_experiences: Discovering how novel nanopore shapes, materials, and designs contribute to increased efficiency and accuracy - Writing an extensive paper modelled after real review articles - Speaking with researchers who are at the forefront of development in their fields
surprised: I was surprised by how friendly and welcoming Prof. Anantram and the others in the lab were when I met them. Even though they obviously know a lot more about what I was saying than myself, they were still willing to listen to my ideas and what I was working on.
takeaways: It's important to always be listening for feedback and how you can change your work to improve it and make it better. People ask a lot of questions so everyone's understanding of concepts can be clarified.

description: He6-CRES is a UW experimental nuclear physics research group working on using Cyclotron Radiation Emission Spectroscopy (CRES) techniques to precisely measure beta spectra of particles. Although not a pure physics internship, instead focusing more on statistics/data science, high-level physics knowledge may be useful and required for certain projects. There are a variety of flexible project options to explore, ranging from statistics modeling to systems testing.
unique_experiences: • Applied statistical models and signal processing methods to messy real-world experimental data. • Learned how data science is used in research. • Presented findings to the research group.
surprised: How supportive the research group was. My mentor Nick was really approachable and would go to lengths to help me understand all the concepts—finding papers and resources to read, creating problem sets, and going through them step-by-step.
takeaways: I learned a lot about how to manage other people's codebases, whether it be through responsibly using Github, testing my code on multiple machines, and writing down documentation for others.

organization: University of Washington Network Security Lab
description: The UW Network Security Laboratory is committed to advancing research in cybersecurity and cyber-physical systems (CPS). As an intern, I worked on various projects relating to identifying and mitigating vulnerabilities in connected devices and infrastructures.
unique_experiences: • Weekly meetings with postdoc mentor and other lab members. • Learned to design and implement different attack scenarios and defense algorithms in AirSim and Crazyflie 2.1 drone (Python/C++) • Created a robust flight controller using an LQR, sensor-fusion, and Kalman Filter to process noisy readings. • Used MATLAB to analyze and process system data • Chill schedule and environment. Freedom in pacing, workload, and etc.
surprised: Be prepared to defend your ideas to their logical conclusion—"I don't know" or "maybe" won't cut it. When we transitioned to a physical drone, every decision, every setup had real-world consequences, so our choices needed to be deliberate, justified, and defensible.
takeaways: Though I entered the internship with little prior exposure to the lab's research direction, I was still able to adapt my existing skills and learn. My biggest takeaway is to stay open-minded and explore as many fields as possible because you never know what you want to do for the rest of your life.

description: The Personal Robotics Lab focuses on developing robot perception, manipulation, learning, and human-robot interaction to allow robots to navigate complex tasks. A lot of my time at the UW Personal Robotics lab was spent learning tools to develop code for controlling robots like ROS (Robot Operating System), reinforcement learning algorithms, and sensor data filtering algorithms. After doing independent reading on those topics, my mentor had problems for me to solve that all related to the UW MuSHR project (small robotic cars that are distributed to undergrad students for a computer science class) or off-road driving simulation.
unique_experiences: • My mentor trusted me to learn and implement a reinforcement learning algorithm with minimal guidance. • Talking to my mentor at the end of each day to debrief what I learned was a unique opportunity to get 1-on-1 time with with an expert in the field I want to pursue. • Working with robotic cars that cost hundreds of dollars each.'
surprised: I was initially worried that I couldn't work/learn fast enough to meet deadlines at an official research lab. But I was pleasantly surprised that I did have the time and resources to build a deep understanding of the topics I worked on.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway apart from just the content I studied is self-confidence in myself to learn new topics. My mentor helped a lot with understanding the content and before doing this internship, I don't think I would've dared to touch the reinforcement learning or filtering algorithms that I now understand.

description: This internship teaches machine learning concepts through a PHYS417 course, then real applications in the latter half of the internship, working with teammates and postgrads with their projects. How it works is there will be a group of students all going through each lab of the course, meeting with Professor Hsu once a week to check up on your understanding of the machine learning concepts in that week's lab. All lab progress will be done asynchronously, so it will take a lot of time management. After all labs are done and Professor Hsu has checked you off, you will form teams with your internship group and choose through a selection of projects using these applications with postgraduate and undergraduate mentors.
unique_experiences: • Learning AI/ML concepts through different scenarios (handwriting recognition, land terrain splitting, etc.) • Forming a close bond with other internship members through collaboration • Working through projects with postgraduate and undergraduate mentors • Experiencing a poster symposium to present at an end-of-year research gathering
surprised: What surprised me the most was how approachable this internship is. I came into this internship with little coding experience, but through consistent effort, a lot of the concepts made sense the more I grinded through it, and it become very intuitive. It was very student-led, and Professor Hsu only guides and checks your knowledge, so be sure that you understand everything that is happening.
takeaways: Collaborate with your team and understand the lab. The internship work becomes almost unbearable if you aren't working with anyone, especially if you don't have an amazing grasp on machine learning already. It's also super important to understand each line of your code to make sure you learn from the lab.

organization: UW Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning Group
description: The internship begins with the PHY417 graduate course training, during which we work on labs and sync up weekly with Prof. Hsu. After completing the labs, we choose a project with a PhD or graduate student mentor and work to create a model for a real-life dataset, such as high energy physics, ECoG signals, or particle collisions. We present our model at the end of the internship during a poster symposium. We have weekly in-person meetings at UW, while the rest of the internship is conducted remotely.
unique_experiences: • Presenting at the poster symposium was exciting as I got to share my work and learn from others in ML. • Personal mentorship by a leading professor in the field • Applying machine learning to complex, real-world scientific datasets.
surprised: How much research happens through conversation. Some of the most important parts of this experience were collaborative, such as asking questions in meetings, bouncing ideas off my mentor and group, or hearing how someone else approached their project.
takeaways: Scientific communication skills. Whether in weekly meetings discussing our lab progress or presenting at the symposium, I learned how important it is to clearly articulate not just what I did, but why it matters.

organization: University of Washington Elementary Particle Experiment
location: University of Washington Physics and Astronomy Building
description: At the start of the internship, you gain skills working with deep learning techniques through completing lab assignments, building basic neural networks, CNNs, or RNNs. Much of the learning is independently done, through watching lab or lecture videos and self-researching. You'll also meet weekly with your group and mentor to collaborate, report progress, and get help. After completing the coursework, you will pair up with researchers, to work on a project applying deep learning to research on a high-energy physics problem.
unique_experiences: Opportunity to apply deep learning to graduate students' research at UW. - Presenting in the UW research symposium at the end of year. - Opportunity to be mentored by professors experienced in their fields (physics and deep learning) and receive constructive criticism. - Was able to apply deep learning for a variety of tasks, such as handwriting recognition, language translation, or iris classification.
surprised: The level of individual learning, collaboration, and problem-solving skills needed to succeed in this internship. Questions you have won't have a simple answer, and you need to experiment and independent research to figure out how to fix a problem or find out what works best. It is also important to collaborate with others to help each other find a solution together.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship is the importance of theoretical understanding and asking good questions. Having strong foundations and a solid grasp of what you are working on help ensure you are learning the right things and sets you up to be able to apply your skills in other areas. To make sure you do, it's important to ask yourself lots of questions, and be able to explain your work clearly.

description: Under my mentor, Dr. Eric Deutsch, I have been contributing to the Human Proteome Project computationally: evaluating 'likely pseudogenes' for protein-coding evidence, using Python to create figures summarizing the nearly 20,000 target human proteins, and mass spectra data analysis.
unique_experiences: Working with one other intern on different parts of the same projects - Completing a paper on the first project described above as a co- first author, which has been accepted to a research journal - Learning Python and machine learning, becoming familiar with various protein databases, currently learning how mass spectrometry is used in proteomics
surprised: The things that surprised me most were how interdisciplinary biology research is and how much is done outside the lab, and that research is a lot more flexible and choose-your-own-adventure than I anticipated. My mentor was also incredibly nice and always willing to help, so I was able to develop skills quickly, especially as I was new to programming.
takeaways: I got to learn about the role of computer science and data analysis in biology research firsthand. More generally, stay inquisitive and always ask questions – your internship is one of the best places to learn and hone skills for your future career.

description: Completed miscellaneous tasks for Prof. Anantram's group. (yes it really was this generic)
unique_experiences: Gained experience with web design - Learn a truckload of math and quantum physics
surprised: I was surprised how much time the group spent studying each of their sources. It is truly astonishing how many details one could be able to gleam from looking at a few lines of text and a few pixels of graph for multiple hours.
takeaways: This was my first experience working in a research lab. Well, not really as I wasn't in the lab very often. Either way I still learnt a lot about how the lab's recruitment and team dynamic worked as I worked on a lot of my soft skills. Otherwise I learned that doing "lecture transcription" is incredibly tedious but does help with building knowledge, if that's what you want to get out of an internship. (not true for me)

description: Worked with my mentor to learn and build neuromorphic circuits and research its functions.
unique_experiences: Build circuits - Attend lectures by grad students and professors - Present about my circuits
surprised: I was surprised how interesting the topic was, because I was skeptical going into it and I enjoyed it much more than I expected
takeaways: I like having a lot of structure to my work, so having a lot of freedom to do work whenever I wanted was something I had to learn to get used to.

organization: University of Washington (Electrical and Computer Engineering department)
description: Interns choose a topic of interest and explore it using real world data. I decided to analyze trends within aviation (passenger counts at SeaTac Airport). - Time series data analytics with Python (Pandas, Statsmodels, Matplotlib). Forecasting with SARIMA on cyclical data with underlying trends. - Processed and visualized lots of data, structured and segmented data to identify trends
unique_experiences: Using hypothesis testing from IB math in my code for certain functions - Going through libraries like pmdarima and diving into how cool functions like auto_arima work
surprised: Data analytics is not as simple as feeding a model data and getting back answers, there are many steps such as data cleaning, preprocessing, differencing, model tuning, etc.
takeaways: Drawing insights from data is rarely straightforward, there are often many confounding variables that must be accounted for when you want to draw accurate insights. This often decreases the accuracy or scope of predictions you can make with your model, hence why it is important to choose the right model for your data.

location: University of Washington Physics and Astronomy Building
description: The UW EPE internship is based around machine learning and its application in physics and other disciplines. Under Dr. Hsu, we start with learning about machine learning's fundamentals from the PHYS 417 class, completing 9 labs each using a different neural network type. After the class work is completed, interns start a project with postgrad mentors about topics like particle physics, applying ML to the project.
unique_experiences: • Touring CENPA and seeing the accelerator and other UW physics events • Learning about neural networks in python through a UW class format • Presenting a poster about our work at a symposium at the end of the year
surprised: The variability in paths to get to the same "answer" . With no defined right or wrong path, it's up to us to decide what's the best to get the rightest answer.
takeaways: It's paramount to set up a strong network of communication to make sure everyone is on the same page of understanding because otherwise people might fall behind and suffer in silence.

organization: UW Department of Electrical & Computer Engineering
description: I would support the preparation of his course materials by reading over his slides and lecture notes, making changes to ensure accuracy. This often also included conducting background research to improve clarity. My tasks also included listening in on his semiconductor lectures and transcribing recorded lecture.
unique_experiences: • Learning about complex physics topics • Working with graduate-level research • Having the freedom to learn independently
surprised: The amount of effort that goes into organizing and communicating complex scientific ideas in a clear, accessible way.
takeaways: Maintaining consistent communication with your mentor is important. Professors are experts in their field and are happy to explain anything that you don't understand. They are trying to help you learn so it's important to reach out if you have any questions.

description: CENPA (Center for Experimental Nuclear Physics and Astrophysics) is a research facility at the University of Washington. A wide variety of experiments and projects take place, including dark matter searches, as well as precision measurements of the neutrino mass and beta decay spectrums (He-6). As part of the He-6 collaboration, projects included: optimizing signal processing algorithms, monte-carlo simulations, and improving a graphical user interface for data collection.
unique_experiences: Used algorithms (kNN, vector search, HNSW) to create high- performance algorithms for signal processing - Created monte-carlo simulations to test algorithms - Created several presentations to professors, post-docs, and grad students at group meetings - Photography
surprised: What surprised me the most was how much computer science is depended on for physics research. While the experiment does have a significant theoretical and engineering portion, a large portion of the data collection and processing pipeline is heavily dependent on software.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was how multifaceted scientific research can be. This experiment required scientists with a wide array of skills, from physics to electrical engineering to computer science.

organization: University of Washington Department of Global Health
description: The UW Department of Global Health is a research institution that aims to address global health disparities through many disciplines, collaborating with researchers from across the world. During my time at the internship, I contributed to academic research on a diverse range of topics, from racial disparities in sodium consumption to AI applications in health research.
unique_experiences: • Participate in research projects with potential authorship • Practice literature review, data analysis, manuscript writing, and more • Learn about AI and help develop an AI tool with public health applications
surprised: I was most surprised that research at the Department of Global Health spans fields beyond biology/medicine. I was able to focus on my fields of interest, public policy and economics, while exploring other fields in the world of global health.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is that responsibility is very important in academia. Because our research influences real-world policies and outcomes, there is lots of effort put into ensuring that we use rigorous methodologies and present accurate information.

description: The BSD Central Office is the headquarters of the district's operations and houses the superintendent's executive team. I interned under the BSD Chief of Staff, who leads the development of district policies, legislative advocacy, and community partnerships. As an intern, I shadowed my mentor in executive meetings, did rounds with the leaders of BSD departments to learn about their work, and worked on compiling research sources to assist my mentor in his work. I met so many wonderful people and truly enjoyed this internship!
unique_experiences: Shadowing meetings with district leaders, trainings for school administrators, community events, and more - Creating summaries and analysis of federal and state legislation regarding K-12 education - One-on-one learning opportunities with department leaders and behind-the-scenes experiences with the district's inner operations
surprised: I was most surprised by the scope of the organization. As a student, it's not really apparent, but there are dozens of departments/project teams within the Bellevue School District. (Some examples: Finance, Counselling, Teaching and Learning, MLL, Nutrition, Athletics and Activities.) Through my internship, I got an inside look into how each of them functions and interconnects.
takeaways: Teachers are some of the nicest people you'll ever meet! Most people who work at the Central Office were former teachers who became administrators and then took on district roles. They still love teaching, so they're always willing to help me out with anything I need or teach me something new.

description: For my internship this year I worked with Mr. Hutcherson as his teacher's assistant. I've done work primarily for his AP Human Geography classes but also his Theory of Knowledge classes. Most of my work has been preparing and grading tests and assignments. I've also created from study materials for the class as well.
unique_experiences: Creating study materials, grading student work and tests, preparing classwork, organizing classroom materials
surprised: I was surprised by the number of accommodations and exceptions made for students when it came to assignments and tests. I didn't realize how much work was put into creating late tests, retakes, Spanish versions of assignments, and alternative versions of assessments.
takeaways: Students learn at very varied speeds and complete work at different rates - Being able to quickly read different kinds of handwriting is often very difficult - Learning to improvise well is a very useful ability to have in teaching

description: Mr. Allen teaches both IB Biology HL and Freshman Biology here at Interlake. The Teaching Assistant role is more engaged with the IB Biology HL classes but can be involved with Freshman Biology. It is very flexible and primarily involved lab maintenance, conducting self-driven investigations, and helping students.
unique_experiences: Conduct a detailed and fully funded investigation of greater complexity than an Individual Investigation over a longer time frame - Help grade tests, provide feedback, and create instructional material - Participate in the class labs and help them with IB test preparation
surprised: The work required to maintain the lab, especially during the time when many Individual Investigations are being run.
takeaways: Improving my time management: for both the investigation and lab maintenance, we had to manage our time wisely to ensure timely progress on the investigation while ensuring the lab was sufficiently stocked for the other investigations to run successfully.

description: I TA for two biology teachers here at Interlake. This involves tasks helping prep lessons and working with students. Some of my most common tasks are copies, preparing labs, workshopping activities/handouts and preparing new curriculum.
unique_experiences: Early in the year I got to make sucrose solutions of various molarities, which was super fun because of the cool equipment. There was a magnetic stirring rod which spun rapidly inside of the beaker where the sugar was being dissolved into solution.
surprised: I was surprised how much input I had on lessons. The teachers I worked with asked me all the time to determine if a website would be useful, if a certain part of an activity should be cut or added, if new curriculum was well-written, etc.
takeaways: Teaching is really hard! The amount of planning that has go into a lesson outside of lesson time is a lot, and there is significant trial and error behind the scenes just trying to get everything together. That it does come together is amazing!

description: As a TA for Ms. Ramos, I graded tests and assignments, prepared classroom materials, and created reading quizzes. I also helped with logistical tasks like printing and copying instructional handouts, which gave me a hands-on role in the day-to-day operations of a classroom.
unique_experiences: Learning to use the school's high-tech printer and copier - Creating student assessments & reading quizzes
surprised: Understanding the behind-the-scenes of teaching, and how things like instruction & grading is organized.
takeaways: I learned the importance and difficulty of organization, consistency, and communication in teaching. I also developed practical skills, like using professional equipment, grading quickly, and writing questions that test for comprehension.

description: I assist Mrs. Costarella in any matters with which she might need help.
unique_experiences: Learning how to grade and experimenting with making rubrics. - Being able to interact with and helping students individually when they need it. - Creating material for students to study and work on
surprised: I initially underestimated how much time, and work went into working individually with students. In general, the time that goes into every aspect of teaching was much greater than expected, and rubrics are more important than I thought when it comes to grading fairly and efficiently.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway from this experience was the importance of efficiency. When it comes to grading there's a lot to get through and so I had to have a system to accurately and consistently score and provide feedback within reasonable timeframes.

description: In this student-led internship, you will maintain the BSD's legacy version of LegSim, a United States legislative procedure simulation used by our school for AP Gov classes. You will also continue the creation of a new LegSim application with SvelteKit. During my time with LegSim, our team worked with and learned about Ruby on Rails for old LegSim as well. You will also have the responsibility of finding a permanent hosting and funding solution for LegSim in the future.
unique_experiences: • Meeting BSD LegSim users (teachers, students) for feedback and feature fixes • Learning and developing with frameworks including Rails and Sveltekit • Creating hosting and funding solutions within the BSD administration
surprised: I was most surprised in seeing the contrast between our new SvelteKit framework and the old Ruby on Rails framework! It really helped me understand the concept of technical debt and the changes in web design philosophies over the past twenty years- and yes, the old LegSim is about that old.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship is probably the practical knowledge and experience I've gained from working on these websites, that I can apply to my own personal websites and web applications. Going in depth to understand how LegSim works while trying to fix it, I will say learned a lot about web development practices- both the good and the bad.

description: Mostly just doing daily tasks, organizing, and helping the teachers. Tasks generally are just cleaning the classroom and loading the kiln for Mrs. Cantrell and setting up stained glass tools for Mrs. Friedle. Other than that, you might need to sort the glass and glaze inventory, photograph and display artworks, and grade student notebooks from time to time. The amount of work varies day to day, but there will usually be some free time and feel free to take some breaks.
unique_experiences: • Working with the teachers to setup the classroom • Seeing all the cool artworks the students create • Creating small exhibitions for student art
surprised: I was surprised by all the cleaning that I had to do. Most Thursdays were pretty much entirely dedicated to cleaning clay buckets and sponges. There was also a lot of time spent on refilling glazes and the melon red color smelt like someone died.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship is taking care of the classroom. Cleaning and organizing is important to keep everything functioning, and it just felt rewarding to see everything organized at the end of Thursdays.

description: As a Teaching Assistant for Mr. Allen's freshmen and HL Biology classes, I helped run labs, sterilize equipment, grade assessments, and support students with their investigations. I also created instructional lectures and offered feedback on how to better scaffold student-context-based curriculum. Outside class, I worked 1-on-1 with students and conducted my own experiments on bioluminescence and allelopathy.
unique_experiences: Designing a "How to Brainstorm Topics with AI" workshop - Mentoring students through the IA writing process - Contributing curriculum ideas to improve tailored support - Sterilizing glassware and keeping the lab safe
surprised: I was surprised by how effective teaching hinges on translating complexity into clarity. It's not about dumbing things down but building bridges between expert understanding and where the average student is. Watching Mr. Allen do this reminded me that the best teaching feels natural but takes serious skill and intention.
takeaways: A lot of meaningful work happens in moments no one assigns you. By paying attention to trends in where students struggled and asking what could be better, I found ways to contribute without waiting for direction. If you pay close enough attention and keep asking questions, you'll always find chances to make things better.

description: Mrs. Cook's TA internship covers two of her classes: LangLit HL2, and Academic Seminar, although most of the TA-to-student communication happens during Academic Seminar. During Langlit, we usually carried out tasks such as printing copies, running errands to other teachers, and aiding Mrs. Cook in creating lesson plans or other projects. During Academic Seminar, we spend the entire time with the students and helping them complete homework or tests that were unfinished from their other classes. The goal of tutoring and mentoring the students in Academic seminar was to make sure they pass all their classes and fully understand the content in their classes.
unique_experiences: What makes this internship unique is the greater part of the internship being very hands-on with the Academic Seminar class. We can create connections with students in other grades and classes and help them every day, forming lasting friendships. Additionally, we are able to almost run the class by managing most of the kids, which differs from a lot of the other TA internships.
surprised: What surprised me the most is how much we impact the students when helping them with the work that they struggle on. I've made friends and got to know each of the kids in class that have all left strong impressions on me.
takeaways: I think taking time to learn how to properly help students in a way that can facilitate their learning is a very important quality and skill that you can have. The students you work with are similar to all of us and being understanding and patient can go a long way.

location: Hamlin Robinson School, 1701 20th Ave S, Seattle, WA 98144
description: I was an intern at the private school Hamlin Robinson. Hamlin Robinson School specifically helps students who have dyslexia and other language- based learning differences, giving them the support that would otherwise be hard to get in other schools or places. At my internship, I helped handle various tasks such as outreach, helping log attendance records, among others.
unique_experiences: Being able to see the different steps or methods taken to help students with learning differences.
surprised: What surprised me the most was that there were many little steps or solutions in order to help kids with learning differences. For example, having quiet time during lunch.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship experience is that the amount of work required to run a school or help out with various things is deceptively large, and just how important it is for kids with learning differences to be helped properly along with the impact it holds.

location: Office Room in World Language Building
description: TA for French 3, French 4/AP , and French 5/IB classes. Possibly only available to French 6 (reach out to Madame Collomb for further information). Help students with French, design worksheets/materials, photocopy
unique_experiences: Converse with students in French and improve yours and others' French - Design worksheets and materials. Photocopy them. - Grade tests and advise underclassmen
surprised: What surprised me the most was how much work there is behind the scenes in managing 5 periods, 3 language levels.
takeaways: Taught me better pedagogy—How to run a class, how to teach students, what types of materials to give to students, etc.

description: This internship covers her Lang/Lit HL2 and Academic Seminar classes. Most of the internship is focused on Academic Seminar, where we mentor and tutor the students in the class to ensure they pass and understand all the content in their classes. In her English class, we aid Ms. Cook in creating lesson plans, quiz questions, finishing tasks like printing copies, and other various tasks around the classroom.
unique_experiences: A unique experience is the Academic Seminar class itself. Every day there is something different and new to teach or deal with, and most days are quite interesting as a result. I think the variety of experiences gained from this class add to the quality of the internship.
surprised: I was most surprised by the actual impact we made on the students in Academic Seminar. I didn't expect to form friendships and close bonds with the students in the class, but I'm glad I did.
takeaways: The value of being flexible and patient. My ability to connect with all the students in this class was quite dependent on being able to understand and adapt to different students' needs, and I think that skill is vital in all of our future endeavors.

description: During my internship I learned thoroughly about SLG's online management system Clio. One of my tasks was to read and summarize new client intake responses for attorneys to read.
unique_experiences: • Reading different clients' experiences while summarizing intake responses for attorneys • Learning about the entire process of drafting/mailing/filing a complaint after the demand letter
surprised: It surprised me how long it takes to litigate and work through a case. Because the legal process is so complex I often encountered cases that began like 5 years ago and were still open.

organization: Sensors, Energy, and Automation Lab, University of Washington Dept. of Electrical and Computer Engineering
location: Paul G. Allen Center for Computer Science and Engineering (185 E Stevens Way NE, Seattle, WA 98195)
description: The Sensors, Energy and Automation Lab (SEAL) has 5 main groups. These include the Industrial Training and Assessment Center (focused on energy audits), Embedded (electrical/computer engineering focused), Plasma, Business (commercialization focused), and Sudoku (lab tools development).
unique_experiences: The most unique experience in the Industrial and Training Assessment Center is by far going on assessments.
surprised: What surprised me the most was the amount of flexibility and the was you are treated as equal with freshmen and sophomore undergraduate students in the assignments that you are given in the lab.

description: Gone.com is a Seattle-based recommerce and redistribution platform that provides free pickups to help individuals and businesses clear out excess or gently used goods. As a member of the engineering team, I primarily focused on building internal operations software. My tasks focused on making the giver (incoming logistics), warehouse, and redistribution pipelines more efficient.
unique_experiences: Even as part of the tech team, I helped other business processes to better understand how to build and design products for the people who'd use them.
surprised: 1. How much waste consumers produce. This is exactly what Gone is solving. 2. How central AI has become to how software and businesses generally actually get built. Almost all of our code is now written with AI assistance.
takeaways: AI is growing fast, and now is the best time to learn as much as you can about how to use it.

description: This radiation oncology lab designs treatment using alpha- radiotherapy, an innovative method reducing damage to healthy cells during cancer treatment. Specifically, we work with astatine, shipping it to labs across the United States, and delivering it to patients at UW Medicine.
unique_experiences: • Getting to see the cyclotron, which makes our product radioactive, was super cool! There are so many teams that work in tandem to make this safe and accessible for patients.
surprised: Treatment design is a very intricate process, especially for personalized medicine. When working on an astatine injection, we would take the patient's weight, severity and several other factors into account when deciding how to proceed.
takeaways: It is really important to be mindful about how research is an iterative process. My mentor was always striving to better the procedures behind the treatment production based on patient feedback.

description: OpenEXA is a Seattle-based fintech startup aiming to utilize AI, predictive modelling, and demand surge pricing to transform non-yielding assets into yielding assets while minimizing risks.
unique_experiences: • worked with APIs for data to create and improve predictive models, experimenting with different adjustments for best fit
surprised: I was surprised by how many different tools and technology go into behind-the-scenes marketing process and by the sheer mass of outreach necessary for start-ups.

organization: UW Department of Atmospheric and Climate Science
description: At this internship, you mostly spend time researching about a select topic in atmospheric physics and the application of weather models.
unique_experiences: • Learning Linux to run and deploy tasks to supercomputers (NCAR Casper)
surprised: The importance of communication in research. While getting data and results are equally as important, being able to communicate and share your results is integral to getting effective feedback. Through group meetings and assisting with a paper, my internship taught me how to articulate my work better along with technical skills.
takeaways: Ask questions whenever you are stuck and try different techniques! In research, the first thing you try won't often work and understanding why it doesn't along with trying new techniques will be what results in success.

description: Designing and creating labs, lab equipment, operating the copy machines in the office, and grading inquiries.
unique_experiences: • Designing nichrome wire-based resistor molds and printing out of PETG • Designing, prototyping, and creating 3D printed rotational inertia equipment for OB
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of preparation to be done for every class, and the way that grading is approached, which I largely misunderstood from a student's perspective.

organization: UW Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning Group
description: The internship consists of two stages, the first of which is going over basic ML (Neural Networks, CNN, Transformers, etc) through labs. The second stage involves choosing a research direction to work on, where students will get assigned a mentor.
unique_experiences: • Experiencing a research symposium and presenting your work at the end of the Internship • Receiving guidance and help from Post- Doc Researchers
surprised: The new things I learned when reviewing material I already knew. .

description: Interns handle administrative tasks (such as prepping and scanning charts), do some research studies in the form of entering data and searching for and reviewing relevant scientific articles, and participate in clinical work such as entering and scoring tests.
unique_experiences: I was able to have the opportunity to sit in on a patient evaluation to learn how tests are administered, their difficulty of completion, and what they test for.
surprised: What surprised me the most was the extremely debilitating effects mildly traumatic brain injuries (like concussions) could have on a person's mental functioning. As I have never experienced a concussion (to the best of my knowledge), the long-lasting effects of an mTBI seemed very vague and distant to me.
takeaways: I think the greatest takeaway was learning how a psychology clinic operates and understanding the clinical and research tools available for diagnoses.

category: Human Centered Design Engineering, Human Computer Interaction
description: I conducted and assisted research at the UW SECURE Center on co-creation and human-centered design with the goal of a research paper.
unique_experiences: • Worked alongside multiple professors, department heads, and graduate students at the University of Washington
surprised: How much different perspectives can aid one goal - as a high schooler I didn't think I would get to contribute that much due to age and inexperience but my team was very agile, finding an important role for everyone and helping me get this amazing exposure.
takeaways: Dont be afraid to try! It sounds dumb but I got this internship after my 50th cold email, you just have to keep trying. Even after I got it behind a senior in high school working with university professors and students was very intimidating, but I had an amazing experience.

description: In my internship, I worked on analyzing the impact of introducing Regional Air Mobility (eVTOL aircraft) in the West Virginia region. I learned about how aircraft are designed based on demand and network data. Then, I worked on collecting data for my specific case study, which focuses on designing a RAM aircraft to travel between Blacksburg, Virginia and Washington, DC. At the end of the year, I worked on developing a mode choice model to predict aircraft characteristics.
unique_experiences: • Learning about the steps in the process of legacy aircraft design • Conducting data analysis on West Virginia's transportation network
surprised: I was most surprised by how eager PHD researchers were to collaborate on this project.
takeaways: My main takeaway from this internship is that it is important to immerse yourself in a field to create something new. A lot of this year was spent gaining the background knowledge necessary to understand how to innovate upon existing methodologies.

description: I supported the outreach and engagement efforts through website development, social media management, newsletter creation, photo editing, and video editing. My work focused on increasing awareness of ITW's mission and programs within communities interested in inclusive technology. In addition to marketing responsibilities, I also contributed to a variety of organizational projects, including editing stop-motion animation projects and developing STEAM curriculum materials.
unique_experiences: • Contributing to annual fundraising campaign through video editing • Stop motion animation
surprised: • Marketing involved much more audience analysis and strategy than I initially expected.
takeaways: • Effective marketing combines creativity with an understanding of audience behavior and organizational goals.

location: AppEsteem Corporation, 156th Avenue Southeast Suite 275, Bellevue, WA
description: Utilizing Prompt Engineering to build an AI scam assistant which helps victims who face scams.
unique_experiences: Pushing my work onto the main page and having my boss commend me.
surprised: How quick I learned how to use AI effectively to solve my tasks.

description: Spend the first semester completing the PHYS 417 UW course, learning Machine Learning structures and code. The course specifically explores the applications of ML in the real world physical environment. Every week is spent on a different lab, then weekly the students meet with each other and Professor Hsu to discuss results, learnings, and implementations.
unique_experiences: • Conducted an independent research project under mentorship from university researchers and instructors. • Learned how research is performed at the college level, from forming a research question to presenting final results.
surprised: I was most surprised by the freedom and initiative that this internship lent.
takeaways: • University research with a team and mentorship

description: Openexa is a small fintech startup that sells services around cryptocurrency. The main service is an AI agent that predicts cryptocurrency prices and buys and sells them accordingly, advises the user about potential opportunities, and acts like a secondary trader with minimal supervision.
unique_experiences: • Working with people from across the country • Building automated n8n workflows hosting the agent logic • Creating web applications and using google cloud/azure hosting
surprised: • A strong focus on artificial intelligence
takeaways: • Try new types of work, even if you're not that experienced at it

description: The first half of the year focuses on building familiarity with various machine learning models and tasks where we unofficially took the PHYS 417 class. In the second half, we apply this learning on a new project and dataset (either worked individually or in a group).
unique_experiences: • I was able to work on an area of great interest in particle physics and AI and go past the typical applications such as image classification or regression. • At the end of the second semester, I presented along with the other interns about our results for the final project.
surprised: Machine learning can be learned while avoiding significant parts of the theory and maintaining the applications.
takeaways: • Don't hesitate to try random ideas. • It is difficult to give a presentation about a niche area. There is going to be a lot of technical jargon that seems important during the time of research but is confusing to others or irrelevant to the results.

description: Learned how a project pipeline happens in a robotics consultation company, essentially how a list of needs can be converted into a final product ready for the requesting company. Through different lessons and projects, I expanded my mechanical/electrical design and software skills related to different robotic mechanisms.
unique_experiences: Getting to work around the giant manufacturing/assembly area with all the machines and enormous robotic assemblies was super cool! I got to see how the smaller mechanisms I learned how to design translated to much, much larger scales.
surprised: I was accustomed to super-fast development with some engineering clubs at school, pushing out end results in a few months, but industrial designs and builds often take years to execute, involving lots of review rounds and redesigns.
takeaways: A lot of work for engineers doesn't just lie in the huge names you hear all the time, they outsource a lot of work to smaller companies that specialize in aspects like design, manufacturing, and assembly, so there are a multitude of positions for engineers in all kinds of fields that allow you to work on cool technology.

description: OpenEXA is a financial technology company that uses AI and software tools to make investing and market analysis more accessible. During my internship, I worked on projects involving automation, AI tools and outreach.
unique_experiences: • Used n8n and AI tools like OpenClaw to create workflows for various purposes including outreach and trading agents • Learned how AI agents connect to stock and crypto markets • Collaborated with other interns while completing independent tasks
surprised: I was surprised by how much independence and flexibility the internship gave me. I had the chance to explore new tools, solve problems on my own, and learn quickly while working on real projects.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway was that communication, adaptability, and teamwork are just as important as technical skills. I also learned to be more confident when using new tools and applying AI to real-world financial technology projects.

location: online/in person at Jack MacDonald Building
description: The Tang Lab focuses on research into the brain and determining where the conscious is located in the brain. My role as an intern was to aid with quantifying mouse movement with deep learning models such as DeepLabCut.
unique_experiences: • Researched and explored deep learning applications to biomedical research • Wrote code to go through outputs of deep learning applications to streamline data
surprised: I was surprised by how elaborate research studies are. My mentors attended multiple conferences around the nation and consulted many neuroscientists in order to gain a sense of how the lab should proceed.
takeaways: Don't be afraid to explore something new and ask questions. Mentors are here to help you and guide you. This was my first experience with machine learning or deep learning, so I had to learn the application and how it works from scratch.

description: City of Bellevue Transportation oversees, constructs, and maintains all transportation infrastructure within Bellevue, including roads, sidewalks, signaling devices, and adjacent related utilities. As an intern, I was involved primarily in the Neighborhood Sidewalk Program, Title VI Planning, and data management for COB projects. This entailed using digital tools to survey planning areas, collect and aggregate data, and present them in an informative and useful manner.
unique_experiences: • Assisting in screening for compliance with Federal infrastructure funding requirements
surprised: I was pleasantly surprised to find the transportation department in the midst of experimenting with a multitude of tools and technologies which they have not yet used.
takeaways: Everybody at the office is friendly and eager to help you, and they really appreciate the help they receive as well.

description: Work alongside the Chief Financial Officer and Head of Domestic Sales at a seafood wholesale business to organize financial paperwork and locate interested buyers.
unique_experiences: Learned proficiency in Excel, experienced working alongside the Head of Domestic Sales and Chief Financial Officer, operating in a corporate setting, and researched how to integrate AI into business.
surprised: How enthusiastic the office members were to include me in business operations and create opportunities for me to learn!
takeaways: It solidified my interest in business as a career and gave me great experience before future internships/jobs.

description: My internship with the Moritz Lab focuses on proteomics (studying proteins/peptides) research using mass spectrometry. We process and analyze data from mass spectrometers to study modifications to synthetic peptides.
unique_experiences: Being able to immerse myself in proteomics research, which is a field that I had little to no exposure prior to joining this internship. Learning about various tools that researchers use to manage their data and codebase, such as Git. Being introduced to science writing and data visualization conventions.
surprised: I was surprised by how much of this internship was comprised of coding and dry lab research. This made me curious about career pathways that do not necessarily involve a lot of lab work but are more centered around informatics and analysis.

description: Neuropsychology and Cognitive Health is a private clinical neuropsychology practice. We provide neuropsychological evaluations for individuals her are having cognitive issues usually due to a neurologic injury, such as a traumatic brain injury, or illness such as multiple sclerosis, stroke, Parkinson's disease or Alzheimer's disease.
unique_experiences: • Learning about neuropsychological tests such as PAI, WAIS-IV , BDI etc. • Interpreting test scores
surprised: I didn't know how much logistical work there was behind neuropsychology, keeping track of all the test scores and making sure all the patient information is accurate.

description: Sensoria Health is a small company in Redmond that provides the healthcare industry with the tools necessary for proactive remote monitoring. Their vision is to make everyday sensor- driven data and patient self-reported data a valuable part of every patient's rehab journey.
unique_experiences: I worked with the Smart Garment Engineer to manufacture hundreds of products and brushed up on my soldering skills in the process.
surprised: Though my first instinct was to assume that this technology was only used for patients, I was surprised to learn that this kind of technology was also used in sports industry as well.
takeaways: Advocate for yourself and don't be afraid to ask questions about how things work. Connecting with your mentor and those around you can help you learn a lot. Don't wait for opportunities to come to you. Seek them out!

organization: Olympic Coast at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
location: Olympic Peninsula, Washington State (most work conducted online)
description: I handled the social media for NOAA's Olympic Coast and conducted data analysis on their social media trends. I created many spreadsheets, posts, and animations for my team.
unique_experiences: I was able to understand the process of submitting proposals within a government bureaucracy. Additionally, I was able to have the freedom to present my original ideas regarding a social media campaign.
surprised: I was surprised that my team would listen to my ideas since I was the only high school student on the team.

description: Hospitality company that deals with optimizing POS systems for restaurants. My job is to help gather data and do basic analysis on P&L ratios and efficiency.
unique_experiences: Team Collaboration, Remote Work
surprised: How easy it was to communicate with a 3 hour time difference
takeaways: Make sure you do something meaningful and fun, otherwise it is a waste of time.

description: Giniw Technologies is a startup that runs a solar power forecasting service. In this internship, I worked on searching for new ways to analyze satellite data to find clouds in the Earth's atmosphere, specifically clouds that were over bodies of water.
unique_experiences: • Learning how different computer vision algorithms work
surprised: I was surprised by how flexible my hours were and that I was able to keep working from another time or place. At the same time, this also meant it was my responsibility to get work done and be productive while working on my internship.
takeaways: • Being proactive about your internship work and communicating with your mentor will give you the most value out of your internship • You don't need prior experience in the specific thing you're doing for your internship. You have plenty of time to learn and mentors to support you

description: Working primarily within the Industrial Training and Assessment Center (ITAC) group, they conducted real- world energy assessments at small- and medium-sized manufacturers across the Pacific Northwest and authored technical reports submitted to the U.S. Department of Energy.
unique_experiences: • Co-presented a DBD actuator-powered aviation concept at the Undergraduate ECE Research Showcase • Attended an Association of Energy Engineers (AEE) Conference at the Meydenbauer Center
surprised: The sheer variety of work was unexpected. In any given week, the focus could shift from developing hardware concepts related to aviation propulsion to authoring technical reports submitted to the U.S. Department of Energy to help industrial facilities qualify for energy efficiency funding..

organization: University of Washington – Department of Astronomy
description: I conducted work towards an original research idea I had, which is using machine learning techniques (variational autoencoders and simulation-based inference) to detect anomalies in strong gravitational lens systems.
unique_experiences: • Having control of your own research and its direction, yet with input from others • Participating in a research group with undergraduate and PhD students to discuss our research, ask questions, and learn together. • Confronting problems that haven't been solved before and mistakes that haven't been made before
surprised: I was surprised by how willing adults are, even when extremely busy, to help students with their own goals and research. Despite nearly all my work being independent, my mentor generously made lots of time to answer my questions, review my work, and propose redirections.

description: My internship comprised of research, in which I specifically continued optimizing a classification model according to my research interests. I analyzed genetic variants sourced from multi-dimensional datasets using machine learning and online bioinformatics tools. I employed data architecture techniques to improve the regression classification score for my variant interpretation model.
unique_experiences: • Employed classification algorithm methods including the use of CNNs, RNNs, and random forest models • Surveyed literature on newest research of rare disease mutation variants
surprised: What surprised me the most is how much I needed to plan out next steps to make progress in the internship.
takeaways: My greatest takeaways from the internship experience include the importance of clearly defining research tasks and tracking progress through deliverables to increase both motivation and results.

description: I was given the opportunity to model a real-world issue using machine learning. I picked out remote sensing, specifically damage classification in a post-disaster setting.
unique_experiences: working directly with large-scale remote sensing imagery and semantic segmentation datasets -exploring the intersection of machine learning, GIS, and disaster management
surprised: I was surprised by the number of insights you can glean from a single dataset. Beyond simply training the model, I used the same data to generate performance metrics, visualize patterns of damage, and more.
takeaways: I learned that machine learning projects often require repeated testing and refinement. There is opportunity to learn or fix something new in each stage of development, so the hyperparameters and function of the model are constantly evolving.

description: Professor Das was my mentor who guided me through the process of creating a machine to help solve a real- world problem. I proposed a topic to him that was within my interest area and would be feasible given how much data there was available.
unique_experiences: • Researching real world issues that can be solved with the application of machine learning • Understanding and building machine learning models (I focused on random forest)
surprised: Professor Das did a really good job explaining concepts and steps – but I needed to be proactive and ask the right questions and independently explore and decide what direction I wanted my project to take.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience was that it's important to start the project with something you're genuinely interested in but also don't be afraid to pivot topics if you feel there is something better suited or something you might be more interested in.

organization: University of Washington Quantum Devices Lab
location: Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science
description: Assorted logistical, archival, and transcriptional work directly under Professor Anantram of the UW Quantum Devices Lab.
unique_experiences: • Transcription of lecture materials concerning emergent concepts and technologies relating to the Lab's current focus; • Archiving and organization of the Lab's catalogue of academic output; • Unfettered access to the Lab's catalogue of academic output; • Maintenance of the Lab's website;
surprised: There is a great breadth of learning and understanding to be gained from simply writing about certain topics in a summative manner.
takeaways: The Feynman adage about the most potent demonstrator of expertise being the ability to condense and export complex topics is true.

description: Researching CUTAC with Lin Lab. CUTAC targets RNA Pol II Ser5p with a low-salt tagmentation step so that antibody- tethered Tn5 cuts at accessible regulatory DNA, producing high-res, low-background maps of open chromatin regions even from very few cells.
unique_experiences: Using the Hutch cluster and working in the Steam Plant.
surprised: A lot of the work we do includes stuff that I learned in biology class.
takeaways: Research is very cool and I want to do more research in college.

description: Professor Das guided me through the process of working with complex neuroimaging and data analysis. After proposing a few ideas, he guided me to a more focused approach to understand how the human brain exhibits different levels of concentration and cognitive load. To explore this, I learned how to use the OpenNeuro CLI to programmatically download and manage massive, multi- subject EEG datasets.
unique_experiences: • Using specialized neuroinformatics libraries like mne and mne-bids to systematically parse brain wave data directly from a standard BIDS directory.
surprised: I was surprised by how much backend data clearing and scaling architecture is required before you can even begin to answer a scientific question.
takeaways: My major takeaway is learning what it takes to draw interpretable, human-understandable conclusions from scrambled, messy data.

description: Helped out on the business side of a seafood product wholesaler by handling financial documents, reaching out to possible new customers, and receiving mentorship from the Chief Financial Officer and Head of Sales.
unique_experiences: I got to research the benefits of integrating AI use into the company, learned how to use Excel in a corporate setting, and made thousand-dollar sales while working alongside the Head of Sales!
surprised: The amount of close mentorship I received and that I could actually make a difference as a high school student in the financials of the company!
takeaways: I experienced working in a corporate office and gained skills in Excel, handling financial documents, and communicating professionally to office members and possible customers.

organization: Vashisth Research Lab, University of Washington
description: The Vashisth Research Lab focuses on advanced composites manufacturing (specifically researching carbon welding methods). As an intern, I had lots of flexibility in which projects to work on.
unique_experiences: • 3D printing various prototypes for lab equipment parts • Learning and applying graduate- level physics and engineering concepts
surprised: I was most surprised by my flexibility in the lab. I had the freedom to explore projects I was interested in, even if I had little relevant technical experience.

description: Jessica Scharf State Farm is an insurance and financial services firm that services clients in Washington State, Oregon, and Idaho. Most of my responsibilities consisted of filing new client insurance applications, filing claims, updating client files, and creating marketing materials.
unique_experiences: • Updated clients/leads in the official State Farm ECRM • Filed claim applications for clients • Handled billing for insurance premiums
surprised: I was surprised by how client-facing the business is. Since the business is primarily sales focused, I learned a lot about how to interact with clients.
takeaways: How to work in a team. A lot of my work involved scanning, uploading, and updating client profiles when we received insurance applications or updates.

location: Neuropsychology & Cognitive Health, 1621 114th Ave SE, Suite 122, Bellevue, WA 98004
description: Neuropsychology and Cognitive Health is a private clinical neuropsychology practice. They provide neuropsychological evaluations for individuals who are having cognitive issues usually due to a neurologic injury, such as a traumatic brain injury, or illness such as multiple sclerosis, stroke, Parkinson's disease or Alzheimer's disease.
unique_experiences: Getting the opportunity to sit in on a patient's neuropsychology testing session was a really interesting experience.
surprised: Finding out how common and widespread neurological issues are was surprising for me.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway from my internship is how important collaboration and asking questions is. When I first started this internship, there were so many new tasks that I was asked to do.

description: OpenEXA is a fintech startup company that focuses on using AI to provide and enhance financial services related to cryptocurrency.
unique_experiences: • Learning how to manage and automate workflows • Working and communicating with interns from different time zones and ages • Researching and learning different marketing software
surprised: Mainly how flexible the workload was, but also how important it is to be independent and proactive.
takeaways: A startup setting is heavily reliant on open communication and collaboration, even in a large group among seemingly unrelated teams. Being able to clearly present and share your research and findings is crucial to finding better, more efficient ways to get work done and make progress.

location: On Site: 1100 Fairview Ave N, Seattle, WA 98109, Roth Lab
description: I study the phenomenon of suspended animation in the nematode C. Elegans. When deprived of oxygen, adult nematodes can survive in an alternative death-like state for anywhere from 48 to 72 hours at room temperature. This phenomenon, termed suspended animation, can be replicated in other animals - including humans – and can prove useful in industry.
unique_experiences: The Roth lab is unique in how it conducts science. Rather than searching for genotype- phenotype correlations, my mentor finds it more convenient to assay for life and death directly.
surprised: I was surprised at both how little and how much I knew at the same time. As my mentor often says, "the more I learn, the less I know. " It was surprising to me to see how true that is.

organization: Stone Lab, University of Washington, School of Medicine, Department of Otolaryngology/Head & Neck Surgery
location: Virginia Merrill Bloedel Hearing Research Center, UW, Seattle
description: One of the lesser understood body systems is the vestibular system, which regulates balance and movement. Research on this system is more current, with many gaps.
unique_experiences: Complete immunocytochemistry experiments Use the Fiji program to measure cells
surprised: I was surprised by the robust collaboration, not just within labs, but with other labs as well.

description: I worked as a Teaching Assistant in Academic Seminar, where I graded student assignments, supported credit recovery work, and helped students stay on track with missing or incomplete coursework. When Ms. Gosselin was absent, I also led parts of lessons and helped manage the class by explaining instructions, answering questions, and keeping students engaged in their work.
unique_experiences: One of the most unique parts of the internship was teaching the class when the teacher was absent. It forced me to understand the material well enough to explain it clearly. I also worked closely with students doing credit recovery, which meant breaking down assignments in simpler ways and helping them figure out how to make up work they were behind on.
surprised: I was surprised by how much responsibility comes with even small teaching roles.
takeaways: The biggest takeaway for me is that teaching is a balance between structure and flexibility.

description: There is a variety of work done at this internship due to the assorted roles that the mentor, Kristi Oosterveen, does.
unique_experiences: The most interesting unique experience I had was simply being able to see how everyday civic projects happen and the processes behind it.
surprised: The number of options in terms of which work I was able to help out on was a surprise at first as I assumed the internship would be relatively structured.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is that there is much more work, effort, and people involved on even the simplest projects such as building a new sidewalk or repaving a street. I also realized that interns can truly have an impact no matter how minor the contributions are. I always believed that internships were simply learning and not much doing but this experience shattered those expectations as I created a form which was integral to the projects being worked on.

description: Entox Sciences is a biomedical technology startup focused on developing accessible and affordable diagnostic tools for healthcare applications.
unique_experiences: • Working in a small team of four, which allowed me to take on meaningful responsibilities and see the direct impact of my work. • Contributing to a single research project over the course of a year and following its progress from development through validation.
surprised: I was surprised by how interdisciplinary the work was. In addition to laboratory experiments and data analysis, I learned about software development, product design, scientific publishing, and how new medical technologies are presented to potential users and stakeholders.
takeaways: The internship gave me valuable insight into how biomedical technologies move from an initial concept to a functional product. I developed stronger technical and research skills, learned how to work independently on open-ended problems, and gained firsthand experience contributing to a scientific publication and real-world medical technology development.

location: Downtown Seattle, 1215 4th Ave Ste 1100, Seattle WA 98161
description: A lot of my work involved scanning documents, as well as summarizing intakes (which are a potential client's answers to the firm's questions which asks them to describe their situation). There was also organizational work on Clio, which is what the firm uses to keep track of their matters. I also did some front desk work with taking calls to the firm, sent letters, and did other miscellaneous work.
unique_experiences: It was cool being in an actual law firm and seeing how attorneys, paralegals, the admin, and the managing attorney interacted with each other.
surprised: It was surprising that a lot of the work was mainly administrative rather than reading documents or directly helping attorneys.
takeaways: A lot of work goes into a law firm beyond just the practicing law part. Unlike what you see in Suits, at SLG I really understood that law firms need clean organization, not just smart people - firms need to preserve documents well, make sure that cases are within the scope of their practice, and schedule their time well.

description: • Co-hosting events in Seattle: Reaching out to potential speakers, managing check-in and introductions, creating promotional material. • Website redesign: Frontend restructuring and copy writing. Some backend workflows for clients and sponsors.
unique_experiences: • Getting to host an event at the top of the Columbia Tower in Seattle • Speaking with a Forbes 30u30 for UI/UX help
surprised: • Venture capital work is very decentralized. No one is ever in office and even with little outreach there are so many companies that flow through the team.
takeaways: • Deadlines are always set very tight. However long you think it takes, the deadline is set for 3 days before.

description: The Baliga Lab The Baliga Lab at the Institute for Systems Biology focuses on systems biology and its applications across environmental science, medicine, and education.
unique_experiences: • Designing a dual-gas PCB sensor from schematic all the way through to a tested, functional product with no prior electrical engineering background • Conducting research and interviews to map out the landscape of careers in biotechnology and drug development and create a template for an interactive website for other high school ambassadors to contribute to
surprised: I was surprised by how many distinct careers and departments are involved in drug development. Everything is deeply interconnected in ways that aren't obvious from the outside, from the legal side of FDA regulations to the roles of patient advocates in clinical trials.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is how to stay resilient when troubleshooting. Self-learning electrical engineering from scratch to build the PCB sensor forced me to face several roadblocks in the process.

description: OpenEXA is a fintech startup company that focuses on helping traders make predictions on traditional stocks and cryptocurrencies using various metrics, such as financial news and market patterns.
unique_experiences: • Building machine learning models to predict volatility • Creating multiple dashboards to showcase existing prediction models
surprised: The internship is very flexible in terms of meeting times, as long as there is a consistent and constant line of communication with your team and mentor.
takeaways: • The best work is done when everyone is communicating whenever possible • Communicating the value of your work is very important.

description: OpenEXA is a technology startup that is focused on using machine learning for financial forecasting and market analysis.
unique_experiences: • Developing the company's machine learning/statistical models (LSTM, GARCH, XGBoost, NLP) entirely from scratch and then serving their outputs through a Flask API that I built
surprised: The fact that OpenEXA allowed me to truly be a full-stack engineer.
takeaways: I previously had mostly developed solo, personal projects, essentially writing code in a vacuum: I didn't really plan for anyone else to be able to read/maintain what I had wrote.

description: In my internship, I helped with sales, marketing, and daily gym operations. I worked on things like promotions, social media, organizing materials, helping members, and looking at sales data to see what marketing strategies worked best.
unique_experiences: One unique experience was getting to see what happens behind the scenes of a business and how much work goes into branding and sales.
surprised: What surprised me the most was how important communication and customer relationships are in running a successful business. I also didn't realize how much planning and data analysis goes into marketing and sales decisions.

organization: University of Washington Alvord Brain Tumor Center
description: The Alvord Brain Tumor Clinic is a clinic in the University of Washington Medical Center that focuses on combining radiation care, chemotherapy, and neuropsychology treatment into a single space for brain tumor patients.
unique_experiences: • Getting to shadow a radiation oncologist and attend appointments with patients • Creating surveys in REDCap and learning how to synthesize REDCap data
surprised: I was most surprised by how collaborative the medical space is across specialties.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is that it is more important to try new things than to obtain immediate results.

description: I interned at Seasalt.ai, a company that develops AI-powered tools for small businesses. Working independently under one mentor, my project was to adapt the existing IndexTTS2 neural text-to-speech model to support Taiwanese Mandarin accents.
unique_experiences: • Learned fine-tuning, Low-Rank Adaptation, and Gradient Reversal Learning for speaker-accent disentanglement • built a fully local Speech-to-Speech system integrating Whisper, Ollama, and IndexTTS2 via a Dockerized Flask backend
surprised: I was surprised by how much ownership I was given from day one. I expected more structured guidance, but instead I was trusted to design and carry out experiments entirely on my own.
takeaways: The biggest lesson I learned is that communication is very important. IS NOT LOOKING FOR AN INTERN NEXT YEAR

description: I interned with a co-founder as a data intern at FEV Analytics. FEV Analytics is a financial technology firm that provides advanced valuation and performance analytics for private asset owners and managers.
unique_experiences: One unique experience I had at this internship was getting to lead an independent project where I got to collect and analyze financial information to find trends in it.
surprised: What surprised me the most was how important clear and timely communication was in private equity.
takeaways: This internship helped me improve my communication, collaboration, and organizational skills. I also learned a lot about the private equity business and the roles and responsibilities of a data analyst.

location: Cascade Yard in the Eastgate District (3380 146th Pl SE, Suite 340, Bellevue, WA 98007)
description: Binaytara is a non-profit working to reduce health disparities in cancer care across the U.S. through their accredited conferences as well as internationally, through their Nepal clinic and through their support of a Nigeria clinic.
unique_experiences: • Cosplayed as a nurse working in the Nigeria clinic to educate about the new cancer care and safety technologies Binaytara funded & encourage attendees of the annual Gala to donate
surprised: The amount of different skillsets required to run a non- profit, and the amount of collaboration required between departments to be able to run events & conferences.

location: University of Washington – Tacoma, University of Washington – Seattle, Center for Urban Waters
description: I interned with Emily Everton, a PhD student in Prof. Ed Kolodziej's lab at the University of Washington. I was working on collecting and characterizing tire wear particles.
unique_experiences: One unique experience was conducting field work with my mentor. We would go and sample different urban streams, specifically targeting active salmon- breeding streams.
surprised: I was most surprised by the number of replicate samples and trial studies needed to validate one set of data.

description: During my internship with Juhua, I learned more about computer science in a real workplace setting.
