I observed how technology is used to support company operations, organize information, and improve efficiency. I also gained experience with problem- solving, communication, and understanding how technical work connects to business needs.
unique_experiences: A unique experience was seeing how computer science skills are applied outside of the classroom. I got to learn about real technology tasks and how different people work together to solve problems.
surprised: I was surprised by how much planning and communication goes into technical work. I realized that computer science is not just about coding, but also about understanding problems clearly and working with others.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was learning that strong technical skills need to be combined with teamwork, patience, and clear communication. This internship helped me better understand how computer science is used in a professional environment and made me more confident about pursuing technology-related opportunities in the future. ===== PAGE 63 ===== Leya Balayoghan leyavb@outlook.com Category: Research: Physics/Computing Research Company/Organization: UW Bothell Gravitational Wave Astronomy Research Group Location: Hybrid (Online + various presentation locations) Internship description: Within the Gravitational Wave Astronomy (GWA) research group, there are two sub-groups that high school interns can join. In the Pulsar Search Collaboratory group, the focus is on doing research: combing plots from large-sky surveys for possible pulsars and more advanced research projects, where students analyze data for pulsar scintillation, fast radio bursts, and/or giant pulses. Unique experiences: • Using the Skynet Robotic Telescope Network to remotely take data with the 20-meter telescope at Green Bank Observatory What surprised me the most: The most surprising thing about my internship was the learning curve—even though some of the techniques taught were somewhat advanced, there were always guiding steps, so that someone with a high-school physics background and little to no coding experience could understand advanced gravitational wave data processing techniques. My greatest takeaways from the internship experience: From my internship, I learned tools for data analysis within radio astronomy and the methods behind research in that field, increased my scientific literacy through weekly paper readings ===== PAGE 64 =====

description: The López Lab focuses on understanding the basic mechanisms of platelet adhesion and the roles that platelets play in inflammation.
unique_experiences: • Gaining hands-on experience in a lab, including using various machines such as the CytoFlex • Processing and analyzing data with Excel, ImageJ, and CytExpert
surprised: I was surprised by how the scientists have approached the growth of AI—they are working on creating a tool to help pull quantitative data from images for analysis. I was also surprised by how passionate every lab member was about their work.

description: Magnolia Soccer Club is a youth soccer club located in the Magnolia neighborhood of Seattle. I helped the club with organizing their internal processes, marketing, and future planning.
unique_experiences: • Accessed marketing data for a real company • Got to see how real businesses organize their operations
surprised: I had no idea how real businesses organized their operations, and seeing that process live was entirely new for me. It was my first time using Asana, as well as other tools used by real companies.

description: I interned in the Geoff Hill Lab at Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, where research focuses on understanding how the immune system responds to cancer such as CAR T-cell therapy. During my internship, I strengthened my wet lab skills by constructing an artificial T-cell receptor (TCR) and introducing it into cells for functional studies.
unique_experiences: • Created artificial TCRs including developing an online outline • Learned college level immunology concepts • Invited to retreats where new research was presented
surprised: One thing that surprised me was the flexibility of researchers' schedules. My mentor worked both as a researcher and as a physician in the oncology department at UW.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this experience was gaining a better understanding of the differences between wet lab work and computational research, and discovering which aspects I enjoy more as I move forward in college and explore potential career paths.

organization: University of Washington School of Medicine South Lake Union Campus
description: 439 8th Ave N, Seattle, WA 98109
unique_experiences: Traveling between multiple research and clinical sites across Seattle, including South Lake Union, the Fred Hutch Research Center, Harborview Medical Center, and the University of Washington Seattle campus, to conduct experiments and participate in research activities.
surprised: The amount of time required for transportation. Without a car, traveling from Interlake High School to the research site and then home each day took much longer than I expected and showed me the level of commitment required to pursue research opportunities.
takeaways: I learned that publishing scientific research often takes years of careful experimentation, analysis, and revision. The internship also showed me the importance of developing strong laboratory techniques, problem- solving skills, and scientific knowledge to succeed in the biomedical research field.

description: Fred Hutch Cancer Center is a large collaboration of multiple labs dedicated to researching specific fields and working together to find essential medical information that helps offer better care to patients. As an intern in the Boeckh Lab, I was primarily in charge of managing, sorting, and filling the biospecimen repository.
unique_experiences: • Hands-on lab experience for the entire duration of the internship • Working in a collaborative environment and having a firsthand view of research in a professional setting
surprised: I was surprised by how collaborative the environment is. Everyone has a specific role they play in the lab and working together is essential to be able to get all the work done. If even one job is left unfulfilled, work piles up and others' work is halted. rather than turn to a guide or someone else to tell me exactly what to do.
takeaways: Being flexible and adaptable are essential when working in a lab environment. Without those skills, the level of work you can do (and will be entrusted with) will diminish and others will also be negatively impacted.

description: Located in downtown Bellevue, interns are tasked with helping keep the International Law Society, a nonprofit run and owned by the firm, up and running as well as aid attorneys in their individual tasks and endeavors.
unique_experiences: • Established collaborations with the St. James Cathedral Church • Participated in the Law Office archiving project • Filed for grants from the UCA Foundation and was awarded 1,300 dollars for the society
surprised: I was really surprised by how much you can learn from individually keeping an entire organization running, from managing its tax records and tracking down its passwords to using funds for outreach and advertisement and establishing noticeable changes and improvements to the organization's size.
takeaways: Legal education in this internship's regard isn't just teaching you knowledge about the law but about just an efficient work person in general and adult skills, it builds organization skills and really builds communication ability with the rest of the firm. This was a wonderful and cherished experience.

description: A remote internship where you help create the elements of a startup, from marketing to investors to generating prototypes to web design. You work closely with other interns to build the company from the ground up.
unique_experiences: automated digital workflows, creating new AI models, researching market competitors, web design, AI integration into workflows
surprised: How many little elements went into creating a company and how the application of AI is so widespread in every field

organization: UW Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning Group
location: UW Physics and Astronomy Building (PAB) 3910 15th Ave NE, Seattle, WA 98195
description: First semester, interns go through all labs of the PHY417 class at UW to familiarize themselves with machine learning techniques. Second semester, interns work with the professor as well as graduate students on a project that puts the knowledge from first semester to use.
unique_experiences: I got to work with EveNet, which is this foundation model for particle physics that Professor Shih-Chieh Hsu was contributing to. Working this new model was a unique experience.
surprised: The skills I developed using current AI tools surprised me the most. I was questioned for not coding with AI. We were encouraged to use AI to help us break down the technical physics side of the project so we could spend more time on the data science side.
takeaways: Overall, this was a very educational internship experience, though you likely will not have any final product to show for it. This internship is more about the process and the learning.

description: As an intern with the City of Bellevue Transportation Department, I worked on projects related to emergency management and transportation planning.
unique_experiences: One unique experience was helping update emergency management resources used by the city. I was able to see how transportation planning connects with public safety and emergency response.
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of planning and coordination required behind the scenes to keep a city running smoothly. Every transportation project involves input from multiple departments, careful research, and consideration of how decisions will impact residents.
takeaways: This internship taught me the importance of public service, teamwork, and effective communication. I gained a better understanding of how local government operates and how transportation systems affect daily life.

description: We were given a variety of low-level tasks. These included collecting the mail, answering calls, making timelines, sending letters, organizing a client's documents, filing things, taking notes during consultations, sending emails, calling clients, etc. Occasionally, we were asked to draft things.
unique_experiences: There were a few strange and interesting clients of the law office. Sometimes, I took notes during their consultations or took one of their calls. They definitely were not people I interact with on a day-to-day basis.
surprised: I was surprised by how the staff was so welcoming and willing to answer questions.
takeaways: It's always better to ask questions and clear up any misunderstandings before you start something than do it wrong and fix the mistakes afterwords.

description: I worked as a dental assistant, got my certification and helped with tasks like x-rays, cleaning and setting up, sterilizing instruments, etc. Shadowed the dentist and researched topics assigned to me.
unique_experiences: Positioning X-ray instruments in patients' mouths, taking blood pressure and charting.
surprised: That bare hands are considered clean in a dental office.
takeaways: Consistency and showing up every day helps you build rapport with your mentor and gain access to more responsibilities in your internship.

description: As a marketing intern at EatHappyNow, I helped edit promotional videos and supported outreach to local businesses and donors to increase community engagement.
unique_experiences: A unique part of my internship was visiting both donors and food recipients to take short interviews and video clips. Seeing the people and businesses involved in person allowed me to understand the real-life impact of my work beyond video editing.
surprised: What surprised me most was seeing how much joy a simple food delivery or donation could bring to someone. Watching people's faces light up showed me that my work has a real and personal impact.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway was that the work felt more meaningful once I got to see the people behind it. Whether I was helping with outreach, editing videos, or delivering food, I realized how much of an impact small efforts can have when they reach the right people.

organization: University of Washington Department of Pharmacology
description: The Sancak lab investigates mitochondrial calcium uptake, signalling, and the consequences of disturbing that in relation to metabolic disorders.
unique_experiences: • Participated in weekly scientific journal and data presentations • Challenging graduate-level research and opportunities for collaboration • Accommodating environment with incredibly supportive and friendly coworkers
surprised: I expected to be washing beakers for hours on end. I was pleasantly astonished to find myself appropriately academically challenged, and as someone who loves getting hands-on, there was plenty of wet lab work to go around. Ask for a new experience and you shall receive.
takeaways: Start looking for internships early! If you get one before your senior year, try coming in a few weeks early over the summer to get the hang of any new equipment or laboratory techniques you have to end up using frequently.

description: At ARC Lab, we push the boundaries of innovation in autonomy, robotics, and connectivity to create impactful, real-world solutions.
unique_experiences: • Created PCBs, software, CAD for test setups • Did lit review & paper writing
surprised: I was surprised by the amount of time spent reading and writing. When I joined, I expected my time to be spent designing hardware or writing code, but I realized reading papers, making posters, and writing papers and documentation are a much bigger part of research.

organization: University of Washington Aeronautics & Astronautics Rotating Detonation Engine Lab
description: The Rotating Detonation Engine (RDE) Lab focuses on emerging methods of propulsion. Right now, rotating detonation engines are more efficient and sustainable forms of propulsion relative to traditional rocket engines, and the UW RDE Lab is working on scaling their model in hopes to commercialize and globalize rotating detonation engines
unique_experiences: The RDE Lab is currently helping a NASA funded startup to test and scale their rotating detonation engines.
surprised: What surprised me most was how intuitive many of the solutions were.

description: The UW MODLYS Lab focuses on spatiotemporal modeling and dynamical control of complex biological systems. As a research intern, I helped develop a deep learning pipeline using Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) to predict future morphological states of tumor cells from time-series microscopy images.
unique_experiences: • Co-authoring and publishing a peer-reviewed paper as a high school student • Working directly alongside PhD students and a university professor in real lab meetings
surprised: I expected that giving the model more data would always improve performance, but in our real biological experiments, adding more time points sometimes made results worse.
takeaways: Research is collaborative and iterative, and unexpected results are part of the process. I learned to think critically about data and model performance, and to communicate findings clearly.

description: Observed mentor's teaching methods and tutored students both during in-class worktime and remotely, helping them understand new concepts, solve the classwork problems, or prepare for tests and quizzes.
unique_experiences: Learning how to optimally tutor and help struggling students enabled me to help my own friends prepare for our own upcoming math exams.
surprised: I was very surprised by my own initial struggles with trying to tutor confused students, because I initially thought explaining a problem or a concept from first principles would allow them to obtain deep understanding of the material, but gradually, I realized this method of teaching often left students more confused, making me realize the tremendous balancing acts required in teaching.
takeaways: While I may not become a teacher growing up, learning how to optimally connect with new people, how to optimally guide and mentor people with lesser experience, and how to optimally evaluate the progress of others are all essential tools to have in the modern-day workforce.

location: Aerospace & Engineering Research Building, University of Washington
description: At the University of Washington's Husky Satellite Lab, I led the design and testing of a DNA origami payload that flew on Blue Origin's NS-35 suborbital mission.
unique_experiences: Collaborating with researchers from the MIT Media Lab and the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS) on a real flight payload while still in high school and then watching the samples I designed return from suborbital space.
surprised: How much I still had to learn. When I reached my limits of machine learning experience, I enrolled in multivariable calculus and algorithms at UW because I needed the math.

description: Helped forge key company partnerships by attending, sponsoring, and hosting events while partnering with multiple adjacent providers in various ecosystems.
unique_experiences: Getting to spearhead and host my very own event.
surprised: The amount of autonomy I had during the internship, I thought I'd be under constant scrutiny and would be told what to do many of times, but I got to put my own twist on a lot of tasks given to me and I ended up providing a lot of value to the startup.

organization: University of Washington, Department of Atmospheric and Climate Science
description: I conducted computational atmospheric science research on how persistent subtropical ridging and atmospheric blocking may condition atmospheric river (AR) pathways and extreme precipitation along the U.S. West Coast. Using Python, I analyzed European Reanalysis (ERA5) data through object-based tracking methods.
unique_experiences: I learned to interpret code outputs physically, especially for mechanisms involving jet extension, Rossby wave breaking, tropical-extratropical interaction, and AR moisture transport.
surprised: I was surprised by how sensitive conclusions can be to definitions and thresholds. Whether a feature is identified as blocking or ridging depends strongly on the methodology, so the research required both programming skill and careful scientific judgment.

description: This role is a teaching assistant for Mr. Allen, a freshman biology and IB HL biology teacher. Tasks for this internship will mostly revolve around assisting him with his work.
unique_experiences: If you come from his IB HL biology class, some of the things you will be performing will be similar. You most likely already know some skills around pipetting, agar creation, microscope, etc. If you are a senior who took freshman biology with Mr. Allen without IB HL biology, this internship will teach you all of the above and even more.
surprised: I was most surprised by how much dirty glassware gets used by all students during the Individual Investigation season. It is hard not to get bogged down by all of it.
takeaways: Mr. Allen is a really, really great person (of course, this is all subjective). This internship is also really good at showing that wet lab skills in IB HL biology can still be improved so much more. Also, I definitely lost all my repulsion of cleaning highly dirty objects through this internship.

organization: University of Washington Lean Research Group
description: I joined Professor Klavins' Lean Research Group, which involved attending group meetings with UW students and working on my own projects. I connected a mathematical proof checker (a programming language called Lean) with cutting-edge large language models (LLMs), creating a feedback loop project where Lean gave feedback to the LLM on its proof, iterating until the LLM came up with the correct proof.
unique_experiences: • Experienced one-on-one mentorship by Professor Klavins with a combination of direct and independent learning. • Participated in a group of talented and invested programmers (UW students).
surprised: I was surprised by the approachability of projects like the one I presented at the poster session (the Lean-LLM feedback loop). While it initially seemed daunting (and did take a lot of time), I eventually understood it well and was able to present it.
takeaways: My greatest takeaways from my internship with Professor Klavins are that research is very self-driven, though mentorship is also integral to success.
mentor_name: Professor and ECE Chair Eric Klavins

description: Helped with marketing, video editing, and graphic design for Kirkland Arts Center, a local nonprofit that supports artists and arts education in the community.
unique_experiences: Got to film and edit videos featuring real local artists, design materials and planning for actual events, and see my work published and used right away.
surprised: How much creative freedom I was given. I expected to just assist, but I was actually trusted to make design decisions and manage content on my own pretty quickly.

description: This hole-in-the-wall optometry office is taking on an intern to do pretty much anything within legal boundaries. This includes shadowing, admin work, or simply helping with miscellaneous tasks.
unique_experiences: Being a small business owner, Dr. Jin's day-to-day involves not only eye care and customer service, but also finances and long-term planning. He will go over the office's stats once a month, and hearing the numbers as a reflection of its operations is fairly revelatory from an outside perspective.
surprised: Most new patients lack knowledge about their eye health. For instance, a common mistaken belief is that myopia (nearsightedness) is an inherent weakening of the eye or eye disease.
takeaways: Patient care is quite undervalued in medical visits these days. The Google reviews don't lie – Dr. Jin and Jackie are patient and cater to their patients' needs as best they can. I often find that new patients leave with a greater understanding about their health than when they came in. In Dr. Jin's words, he prioritizes patient care over patient volume.

description: HOA is an architecture firm based in Kirkland that specializes in designing school buildings, although their actual portfolio is much broader, and is the architecture firm that is currently working on the remodel of Interlake High School.
unique_experiences: Aside from a basic look into the world of architecture, I also had the opportunity to work on and design two bid sets for the World Language Building, that might end up being built in the upcoming remodel of the school.
surprised: I was surprised by the variety of work done by architects that are actively practicing in the workplace. While design and creativity are key aspects to the role, the role of an architect expands much farther as they look at smaller things like wood stains, paint colors, and more important but understated things like land use code and city code.

description: I worked on human-computer interaction research with Jaewon, focusing on how people use technology and online platforms.
unique_experiences: A unique part of this internship was contributing to real university research while still in high school.
surprised: What surprised me most was how much research involves careful observation and interpretation.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway was learning how computer science can be connected to human behavior and social impact. This experience strengthened my interest in designing technology that is thoughtful, ethical, and centered around people.

description: The Taskar Center for Accessible Technology is a lab within the University of Washington that works on developing new technology to help make the world more accessible for everyone.
unique_experiences: The lab environment itself was very unique as although this is mostly a computer science internship, the lab works on many real devices and tools so there are always fun gadgets in the lab to ask about and learn from.
surprised: The thing that surprised me the most in my internship experience was how AI use was not just accepted but encouraged within the workplace.
takeaways: It's really important to maintain a good relationship with your coworkers in the lab. You will have significantly more fun and enjoy yourself more if you talk and get to know the other people around..

organization: Oregon State University - Driving and Bicycling Simulator Laboratory
description: The Hurwitz Research Program at Oregon State University focuses on advancing transportation safety by researching traffic control systems and user behavior. As a research assistant, I analyzed traffic sensor footage for the Portland Bureau of Transportation to help design new Rest-in-red safety treatments.
unique_experiences: • Transcribing and classifying multimodal traffic data from real-world intersection footage. • Conducting rigorous peer quality checks on 100- observation data sets to minimize individual biases.
surprised: I was surprised by how much autonomy I was given. Being completely self-directed allowed me to choose my own hours and work when I could concentrate best, which was crucial given the intense focus and meticulous accuracy required for transportation research.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is the importance of accountability and peer review in engineering research.

description: SEAL focuses on sensors, energy, and automation, harnessing electrical and computer engineering to submit papers and proposals in areas like medicine, aviation, defense, and the environment.
unique_experiences: • Conduct VLF and ULF experiments in the ECE department basement
surprised: The lab has a unique system that retains efficiency despite numerous projects being worked on every day. There's a separate department within the lab called Sudoku that maintains a system that the lab created to maintain productivity, making operations very streamlined. I never had any busy work, or any worries about missing meetings/work sessions thanks to this system.
takeaways: A lot of the concepts that are being researched about are not impossible to learn for a high school student. They just have a very steep learning curve and everything is made easier by just understanding relevant literature using AI.

description: Generally, School/TA internship tasks will vary significantly based on the teacher. As an intern for Mrs. Cook, who had the IB Language & Literature and Academic Seminar during 6/7th period, my main tasks were: • Participating in 1-on-1 tutoring during Academic Seminar for students working on credit recovery and reaching graduation requirements (AVID-style class) • Serving as a stand-in teacher on days when Mrs. Cook is absent.
unique_experiences: • 1-on-1 tutoring with students in Academic Seminar led to many genuine friendships and bonds with students I probably wouldn't have met otherwise.
surprised: The wide variety of subjects covered in Academic Seminar.
takeaways: I learned how to connect with all different types of students with different personalities, interests, and temperaments. I realized the importance of showing up every day reliably.

description: A behind-the-scenes look into how district-level decisions are made, how different demographics are prioritized, and who is impacted by these decisions
unique_experiences: Presenting research on prioritizing student voice in Panorama surveys to the school board members; recording for podcasts/advertisements for the communications team; attending the Bellevue Chamber economic summit; providing feedback and research on communicating district policy changes to the community; supporting student placement and new families at the Welcome Center.
surprised: How large level organizations make human- centered decisions, and prioritize different demographic groups.

description: I have done administrative work with helping in workplace functions and general planning for maintaining and operating the workplace. Additionally, I also work on assisting in bug testing and research in that function through conducting tests and game testing certain features like phone rendering to optimize the new operating system for newly releasing phones
unique_experiences: Testing and working on bugs for a large company and communicating with test teams overseas.
surprised: How complex many of the simplest tasks could be. Some tasks required permissions that often took weeks to get approved.
takeaways: Speak up at internship to get the best opportunities. Although I was only there to help with administrative activities, I got to do so many CS related activities and game testing by asking around proactively.

description: A pediatric therapy clinic that offers occupational, physical, and speech therapy for kids that are neurodivergent, ADHD, etc. Shadow and connect with various therapists/pathologists in their line of work. Help with organization and simple tasks to ensure the clinic works smoothly as an intern.
unique_experiences: Every summer, CTW hosts a summer camp where I volunteered in assisting the speech pathologist and occupational therapist leading the camp. In these 2 weeks, it really opened up my perspective in working with kids and how they see the world.
surprised: How welcoming the therapists are and how nuanced their line of work is as they have to adapt to each different kid
takeaways: The internship is what you make of it, so it is important to put yourself out there and connect with others as much as possible to get the most out of it.

location: University of Washington NanoEngineering and Sciences
description: Worked under Dr. Sara Mouradian, implementing and improving numerical models of a trapped ion.
unique_experiences: • Was able to analyze really interesting data gathered from ion traps • Discussing and talking with others working at the lab other than mentor
surprised: The people there are really relaxed and nice to be around. I thought it would be really scary and kind of daunting, but everyone was really welcoming! Turns out they're all just normal people.
takeaways: It's a lot of what you make it. Your mentor isn't going to always pester you to do stuff, check in with you every day, etc., since they're busy with more important matters.

description: As an intern at the Lopez Lab, I was responsible for analyzing some of the data that had previously been collected, along with running a few flow cytometry labs for some practice and support. I used tools such as ImageJ to analyze qualitative data collected regarding blood cells, specifically the movement of platelets during blood clotting.
unique_experiences: • Learned how to conduct a complete flow cytometry lab from scratch, including determining which fluorophores to use for which types of cells
surprised: I was most surprised by the amount of information I had learned in IB biology HL that was used throughout my internship.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway has been learning how to use tools such as ImageJ and CytExpert, which I would not have used in any other setting.

description: This OpenEXA internship primarily provides roles as a software developer. As a software dev, you get to work on projects related to trading bots and crypto software, often using AI agents to power the trading and the software.
unique_experiences: The internship gives you access to unique learning experiences that high schoolers typically wouldn't get from an internship.
surprised: I was surprised by the wide variety of skills this internship trains you for. Instead of being stuck in a specific role or project for months at a time, this internship frequently rotates you between tasks, so you are never stuck on the same thing for months at a time.

description: As a medical intern, my primary role was performing the duties of a medical assistant, including rooming patients, taking vital signs, reading lab, and assisting with clinical tasks.
unique_experiences: o Learning to review and interpret lab results o Conducting urine analysis and understanding testing procedures o Shadowing with sick patient visits
surprised: What surprised me the most was how much healthcare involves communication, teamwork, and interpersonal skills beyond medical knowledge alone. It was interesting to see how the healthcare system works beyond just the physicians but the technician, MA's, etc. and how they all manage and contribute to patient care in a different way.
takeaways: Working in a clinical setting deepened my understanding of medicine and the role of healthcare professionals.

description: Centific helps businesses build, train, deploy, and govern intelligent systems through high-quality data, human expertise, and end-to-end platforms..
unique_experiences: • Interviewing and setting up the company summer program • Creating SOP for student worker program team
surprised: The casual environment of the office. People would often engage in casual conversation, discussing topics using native languages.
takeaways: Everything is self-driven, you will often have to find learning rather than waiting for it to come to you. Relationships are built on small moments rather than large projects, finding those conversations will give you more than you can imagine. Learn from everything, there is opportunity in details.

location: 4010 Lake Washington Blvd NE, Kirkland, WA 98033
description: Working alongside a team, I conducted focus groups, designed new spaces, redesigned existing ones, navigated permitting processes, and built connections across different sectors of the industry. This allowed me to get hands on experience with professional work before going to college.
unique_experiences: I got to design a portion of Interlake that was not initially within the scope of the project. The district asked HOA to look into the space and I was tasked to try to create some options based on info from personal experience and teacher feedback.
surprised: The efficiency that architects being able to balance constant events and meetings, still getting their designs done, and code analysis done.
takeaways: Getting to do real work that I can expect to continue in college, creating connections in the industry,

description: Conducted research at the University of Washington's High-Performance Data- Intelligence Computing (HPDIC) lab under mentorship from university/graduate researchers.
unique_experiences: One of the most unique aspects of my internship was contributing to research that allowed me to first- author a preprint paper.
surprised: What surprised me the most was the amount of creativity and thought that could go into deeply technical research, and just how fun applications-based research can be!
takeaways: My greatest takeaways from the internship experience included how to approach open-ended and interdisciplinary subjects in research, and also learning how researchers design, analyze, and redesign experiments.

description: As a Teacher's Assistant for IB Biology, I supported students during labs and class activities by answering questions, helping explain biological concepts, and assisting with experiment setup and organization.
unique_experiences: One of the most unique aspects of the internship was being able to see biology from both the student and teaching perspective.
surprised: What surprised me most was how much behind-the- scenes work goes into making a single lesson run smoothly.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway was learning the importance of clear communication and mentorship in education. The experience strengthened my leadership, collaboration, and problem-solving skills while deepening my appreciation for biology and scientific inquiry.

description: Cole Health is a pediatric therapy clinic with locations in both Bellevue and Bothell. They provide outpatient speech, physical, and occupational therapy for children. My main responsibilities included helping therapists with sessions by preparing materials/setting up supplies, maintaining a clean clinic environment, and many opportunities to observe a variety of sessions.
unique_experiences: I got a lot of opportunities to work with kids with diverse special needs. It was both challenging and fulfilling to learn to communicate, interact, and connect with them.
surprised: It surprised me the most that all the providers were super willing to share their knowledge and experiences with me and made sure I was learning.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship experience is being able to get a good idea of what it is like working as a pediatric rehab therapist in this outpatient clinic setting.

description: I TA'd with Ms. Cook for her IB Lang/Lit HL 2 Class and her Academic Seminar class. For IB Lang, I'd help organize the classroom or help grade assignments. Some tasks included grading learner portfolios, rearranging books, chopping paper, and helping take down posters on the walls.
unique_experiences: One unique experience was working directly with students in Academic Seminar and helping them complete assignments and knowledge checks.
surprised: I was surprised by how determined and motivated many of the students in Academic Seminar were. They'd faced academic challenges but were still working hard to complete assignments and improve their grades.

location: Online/8eIlevue City Hall Internship description My internship was about transportation planning within Bellevue. My mentor handled a variety of things (like mapping, streets, and transit) and I was assigned to sidewalk construction. While sidewalks seem simple, the process is a bit bureaucratic. I outreached to residents about the city's safety initiatives, gathered their requests for sidewalks in their neighborhoods, evaluated their effectiveness and cost using city databases, and presented my findings to the transportation committee who makes the decisions. I also occasionally worked on other projects in the transportation and other departments. Unique experiences Learning how to use the city's transportation databases, like Mapshot and TIMS Seeing how city initiatives and policies are implemented and how residents can work with the government to get stuff built in their neighborhoods Overall getting experience in how city governments work
description: My internship was about transportation planning within Bellevue. My mentor handled a variety of things (like mapping, streets, and transit) and I was assigned to sidewalk construction. While sidewalks seem simple, the process is a bit bureaucratic. unique_experiences: Learning how to use the city's transportation databases, like Mapshot and TIMS Seeing how city initiatives and policies are implemented and how residents can work with the government to get stuff built in their neighborhoods Overall getting experience in how city governments work
surprised: There are a lot of steps and people involved in getting something built by the city. Streets and sidewalks, for example, have to be specifically requested by residents, evaluated for equity among other things, sent to review committees, and engineering!
takeaways: My mentor was and lenient with what things I had to do and when I had to get them done. I got everything done in a (mostly) timely manner, but even in a professional setting, it's okay to ask for extensions. As an Intern, mentors expect questions from you, so don't be afraid to ask them about anything (just don't send too many emails, because most people don't like them).

location: Kirkland, Washington Internship description The Law Office of Jenny Cochrane is a general practice firm that handles a variety of legal matters. My experience as an intern consisted mainly of client outreach and assisting the paralegals and Ms. Cochrane wth paperwork for different cases. The tasks changed daily based on what was needed for the casesv The day-to-day experience varied from scanning documents for clients' case files, reaching out to potential new clients, typing case notes, communicating with opposing counsel, creating exhibit binders for trials etc. There are also many organizational tasks to be done around the office such as organizing the client contact information and the client hard files. Unique experiences Communicating with first-time clients and taking notes/summaries of their cases. Creating exhibit binders for trials. Listening in on how a virtual trial operates (could shift to in-person trials). Processing and recording payments for clients.

surprised: I was surprised at how beneficial working in e small business can be. Working in an environment where communication is streamlined across the company was very helpful as there was always someone to answer any questions I had. I got the opportunity to learn how a small business markets themselves, prices services, records payments, and maintains a brand image.
takeaways: Working in a law office was a new experience for me. My greatest takeaway was that working in the legal field entails the skill of maintaining objectivity when dealing with emotion. Almost of all the clients are biased towards the case they are presenting so it was important to maintain professionalism when hearing about emotiona ly charged stories. This strengthened my proactivity. Initially, I was a little nervous when talking to clients over the phone and not being able to give them the information they needed at the time. However, as I gained more experience throughout the internship, I learned how to think on my feet quickly.

location: Bellevue Internship description Equinox Business Law Group is a law firm which serves small and mid-sized businesses in Washington. Part of the main methodology behind the firm is providing "general counsel services" (GCS) to businesses that aren't big enough to have their own in-house general counsel. GCS entails advising on all kinds of legal issues companies might face, as well as consulting for legal and management strategy. My internship projects typically fell into one of three categories: research (helping lawyers investigate legal issues clients have), marketing (looking over promotional material, social media, newsletters), and operational work (administrative systems related work). Unique experiences Valuable hands-on learning about conducting legal research and legal issues that businesses face (especially small businesses and startups) Interviews and discussions with lawyers and business owners — very useful If you're considering going into law or business

location: Online Internship description Fred Hutch is one of the leading cancer research organizations in the greater Seattle area and is internationally recognized for their scientific contributions. My time at the Fred Hutch consisted of working on a meta-analysis paper where I review all the literature surrounding a specific topic to see the overall trend or rough consensus regarding the topic. My meta-analysis covered the correlation between kimchi consumption and gastrointestinal cancers, where I used PubMED and other science literature websites to search for different papers regarding my topic. Unique experiences Brainstorming potential topics for a scientific paper that I was passionate about Organizing and conducting my own research for the scientific paper and getting used to navigating and reading all different lengths of scientific literature
description: Fred Hutch is one of the leading cancer research organizations in the greater Seattle area and is internationally recognized for their scientific contributions. My time at the Fred Hutch consisted of working on a meta-analysis paper where I review all the literature surrounding a specific topic to see the overall trend or rough consensus regarding the topic. My meta-analysis covered the correlation between kimchi consumption and gastrointestinal cancers, where I used PubMED and other science literature websites to search for different papers regarding my topic.
unique_experiences: Brainstorming potential topics for a scientific paper that I was passionate about Organizing and conducting my own research for the scientific paper and getting used to navigating and reading all different lengths of scientific literature
surprised: It is very important to be very organized while writing a scientific paper. From keeping the Excel sheet easy to follow to diligently tracking your sources, everything needs to be very methodical and if you're sloppy on one thing, such as not noting down an author's last name ofa paper, going back to fix your mistake and find the lest name is very tedious.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is that everything won't be perfect, but that shouldnJt stop me from trying to make it as perfect as possible. There were a lot of times where I was discouraged, such as when I was first trying to find papers for my original topic but had to change it because there was not enough literature covering it. aut instead of giving up on my topic and changing it altogether, I found literature that was tangentially related to keep one part of my scientific topic I was initially passionate about, My research wasn't perfect, and I could have saved a lot of time scrapping my topic and getting a new one, but I was really passionate about a certain part of my topic and tried to keep it the best I could which turned out to be successful.

location: Seattle Internship description ADCS is a dental practice in downtown Seattle that sees patients four days a week. surprised: I was most surprised by the extreme importance af customer relations in dentistry. While an obvious skill required in dentistry is a doctorate degree, an equally important skill that is often overlooked is making sure that patients feel safe and comfortable. When owning a dental practice, it is important to recognize that it is a business and that business practices are still important.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway is the extreme level of insight I gained by simply working at a dental practice. I was able to see the daily joys and struggles of owning a dental practice and this experience allowed me to better analyze my own college goals and ponder whether or not dentistry is the right career for me.

description: Discover Magic is a program that partners with grade schools to teach magic, while also helping kids develop many other fundamental skills. Also, that you cannot plan for everything, no matter how much you prepare there are elements that can go wrong in live performance or in teaching.

As a creative writer, I think I would've liked an internship that was leaning more towards the arts instead of directly rehashing what happens in the community to the paper. I think if you're looking to go into communications, NWAW is a great place for you.

location: Seattley Washington Internship description In the beginning of the internship, Professor Colin Marshall and I decided to focus on German philosopher Immanuel KantJ as that was his specialty. However, throughout the year, we explored topics such as the ethics of persuasion (namely, the art of rhetoric) as well many modern philosophers* interpretation on Kant. My meetings with Prof. Marshall revolved primarily around discussing the previous week or two weeks' readings. These readings consisted of material anywhere from chapter in a book, manuscript, or drafts. This year specifically, because of Prof. Marshall's schedule, allowed for more editorial and independent projects such as writing up commentary an his graduate students' papers and personally choosing philosophical topics to discuss. Unique experiences Flexibility to find and focus on my own philosophical interest through constant reading and research Reading and editing numerous 30+ page long drafts as part of the I<FA workshop hosted by Prof. Marshall and other academics Writing my own mini paper regarding Kant on learning from example Exploring topics such as "Punishment", "Outies", and "Ethical Theory" in a highly educative yet conversational manner

surprised: I was surprised at the underlying process of writing and publishing a philosophy paper. Even though my internship was not focused on this work, I ended up seeing a lot of the behind-the-scenes that goes into philosophical writing, For example, the textual denseness of each draft involved hours of research and commentary back and forth between editors and contributors. Additionally, being able to write my own mini paper showed me how difficult [t was to narrow down on a specific question/topic while including enough nuance and analysis for the reader to be able to understand on their own.
takeaways: My biggest takeaway is the importance of having a mentor you're comfortable with, whether that be a teacher or professor, in learning in academia. Especially in a field as extensive and male dominated as philosophy, it was incredibly helpful to have my opinion and questions respected by someone as understanding and knowledgeable as Prof. Marshall.

My time with Dr. Berry provided me with hands-on experience in the pursuit of scientific research. Outies included wet-lab procedures with direct supervision, image analysis with ImageJ, worm maintenance, and scientific literature discussion. There are also opportunities to learn how different equipment is used such as the Worm80t and different types of microscopes. The internship is very adaptable depending on whatJs needed and everyone's availabilityv Unique experiences While I was there, my mentor submitted a paper to a very prestigious journal. The article was peer reviewed, and we discussed the comments and how to implement the edits. It was very interesting to see the real-time process of paper submission and review. gat to run my own experiments involving the movement of worms in a liquid medium and wrote a short report on the results.

location: Seattle University Physics Department/Remote Internship description This internship was similar to the sort of research you would do in a semester/year-long independent research class you would take in college. I worked with Dr. aoness to learn about the equations of state, exoplanets, and how data is collected for objects far off in space in my first couple months of the internship. After my initial few months, I was mostly reading a lot of papers on thermodynamics of Earth's core and other planets. I also learned to code in python and compiled data from various papers to create graphs on the Pressure-Temperature-volume relationships on certain compounds found on exoplanets. After transitioning to remote half-wav through the year, I was still able continue with the internship as I was before, This internship was a lot of reading published geophysics papers and coding. Unique experiences I enjoyed getting to work with a physics/chem[stry student at Seattle University. The internship could be what you wanted it to be, and it was very independent. If I wanted to go deeper into a certain topic, could do so. If I wanted to learn more about an overarching theme, I could do so as welln I did a "unit" on the exoplanet WASP-12b, and I was also able to research and read into the factors that indicate the possibility of life.

surprised: A lot of the research I did in my internships extended on past research and knowledge done in the field (e.g., hormone therapy use and lung cancer), so what surprised me the most was how never-ending the work is in science Once you find one discovery, there are bunch of other unanswered questions that are built off that.

I found it so interesting that just typing a couple of functions into the computer would be able to create graphs/variables that would provide insight into the health of a certain population. I was able to take my skills to lead to conclusions in the data that allowed me to understand the relationships of diseases to lifestyle/genomic factors.

My specific research focused on several species ofyeast, namely S paradoxus and S cerevisiae, and the variations seen in wild-type mutations versus lab-grown. Unique experiences Trained in "big data" genomic computation, addressing unique challenges of data size and cleaning to preprocess whole genomes Developed tools and packages {in both R and python) to create a pipeline to extract SNPs and graphicallyvisualize them Learned hawto create publication level posters and graphics as well as organize manuscripts Learned to perform several wet lab procedures from tetrad dissection and genetic transformation to DNA extraction and PCR

I am also surprised at the synthesis of lab data produced by us and published data by others to create a final cohesive project.
takeaways: The biggest takeaways I had were the ability to really apply computational skills to actual biological problems with a real-world impact and the synthesis ofthe traditional and the modern. Before my internship, I didn't see en actual application of computational biology ta fully address biological inquiries but through it, I have learned how biology end CS can be put together to solve previously unanswerable questions. organization: Elementary Particles Experiment Machine Learning Study Group
location: UW Internship description Professor Shih-Chieh Hsu leads many PhD and post-grad students in research in the fields of high energy physics and machine learning. Members of the EPE ML study group study the fundamentals of python programming and machine learning through tutorials designed by EPE as well as UW and NTHU courses. The group meets weekly to share progress and discuss coursework, and members of the group are expected to attend weekly EPE meetings to learn about the different research projects end eventually implement the ML knowledge by shadowing/participating in one of the research projects lead by PhO/postgrad students and supervised by Professor Hsu. Unique experiences Learning fundamentals in high energy physics end machine learning, basics of programming„ and experience in physics research. Most of the work is independent and remote. A lot of self-motivation is needed to get through the coursework and materials. Professor Hsu is super passionate about his research!!

Learning effective research strategies using the New York Time historical archives.

location: Home (virtual} Internship description Worked on machine learning projects related to natural language processing, which is my mentor's field of study. I began the school year by learning the basics of machine learning from resources my mentor provided, and then primarily worked on making a tweet sentiment classifier. starting with training simple models and working my way to tuning state-of-the-art models for the task. Unique experiences Gained understanding of how many machine learning algorithms and NLP techniques work Hands-on ML experience, from making a neural network from scratch to working with PyTarch and trying out BERT and RoBERTa, and learning a lat about training, improving, and fine-tuning models along the way
description: Worked on machine learning projects related to natural language processing, which is my mentor's field of study. I began the school year by learning the basics of machine learning from resources my mentor provided, and then primarily worked on making a tweet sentiment classifier. starting with training simple models and working my way to tuning state-of-the-art models for the task.
unique_experiences: Gained understanding of how many machine learning algorithms and NLP techniques work Hands-on ML experience, from making a neural network from scratch to working with PyTarch and trying out BERT and RoBERTa, and learning a lat about training, improving, and fine-tuning models along the way
surprised: Having had little prior research experience, I was pretty surprised by how much effort people have spent making cutting-edge ML tools accessible for a wide audience, including beginners like me.
takeaways: As my mentor told me at some point, you can't expect to read (say) 15 pages of a dense textbook or article in half an hour and expect to truly understand whet is going on. This is especially relevant for me since I tend to rush through material so I can use it as soon as possible. Additionally, being able to discuss concepts and work with a mentor is a big bonus beyond simply learning from textbooks; my mentor gave me a lot of advice/insights and resources that helped me learn and make progress much more efficiently and effectively than if I had tried doing it all myself, especially as a beginner. For example, instead of thinking something is feasible and spending two weeks to find out that it is not, my mentor could tell me thet and then give me advice on what to do instead. Also, you can probably ask your mentor about nearly anything relevant and they will be happy to talk about it.

takeaways: My biggest takeaway is understanding how research is conducted and communicated in the professional world. The specialization and teams of people who work together ta present the product to the head researcher, who then assembles all the data from different teams into a publication to present the findings to the world is a way more complication process then I originally thought. location: Remote Internship description I worked with a PHD student on their project (DISTILL DISTIL is an active learning toolkit that implements active learning strategies with a particular focus for active learning in the deep learning setting. It is built on PyTorch, and focuses on reducing labeling costs for enterprise and academic users. My contribution was in the tutorials side, where I would write models using the 01ST IL library. My experience was more about learning Al modelling and the math behind it, rather than making big changes in the frontier. Unique experiences Learn about the calculus and statistics behind Neural Networks, Active Learning, and derivative Al techniques Write models with PyTorch and with DISTIL on top of that.
description: I worked with a PHD student on their project (DISTILL DISTIL is an active learning toolkit that implements active learning strategies with a particular focus for active learning in the deep learning setting. unique_experiences: Learn about the calculus and statistics behind Neural Networks, Active Learning, and derivative Al techniques Write models with PyTorch and with DISTIL on top of that.
surprised: The people I worked with were chill and didn't expect me to be some sort of college student pouring my life force into the research. Just doing the work I was given, and when I struggled, showing what I tried, and asking why it failed was enough. They weren't asking me to make their product work, they were trying to make me learn, within the scope of their project. I was a student, not an important employee, and this meant I got to learn at my own pace, according to my interests (to an extent). I was surprised about how research prioritized learning and was understanding that I wasn't going to change the world in 9 months.
takeaways: Research is about a LOT of study, and then taking natural interests further by trying to do cool things. aut it's not lectures like school, it's a lot of self-studying, reading things written by important people in the field, and seeing the field progress around you. I think I will continue doing research-oriented internships during college, and I will consider it as a potential career option. 56

description: Being a research assistant under SIBL consisted of all the things any person ould do when conducting psychological research; literature reviews, reference checks, citation audits, cleaning up excel sheets, and my favorite, conducting participants in a study. I would also often pretend to be a participant in a study before it became officially tested, to see if there were any flaws. Depending on what field of study you decide to become a research assistant in, what you do may vary 4 rom what I did (mine was psychology research). However, most of the time it was looking up articles o back up claims made in research papers.
unique_experiences: My very first day my mentor gave me multiple excel files and asked that I sort them out. I wanted to prove myself worth more than just excel sheets, so I found online excel trategies to speed through the files and I was rewarded by being able to work on more challenging hings quickly afterwards. Someone my age who started the same time as me was still doing excel heets for the first two months while I had already ran a study. (Speed through the easy work!)
surprised: How understanding people are. Working underneath a college professor, it's a little daunting and you're sometimes stressed that you'll mess things up. But as long as you're ood at communicating that you don't know something, and you're trying your best most of the time, most people will be very kind and accommodating, even if you're just a high schooler. I messed up a reference check a couple of times and the people were super understanding and let me redo what I messed up instead of firing me.
takeaways: internship (research assistant position) with a cold email to the professor who ran the lab. I asked her uestions about my theory of research, and I simply asked if I could be a part of her lab. It's a little cary because we are all just high school students, but if you put in the work and you show the etermination, I'm sure many college professors might actually prefer you over some college tudents.

location: Bothell Internship description Evergreen Avian and Exotic Animal Hospital (EAEAI-I} provides veterinary services for exotic species of pets. I was able to observe surgeries and examinations, ask questions about veterinary science, and experience the inner workings of a medical clinic. surprised: I was surprised by haw much detail goes into examining the pets. From spraying the examination room after each visit to writing detailed notes about almost every aspect of the pets' lives, veterinary science can be labor intensive in many ways.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is that it takes more than just talented and knowledgeable veterinarians to run a vet clinic. Receptionists are needed to keep track of paperwork and guide patients and assistants are needed to make medications and do bloodwork. Veterinarians are not the sole employees of the clinic end there are many other jobs that need to be done.

location: Online/Renton Internship description Pasado's Safe Haven is a non-profit focusing on many different aspects of animal welfare, including running an animal sanctuary and providing services to low-income pet owners. The pet services department, where I interned at, provides dog and cat spay/neuter surgeries and pet food for low-income owners. I helped answer client emails and voicemails asking for assistance or wanting to learn more about the services Pasado's provides. I also helped work on projects my mentor gave to me, such as researching other low-cost pay/neuter clinics in the area and writing manuals for the different spay/neuter clinic stations. I also went once a month to help out at the Renton spay/neuter clinic and visited the sanctuary a few times. My mentor as also very flexible about when I could complete my work. Unique experiences Working at a spay/neuter clinic, including preparing cats for surgery and post-op monitoring Learning how to use ClinicHQ Answering client emails and questions about what services Pasado's offers

description: The International Rescue Committee is a refugee resettlement agency which works to help those who have been affected by international humanitarian crisis to survive, recover, and rebuild their lives in the United States. The IRC does this through supporting refugees and victims of human trafficking through education, economic empowerment, health, safety, advocacy, and supporting women and girls. During my time at the IRC, I worked as a development intern with the goal of supporting the IRC in building community and partner connections, so that the IRC can best serve Washington's refugee population.
unique_experiences: • Planning events for refugee youth to shere their staries and poetry • Helping start and run an IRC Family Mentorship Program • Develop outreach materials and community connections with stakeholders
surprised: I was surprised to learn about haw such a large international NGO could also be so connected and mated within the community here in Seattle!
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship was to never be afraid to speak up if I have an ideal In the beginning I was intimidated and nervous to speak in meetings because I was constantly afraid of not knowing enough about the IRC and about refugee resettlement, but I quickly learned how openminded and welcoming my colleagues were, and they helped me put ideas such as photography projects, a partnership with Barnes and Nobles, and writing empowering articles about IRC clients and volunteers into fruition ! Mentor's name Sharon Tsoi Mentor's email • Sharon.Tsoi@rescue.org

organization: University of Washington Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning group
location: UW Seattle Internship description Or. Shih gmsDHsu leads a group of UW PhD and postgrad students in researching machine learning applications to particle physics. Interns first learn the basics of python and machine learning programming over the course of several months. Afterwards they transition into choosing a project they are interested in and working closely together with other postgraduate students over the course of the year. Progress updates and suggestions are given at an in-person meeting once a week. Unique experiences First, I studied lab assignments from a PHYS427 machine learning course offered at UW to learn the basics of machine learning. Then, I began working together with a PhD student to improve the performance of a conditional variational autoencoder network to detect anomalous particle events. At the end of the year, we all create a poster and present our results to one another.

organization: Elementary Particles Experiment Machine Learning Study Group
location: UW Internship description Professor Shih-Chieh Hsu leads many PhD and postgrad students in research in the applications of machine learning to high energy physics. Interns of the EPE ML Study Group first learn the fundamentals of Python programming, Pytorch, and machine learning through the UW PHYS 427 labs designed by EPE. They are expected to collaborate and share progress during weekly meetings with Professor Hsu. Additionally, interns are expected to learn about the different research projects of the EPE group and eventually join one of the projects to apply ML knowledge. They will then work closely with the PhD/postgrad student leading the project and share findings with Professor Hsu and fellow interns in weekly meetings. Unique experiences • Learning about current research in high energy physics and machine learning • So many different projects to choose from —calorimetry clustering, gravitational waves, etc. o Professor Hsu wants you to choose a project you're truly passionate in, so you can always switch if something else interests you • Mostly remote but weekly meetings are at UW surprised you most? I went into this internship with no ML knowledge and limited coding experience but became proficient enough to understand ongoing research • Professor Hsu is very open to students who have limited experience in ML or particle physics. He gives you the necessary resources and time to ensure you have enough knowledge before joining a research project • The research Professor Hsu is supervising is all really interesting, and it got me inspired to pursue ML is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience? Research takes a lot of self-motivation and rigor but as long as you're interested, it is exciting and rewarding. our mentor is there to support you, so never be afraid to ask questions or for help.

location: University of Washington Internship description e Elementary Particle Experiment Machine Learning Study Group is lead by Professor Shih-Chieh Hsu and is composed of PhD and graduate students, to use machine learning in particle physics research. These topics include anomaly detection, quick calorimetry data generation, and gravitational wave detection. Interns initially go through a basic machine learning course at UW, and later shadow a grad student mentor on their research topic. Personally, I investigated calorimetry data generation and jet tagging, comparing different models to complete each task. Unique experiences Learn about different current research topics and directions in particle physics, and how machine learning is impacting each topic. You're able to interact with graduate students and talk to them about their research. The speed of which you progress through learning your topic is very much up to you! Lots of the work is quite accessible / your own pace and can be done asynchronously. surprised you most? Using machine learning in application is quite accessible, even without any prior knowledge on the subject or of programming. There is also no need for a strong understanding of particle physics going into the internship either. The most important thing needed was the commitment to consistently spend time on the research. is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience? One of the most important things to do is communicate often with the people you are working with and make yourself an environment where you know what you should be doing. If a task is too difficult for you and ou don't know what to do, tell that to the person you are working with, because they likely do not know at.

organization: University of Washington Center for Experimental Nuclear Physics and Astrophysics
location: University of Washington Internship description ENPA (Center for Experimental Nuclear Physics and Astrophysics) is a research facility located and ponsored by the University of Washington. As its name implies, multiple projects take place involving nuclear physics and astrophysics, such as the g-2, PIONEER experiments (subatomic particles), and ADMX (dark matter). During my time at CENPA I was part of the PIONEER project, where I was able to develop data nalysis tools from the ground up and develop tools for the particle simulation that the project utilizes. Unique experiences Many opportunities for individual creativity regarding software and what tasks to do, such as c++ simulation work and python data analysis work. Being able to build useful tools for the project which are used by the scientists. Ability to learn from and talk to knowledgeable scientists about particle physics.

location: CENPA new physics lab, I.JW, Seattle Internship description International particle physics research! Working with PhD and postdoc scientist mentors from around the world, you learn and code physics simulations to directly contribute to an internationally recognized particle physics experiment named PIONEER—-a pian decay experiment that tests for new physics beyond the Standard Model. This ranges from coding C++ particle collision simulations, to making Bash scripts on Linux computers, to performing Python data analysis and visualization. If you're good, you get to present your results to award-winning professors at the CENPA lab; you might even get to write a paper! Unique experiences • Generated, graphed, and analyzed terabytes of physics data to fix major s•mulation errors Installed an RTX 3070 GPIJ and then programmed it to parallelize optical photon simulation • Celebrated e successful physics presentation to Professor Hertzog with cake and donuts

organization: University of Washington Electrical and Computer Engineering Department
location: University of Washington Seattle Internship description I worked with a professor from the University of Washington's Electrical and Computer Engineering Department throughout the summer and the school year. As a part of the internship, I took a summer quarter quantum mechanics class (graduate student level). Using this knowledge, I worked with my professor to write and edit the math of the textbook that he plans to publish in the near future. During the school year, I authored a solution manual independently. Unique experiences Basic to intermediate quantum physics/computing understanding Taking an actual course at UW with graduate students (for free!) Having the trust and guidance of an expert in the field, who provided great support systems for growth and learning

location: Seattle Internship description I worked on analyzing ML models for weather forecasting. I utilized existing ML models in order to conduct research regarding patterns in forecasts, how perturbations in the model affect forecasts, and more. I compared these results to other ML models to learn more about these new tools in the field of atmospheric cience. Unique experiences Attend weekly group meetings with graduate and post-doc students Work one-on-one with my mentor on an ML project related to Atmospheric Science Present my work to the group at the end of the year at surprised you most? How collaborative research is. I was not expecting the whole process to be such a team effort. Although there is a lot of individual effort required, the process involved a lot of input from my professor who helped me come up with new angles to solve problems and new ideas on tests to run. at is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience? How curious and open to new ideas everyone is in research. It seems like almost a prerequisite to conducting research. All of my research group members, as well as my mentor, were always very interested in each new result and paper. This approach to research and to science is what I will take away from my internship.

location: Electrical and Comp Eng department Intemship Description Learned Machine Learning and Data Mining algorithms Worked on multiple projects related to collecting data and using different models to predict certain outcomes My mentor is passionate about sports, including cricket. location: University of Washington Internship description Learn to create a machine learning model for predicting the age of fruit flies based upon certain walking parameters such as the step and swing length of a fly's step based off of a LEAP (Social Leap Estimates Animal Poses) model. Involved a lot of data pruning, with basic techniques like box cox normalization and KNN imputation, along with basic model pes such as ridge, lasso, or elastic net models. Unique experiences ince I was free to do whatever I wanted with the data with just some suggestions from my mentor, I was free to just try out whatever I wanted, which included stealing what other interns learned at their internships and trying to apply it to my own data.

location: University of Washington Internship description I have a human computer interaction research internship at the University of Washington. I am part of a team f researchers under Prof. Gary Hsieh. We are currently working a project that compiles various studies on a cientific phenomenon, much of the time related to a current event or viral topic. This information, combined ith information gathered when we conduct our own studies, is used to create a meta summary where all he data is summarized and laid out in an organized fashion. My internship requires some data analysis skills s well as web development skills. Unique experiences s a computer scientist who has experience with only the more technical aspects of the field, it was a change f pace for me to enter the field of human computer interaction where psychology becomes a prominent actor as well as technical skills. I found that the experimental and research process for HCl is quite omplicated and sometimes subjective which I found to be interesting.

organization: University of Washington Industrial Assessment Center
location: University of Washington/Online remote Internship description ponsored by the US Department of Energy, the Industrial Assessment Center Program provides no-cost assessments to manufacturing companies nationwide, recommending budget-friendly solutions to prepare hem to lead the global clean energy transition. At the University of Washington's local IAC, we provide such ervices for companies in Washington State as our contribution to this great endeavor. During my time at IAC, I learned and worked with UW students and professors to sort and analyze assessment data, research ways o improve energy efficiency, and compile detailed reports for our client businesses. With only one weekly meeting, the schedule is very relaxing, and I often find myself losing track of time in my work! Unique experiences Researching advanced ways to save energy on a mass scale Writing professional and detailed assessment reports Touring industrial facilities with industry professionals surprised you most? I was surprised by the sheer efficiency of the IAC team in conducting facility tours, analyzing data, and presenting recommendations. The whole process is very streamlined and every recommendation is saved as a template to be reused for similar situations. is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience?

location: Seattle Internship description Seattle University's Environmental Engineering department has a wide variety of research projects focused on improving the sustainability of our way of life and reducing the environmental impact of Seattle University within the local community. Unique experiences Learning how to use various lab equipment such as HPLCs and solar simulators Using DPD and UV-Vis Spectrometers to identify concentrations of chlorine within various solutions Building and maintaining webpages on the Seattle University website
