description: Dr. Ebi specializes in the intersection of climate change and human health. As a renowned researcher in her field, Dr. Ebi does work both teaching at UW and as a researcher. From having more than 200 publications to testifying in Congress, Dr. Ebi is incredibly knowledgeable and is always ready o answer any questions. Dr. Ebi was also very accommodating to my schedule and was very focused on nsuring that I was interested in whatever I was working on at the time.
unique_experiences: Help prepare lessons and lesson plans for professor's lecture-style classes Exposure to and experience in researching various topics within the intersection between climate change and human health Assist in research for academic paper through reviewing and compiling already existing material
surprised: I was surprised at the freedom and flexibility Dr. Ebi was able to have as both a mentor, professor, and researcher. Dr. Ebi was able to pick what topic she wanted to work on and when, which also provided me the ability to do research in a wide range of topics from climate change conferences to health-related loss and damages.
takeaways: his was my first experience within the educational/teaching and research space. Education and research are enerally not the most glamorous work out there, but can be incredibly rewarding once the work is complete.

organization: University of Washington Mechanical Engineering Department - Human Photonics Lab
location: Seattle Internship description The Human Photonics Lab (HPL) at UW's Mechanical Engineering Department aims to create a space for aspiring engineers and scientists to pursue a research project they are passionate about and make a difference in real-world application with a hyper focus on biotechnology in health. While I was at the HPL, I was able to take part in my own project, "VAP Buster", that aims to reduce morbidity rates for patients with ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in the ICIJ. Along with my own project, I was able to observe other undergrad/graduate student projects and take part in weekly seminars where lab members would present their current research projects and findings, displaying many different research pathways and interests. Unique experiences Gave a 45-minute seminar at one of the weekly HPL Group meetings regarding my "VAP Buster" project and my additional research. Shadowed lab experiments. Drafted final report for the "VAP Buster" project to submit to the National Science Foundation (NSF).

surprised: I was quite surprised to see the sheer amount of time and effort required for research and how many rabbit hole's you can go down without actually making any progress. Research is anything but a linear process, it takes a lot of failures before you can have one success.
takeaways: This was my first experience working with science and engineering at the collegiate level and actually learning how knowledge can be applied to real-world application. I learned about the proper methodology and processes of conducting a research project along with all the non-scientific factors that come into play like grants, resource availability, and regulatory and compliance clearances. Additionally, being exposed to so many diverse projects and people, I was able to expand my knowledge base and really get a sense of what a career in engineering could entail.

location: Seattle (South Lake Union) Internship description I interned at Bai Lab, a neuroscience lab that works primarily with C. elegans (worms) but also does a lot of microbiology. At Fred Hutch, I learned to construct and transform various DNA fragments into ectors/constructs by growing them in stable bacterial cells, perform microinjections of the worms, and assist on various projects around the lab (for example, one student there was working on researching distractibility, and there was a mutagensis paper being floated around that I helped on a bit). Unique experiences Dr. Bai's lab is pretty large, so there is almost always work to be done and nobody wants you wasting your time Because the lab takes a lot of interns/students, they have a lot of experience with people starting out in labs Chance to help work on new neuroscience-related projects

surprised: Most things aren't as precise as yau think—they work in a range, so it's 0k if you mess up a little! Scientists will do whatever they can to save money and love excuses to throw a party
takeaways: Really important to develop your basic skills early, and that only comes with practice Practically speaking, science is a lot of menial, repetitive motions that aren't too difficult—the theory is the interesting part

description: I worked under Dr. Smith in the Smith Lab, researching how the presence of the RecBCD enzyme in E. coli affects the growth of bacteriophages and their ability to infect bacteria. My time at my internship mostly consisted of wet lab work, conducting experiments on, and comparing bacteriophages of interest.
unique_experiences: Practiced wet lab techniques — Burst size analysis experiments, lysogeny tests, PCR/geI electrophoresis, and culturing bacteria and phage Used tools like BLAST, Sequencher, and Snapgene to analyze bacteriophage genomes and to design primers for PCR Discussed scientific papers and reviews with mentor and other lab members •
surprised: I was most surprised by the amount of freedom I had in deciding which experiments to execute, designing procedures, and deciding my own daily tasks. I had a lot of discussions with my mentor where he offered me uidance but ultimately, I got to decide what to do to proceed with my project.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship experience is that what you get out of your internship depends on hat you put into it. You need to ask questions and take initiative to be able to get the most out of the experience and to be able to try/learn as many different skills as possible.

organization: Srivastava Lab, Fred Hutch Cancer Center
location: Fred Hutch Cancer Center Steam Plant Internship description Shadowed/assisted grad students and lab technicians. Learned basic lab skills like plasmid miniprep and DNA gel extraction, as well as basic analysis skills using FlowJo (flow cytometry) and Prism (graphing). Conducted personal experiments (concerning equipment efficacy) and presented on them at the end of the year at the weekly lab meeting. Unique experiences Was given multiple opportunities to research, plan, and execute personal experiments. Attended weekly joint lab meetings with nearby labs for presentations and "data club" concerning recent experiments. Taught basic to complex lab procedures and immunology science by graduate students and lab technicians.

location: South Lake Union (in Seattle) Internship description is internship is a hybrid between wet lab and biostatistical analysis — the lab focuses on virology and how specific knock-out genes can influence the proliferation of viruses (mainly retro and lenti). We also try to understand how the structure/phylogenetic history of ancient viruses have on our current genomes through biostatistical analysis. Unique experiences • Constructing independent gene cloning projects • Filing through millions of NCBI database accession numbers and conducting data validation • Handling a sample of ancient black lung! t surprised you most? Being a relatively new lab (and consequentially, small), there are limited wet-lab opportunities in the first few months. I was tasked with computer work since I was proficient in biostatistical program prior to the internship, but the wet lab opportunities had to be built up and you had to have the initiative to ask questions. t is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience? Research is a different beast than the typical "STEBR' classes — the hours of troubleshooting, praying for results, is definitely worth considering before you go into research as a career. There is a lot of self-guidance nd isolation during lab days, but the happiness of getting adequate results is worth the hours of struggle. It requires a lot of time commitment: you need to accommodate around incubation times, 3-day protocols, and PCR run times. You're able to witness the wonders of scientific discovery, but there's a lot of niche rules and protocols that are necessary to be successful.

organization: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Public Health Sciences Division
location: Fred Hutch /Home Internship description e Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center is a group of scientists that work together to eliminate cancer and diseases that cause human death. The Jones lab specifically researches evidence-based practice in clinical are and the financial hardship of people with breast cancer and other types of cancer. At the Jones Lab, the role of the intern is to assist with ongoing trials and investigations. Unique experiences • Program surveys using Qualtrics, REDCAP, and Pilot Study • Analyze survey data from hundreds of cancer patients • Writing experience for academic journals • Complete literature reviews using PUBMED and Google Scholar • Create cost and time measures surprised you most? I was surprised to see how much time and effort goes into survey development. When working with Dr. ones, I saw that it took multiple rounds of reviewing survey questions for documents to be approved and ent out. It was also really exciting to see the process of submitting grants and other deadlines. is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience?

location: University of Washington Health Science Building D-514, Seattle, WA 98195 Internship description The Kaeberlein Lab studies aging at the molecular level. My specific group works to identfy genes linked to Alzheimer's disease by using RNA interference gene knockdown in C. elegans worms. Learned how to use R in order to analyze data Dry lab skills include graphing worm paralysis rates, lifespan scoring, and maintaining a spreadsheet of data for tested genes. Also required to complete quarterly presentations on a related molecular biology topic. Unique experiences Learning R was quite a new and interesting experience for me. Although R starts out very basic there are data packages that the user can download and use the new feature in order to address the problems at hand.

organization: University of Washington, Dept. of Medicine and Pathology
location: Remote Internship description Under Elena Vayndorfs mentorship at the UW Dept. of Medicine and Pathology, we researched the effect of knocking dawn or blocking gene pathways, and the effect ofvarious drugs/chemicals on the progression of neurodegenerative diseases using a C. Elegans model. As a high school intern, I primarily recorded, processed, and analyzed data, and presented such data in the form of graphs and statistics to the mentor and a team of interns. Unique experiences Saw how certain drugs and genes could be used to slow the progression of neurodegenerative diseases, such as with the Alzheimeds Disease gene. Toured the UW labs and Health Sciences building. In addition to working with the mentor, also had opportunities to work with current undergraduate students for further mentorship. This allowed for learning about the internship, experiences as a biology student at the LIV and delving deeper into the biological processes behind these experiments.
description: Under Elena Vayndorfs mentorship at the LIW Dept. of Medicine and Pathology, we researched the effect of knocking dawn or blocking gene pathways, and the effect ofvarious drugs/chemicals on the progression of neurodegenerative diseases using a C. Elegans model. As a high school intern, I primarily recorded, processed, and analyzed data, and presented such data in the form of graphs and statistics to the mentor and a team of interns.

This allowed for learning about the internship, experiences as a biology student at the LIV and delving deeper into the biological processes behind these experiments.
surprised: I was most surprised by the length of the research process. The sheer number oftrials that must be conducted, and the number ofvariables that can be adjusted for a different outcome were astonishing to see. It put into context how meticulous and delicate this process is. After these pre-clinical trials, clinical trials must also take place. This is why research for medicines tend to take quite e long time.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is the importance of frequent communication of updates in research. Initially, I thought that most research work is done individually until presenting findings, when actually, teamwork throughout the whole project is necessary for efficient and high-quality researche Frequent updates keep the teem on track and allow for possible issues to be caught and resolved earlier in the process.

organization: University of Washington Department of Global Health
location: The University of Washington Hans Rosling Center for Population Health Internship description he University of Washington Department of Global Health (UW DGH) focuses on addressing world health isparities through a multidisciplinary lens, collaborating with over 145 countries and countless professionals orldwide. As an intern for the DGH, I had the opportunity to work closely with a professor and their research team on research and course development. My time as an intern has been extremely valuable for both my academic and personal growth, and I felt that my mentor intentionally exposed me to many ifferent areas of academia, enriching my internship experience. Unique experiences Participate in all stages of global cardiovascular health research — conducted literature reviews, interpreted results, drafted introduction and discussion sections, collected and quality checked data, submitted manuscripts Draft grant applications Network with and learn from global health professionals at local research conferences Develop a graduate level-course, creating slides and contributing to the syllabus •

unique_experiences: Participate in all stages of global cardiovascular health research — conducted literature reviews, interpreted results, drafted introduction and discussion sections, collected and quality checked data, submitted manuscripts Draft grant applications Network with and learn from global health professionals at local research conferences Develop a graduate level-course, creating slides and contributing to the syllabus •
surprised: he dedication and length of time required for the research process. From an idea, to a full manuscript, to a publication, I witnessed firsthand and was inspired by the passion and rigor required to create a research paper. I was also surprised by the openness and adaptability of global health professionals — they come from II sorts of backgrounds and varying levels of expertise, yet are always willing to answer my questions or listen to my (or anyone else's!) thoughts.
takeaways: hile the hard skills I've learned from my internship are undoubtedly important, as is learning about the research process, my greatest takeaway from this internship experience is a broadened worldview and onnections with people from around the world. Learning about diseases of poverty, social determinants, and he lives of medical personnel in low- and middle-income countries has allowed me to gain a greater understanding of the world in which we live in.

organization: The Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture (Paleontology Department)
description: To familiarize myself with fossil handling, museum collections, and anatomy, the first few months were dedicated to a fossil digitization project photographing a collection of choice: the Burke's entire collection of Bridger formation primates. Then, I joined (and am still working with) graduate student Elliott AgmpycSmith on a microfossil project processing—then identifying fossils among—sediments from the formation at the Petrified Forest National Park. There currently isn't an established internship program at the Burke Museum's Paleontology Department, but I was able to create an internship position with the generous help of Curator and Professor Christian and Collections Manager Katherine Anderson (plus major thanks to Mr. Allen). However, I've heard that there are ongoing efforts to establish an internship position for high school students, so by next fall there may be internship positions for anyone interested in paleo.
unique_experiences: I got to work with amazing and super intelligent and helpful researchers who have just never lost their childhood love for fossils. I got to get a glimpse of the "boring," not-digging-up-fossils part of paleontology, which turns out is still super cool (like being able to access almost the fossils in the Burke's ginormous collection) I got to contribute to Burke's online database and to an ongoing research project at the edge of paleo research.
surprised: I was surprised to learn that there is some sort of hierarchy with the type of bone one picks up: the most useless bones are rib bones, and the most useful bones are mandibles and teeth. This is because rib bones almost always have no distinguishing features, while mandibles and teeth offer much more information on the specimen, like which species it came from. So, I'm super hyped to find some mandibles as I work with Elliott!
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is the skills I've developed working at Burke. Designing and streamlining experimental processes, communicating and collaborating with other researchers, and isolating and recording good data are some of the skills I see myself using going into college and beyond.

location: Online/Bothell Internship description Dan Berger is an Associate Dean for Faculty Development and Scholarship. His research focuses on the history of incarceration in Washington and more broadly the US. Dr. Berger was flexible with the internship and gave me multiple projects to work on (e.g., transcription; archival reading, and readings). I spent most of my time exploring my personal interests in history and critical theory with biweekly conversations with Dr. Berger. Unique experiences Insight into academic work e.g., the day-to-day work, journal article v. book publishing and peer review, the job market. • One-on-one conversations about critical theory e.g., abolition, Afro-pessimism, black Marxism, critical fabulation, genealogy, etc. Flexibility to explore personal research interests. at surprised you most? Dr: Berger's last book and upcoming project are both written for the public (as opposed to niche academic audiences). I was surprised to learn the difference in scale between academic publishing and commercial publishing, as well as how academic work could be directed toward the commercial press. at is your greatest takeaway(s) from the internship experience? e importance of reading and taking notes. Notes don't have to be perfectly organized nor compiled in one place but writing physical notes really does increase retention and comprehension of readings.

location: Seattle Internship description attle Children's houses a wide variety of labs ranging from molecular biology to tech research. I work in the attle Children's Innovative Technologies Lab, which researches ways to apply tech in autism research. As an intern, I cleaned and entered data from past studies, wrote literature reviews and compiled publications with my mentor, and worked on side projects relevant to behavioral and tech research. Seattle Children's typically only hires UW students, so as a high schooler I never had access to the system or PHI but was still able to help in other ways. Unique experiences Writing a literature review on applications of eye tracking in autism research Helping to research and compile sources for a book on eye tracking as a potential psychiatric biomarker Working on Al generated images for an equity in healthcare careers comic book surprised you most? I was surprised by the amount of coordination and time that goes into a successful study, we were still processing data from studies completed 8 years ago and working to publish any findings. t is your greatest takeaway(s) from the internship experience?

location: University of Washington, Seattle Internship description rving as lab assistant in UW Materials Science & Engineering laboratory. Assisting in two different research projects Armored fish nanoindentation for the development of stronger plastic materials and Radio requency waves to eradicate invasive burrowing shrimp species. Completing day-to-day tasks within the lab, including data collection and reading scientific literature. Unique experiences Being able to work in a university laboratory is a completely different experience from a high school science lassroom. I have gotten to use machines like a Profilometer and helped with the making of polymers. t surprised you most? e lab setting is very relaxed and supportive. While many work within the lab, only a few are working on the ame projects as me, yet people from different projects offer advice and suggestions all the time. Many labs in the UW engineering department share spaces, and the professors are very welcoming and collaborative. t is your greatest takeaway(s) from the intemship experience? e research process may seem daunting at first, but there are so many opportunities to get involved in a meaningful topic. The RF wave burrowing shrimp research project is helping shellfish farmers in the Puget und, which is very relevant to our community. The positive experience I had within the research setting is a big reason why I chose to study chemical engineering in college, and I look forward to joining other research projects in the future.

location: Online (remote internship) Internship description As an intern for Ms. Okwandu, I researched the relationship between race and postpartum mental illnesses in mothers. My work primarily entailed finding and analyzing various primary and secondary historical sources about women with mental illnesses thet surfaced during pregnancy or childbirth. In our meetings, Ms. Okwendu and I discussed numerous factors that affected the shifting societal treatment of women with mental illnesses. My work, paired with learnings fram our discussions about the history of patient mental diagnoses, will be used in Ms. Okwandu's PhO dissertatiom Unique experiences Accessing new, larger source databases Collaborating with professionals in a fairly obscure field Creating organized models and detailed briefs ta share research findings

unique_experiences: Accessing new, larger source databases Collaborating with professionals in a fairly obscure field Creating organized models and detailed briefs ta share research findings
surprised: I was most surprised by how Ms. Okwandu's seemingly-niche dissertation tapic (i.e., the historical effects af race on maternal mental illness) actually has many tangible real-world applications. I've learned that studying both the history of racial bias in healthcare and the evolution of attitudes towards young women's mental health is important naw more than ever.
takeaways: Persistence is key! I wouldn't have been able to secure this internship if I hadn't emailed Ms. akwandu multiple times expressing my interest in her work. Ms. Okwandu also taught me that in research, it's important to be persistent—struggling through dense sources to fish out small, important pieces of evidence is just part of the process.

category: Art, Design, Architecture and Technical Productions
description: Public secondary school in Bellevue School District enrolling 1588 students in grades nine through twelve Programs offered include International Baccalaureate, Advanced Learning, PACIFIC and Evergreen Transition Program. As an Art Teacher Assistant Intern, my responsibilities included promoting the rts, mentoring students across four art classes each semester, studio management, cataloguing inventory, nd ceramics studio operations.
unique_experiences: Promoting the arts and increasing student involvement by giving announcements and initiating award programs Learning the inner workings of a ceramics studio, gaining experience with glazing, clay recycling and operating kilns Interacting with other art students across disciplines, including Stained Glass, Metals and Jewelry Making, and Ceramics
surprised: hat surprised me was the amount of work that is done by art teachers behind the scenes, whether it is utting paper and copperto prep for projects, maintaining the classroom space, or loading and unloading kilns,
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship is gaining experience with multiple artistic mediums, including orking with glass, metal, ceramics, screen printing, and ink. The hand-on experiences and studio work were helpful in creating my college art portfolio and will prepare me for majoring in Fine Arts in college. MentorJs Name : Amy Weber Cantrell Mentor's email: webera@bsd405.org

category: Art, Design, Architecture and Technical Productions
description: Kirkland Arts Center is non-profit organization that runs galleries as well as offering a variety of art classes in 2D art, 3D art, printmaking, ceramics, and more. My role at KAC involved planning and designing both physical and digital posters. I also did a lot of hands-on work in the gallery which allowed me to meet and work with local artists, become more integrated with the community, and understand the fundamentals of running an art gallery.
unique_experiences: Elevated my awareness regarding the importance of community-building and collaboration in e non-profit environment. Built a portfolio of digital graphic design work. Worked to schedule meetings and arrange drop-off times with artists.
surprised: I was the most surprised by the significance of community in every aspect of Kirkland Arts Center. Everyone has such a strong sense of community and camaraderie which allows them to look out for each other, allowing each community member the opportunity to grow and hone their skills in the process.
takeaways: (s) My greatest takeaway was the importance of creative thinking and problem solving in a work environment. Especially in a non-profit organization, creative solutions must be utilized to maximize resource efficiency and reduce possible expenses.

location: UW Seattle Internship description Medical imaging in its current form is expensive, inefficient, and inaccessible. We have worked on a methodology to stitch images from the OpenMV H7 camera such that in-depth LSCI imaging can be overlaid with the area of the body being captured. This will enable more efficient imaging of subcutaneous venous structures and injuries, providing a hand-held, quick alternative to traditional medical imaging. We focus on the panoramic stitching of the images captured by our handheld camera, or putting segments of separately captured images together to accurately capture a larger area. Namely, we've focused on the preservation of sufficient detail and accuracy in the final image so that it may be useful for medical applications. Unique experiences Working through unknown code and writing documentation Learning about ML applications in image processing Creating and tinkering with algorithms and workflows for tangible outcomes

Among the types of algorithms I used were SVMs (Support Vector Machines), CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks) and KNN (K-Nearest Neighbor) algorithms. I programmed in Python, using machine learning libraries like Tensorflow, Keres, and scikit-learne The internship is very open-ended; there isn't a clear solution or one "right" wayto approach the problem.
unique_experiences: The wherewithal to discern visually and aesthetically pleasing pictures of clouds, perchance Training cutting-edge machine learning models and tailoring them to specific problems Doing interdisciplinary research between Computer Science and Environmental Science
surprised: How much open-source material exists online to be used for whatever you want— for almost any problem I encountered there was some useful information online.
takeaways: My greatesttakeawav was learning haw to approach a problem with no clear solution. I learned how to adapt when faced with an unexpected challenge, howto be resourceful, and how to think about something from muftiple angles.

location: Bellevue City Hall Internship description The Bellevue Department of Transportation is what makes our city move. Bus lines, light rail, and road repairs may seem like very/' mundane things, but their impact over time is immeasurable. The simple addition of having a light rail stop could mean the difference between a neighborhood turning •nto a bustling suburb or becoming a ghost town. Transportation decisions have a huge impact an whether a community thrives or dies. Furthermore, it is extremely difficult to know where, when, and how transportation projects are needed, resulting in many communities being neglectedv My internship is to help ensure that every community thrives, through increasing pu blic involvement with discriminated people. One of the main ways the transportation department plans its projects is through feedback from the public. But many people cannot give feedback, due to language barriers, time restrictions. distrust in government. and countless other reasons. Without feedback from those people, their communities are left behind. By researching newways to reach out to those discriminated groups, transportation projects can be distributed more equitably. Unique experiences I had many unique experiences by meeting other people. For example, meeting councilman Conrad Lee. I also got to know the other kids who intern for Kristi, who are from Big Picture. They are all great people who can provide you with even more connections.

unique_experiences: • Creating filings for use in courts at various levels, and seeing the immediate impact of those filings as opposing counsel make attempts to resolve them. • Contacting clients from various backgrounds to gain an understanding of their perspective on how a particular event unfolded. • Research case law using professional tools (Thomson Reuters Westlaw, LexisNexis), preparing summaries and developing strategies based on precedent from various sources,
surprised: I was particularly surprised by the complexity of filing a case, even one as othemvise trivial as a neighbor dispute or patently obvious types of fraud. In addition, I was also pleasantly surprised at the amount of autonomy and creativity I was able to express in this internship, since I was able to freely contribute to the cases I was working on and discuss with staff attorneys about the progress we were making and strategies we planned on adopting.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship experience is the ability to "think like a lawyer" and to analyze social and scientific issues in the context of the case law and statutes that have led us to where we are today. I've also found that my ability to communicate has improved dramatically since I've begun to write for various audiences; in some cases, the target audience is the junt or a citizen witness, where it isn't as appropriate to load up on complex jargon. In others, you're writing to judges or other attorneys that are well in-the-loop about the latest developments, and some lawspeak is acceptable. Being clear about who you're talkingto and changing your approach accordingly is an important skill and one I'm pleased to say I've improved greatly overthe course of this internship.

location: Fred Hutch Weintraub Building Internship description Investigated the role of the organelle vault in cancer cells in an MVP-KO mouse model, seeking to inform the development of better cancer therapies. It's interesting how we know so little about so a ubiquitous organelle. I was mentored by two people in the UW's prestigious MD/PhO program. I got to do e lot of lab techniques I learned in the past (e.g., PCR, gel electrophoresis) in e real lab setting. The lab I work at is very interdisciplinary. surprised: What surprised me most was how chill everyone is. Coming into a lab with such talented researchers, I was expecting a very hardcore environment. However, everyone was very awesome and helpful!
takeaways: Science is extremely collaborative. During my internship, I met and talked to researchers with a • huge diversity of specialties- from computational biologists to hematologists. f'Doing science" is a long process. This makes obtaining good data or publishing a paper extremely rewarding. Dr. James Waston is wrong.... RNA is objectively more interesting than DNA in that RNA is more flexible (hence can take on mam/ more shapes/forms) and has catalytic properties.

location: Weintraub Building, Seattle Internship description This internship at the Fred Hutch Smith Lab aimed at finding phages impacted bv the E. Coli as well as the ONA sequences within then that result in this affect. The reason is to investigate the widely accepted theory that Rec8CO kills foreign DNA, meaning that phages are killed by this enzyme. The goal was to find phages that grew with or without the enzyme to find similarities in genomes of phages that acted the same way. This internship involved finding phages through sample collection, DNA sequence analysis, and agar plating. The plating involved various techniques such as making filtrates, streaking, picketing, and adsorption to allow for optimal results. Through this internship, Dr. Gerry Smith taught us valuable skills on plating techniques as well as analyzing our results to plan experiments to test our findings. Unique experiences Working with the Woodland Park Zoo and reaching out to various researchers for phages to test. Reviewing DNA sequences of phages and learning to utilize technology such SnapGene. Helping compose a paper on leb sample collection findings from us end previous interns.

takeaways: My greatest takeaway from the internship experience is to record everything, whether or not it made sense in the moment or seemed important. description: Biasly is a platform that offers analysis of news articles, politicians, news outlets, and more. It features political bias ratings and accuracy ratings based on the factuality of the information presented and whether or not the author inserted their opinion into the articles. During my internship, I contributed by writing articles on political bias, and providing analyst ratings to enhance Al-generated ratings.
unique_experiences: Researched and writing: authored articles analyzing the bias ratings of prominent news outlets and summarizing politician viewpoints from the primary debates. Data entry: provided analyst ratings on how conservative or liberal each politician's stance is on different topics. What surprised you the most? I was surprised by how much effort and detail go into analyzing and determining the bias and factual accuracy of news articles. The process requires a deep understanding of both the political spectrum and journalistic standards, which I hadn't fully appreciated before. I was also surprised by how widespread bias is in news articles and outlets, reminding me to always be aware of bias when I absorb information online.
takeaways: I learned how to discern subtle biases in news articles and gained a deeper appreciation for the accuracy and impartiality in journalism. I also gained a comprehensive understanding of the core tenants of conservative and liberal thought and or policy making.

Most of the labs done in 1B Bio SL nd HL are provided by SEP; I packed a few for Interlake this yearl Unique experiences Ladder project • Self-directed project testing the findings of a research paper to determine if a cheaper DNA ladder could replace expensive commercial ladders currently used by SEP. location: Interlake High School Internship description Being a teaching assistant for biology entails setting up labs and organizing lab equipment, working closely with the biology teachers, organizing and creating lesson plans, managing and overseeing classes, grading, making copies of classroom materiels necessary for certain activities, etc. The teachers really appreciate the help, as there is a lot of work that they do behind the scenes that we don't know about. The hours depend on your mentor, but usually it includes 6th and 7th period throughout the week where you will be tasked with various items. Although it doesn't seem important* it is a great help to the teachers and it gets you experience with materials end procedures you may need for the future. It also puts a different perspective an learning which can be applicable to your own work. Unique experiences Learning how to utilize the copy machine Being able to evaluate student work Basic biology knowledge to setup labs and procedures Learning how to create assignments for students to optimize their learning

location: Seattle (307 Westlake AVE) Internship description At the Vaughan Lab in the Seattle Children's Research Institute, Center for Global Infectious Disease, the primary focus is research into malaria through the use genetic recombination with the ultimate goa of alleviating the suffering of those infected. My role was largely that of a research assistant, helping the research scientists with their experiments, recording data, and creating data tables to be published. My work primarily dealt with the P. falciparum strand, genetic crosses of the lab-strain with wild-strains from across Africa (Sudan, Mali, and Malawi), end developing a vaccine that is more representative of malaria in the field. By measuring and comparing the effectiveness of these hybrid crosses at infecting the liver, we sought to determine which hybrid is most suitable and effective for vaccine development. Unique experiences Hear from scientists that speak at global conferences. Work with human malaria and humanized mouse livers through laboratory procedures such as PCR, IFA, microscopy etc. Analyze different research papers and learn more about publication through first-hand experience.
description: At the Vaughan Lab in the Seattle Children's Research Institute, Center for Global Infectious Disease, the primary focus is research into malaria through the use genetic recombination with the ultimate goa of alleviating the suffering of those infected. My role was largely that of a research assistant, helping the research scientists with their experiments, recording data, and creating data tables to be published. My work primarily dealt with the P. falciparum strand, genetic crosses of the lab-strain with wild-strains from across Africa (Sudan, Mali, and Malawi), end developing a vaccine that is more representative of malaria in the field. unique_experiences: Hear from scientists that speak at global conferences. Work with human malaria and humanized mouse livers through laboratory procedures such as PCR, IFA, microscopy etc. Analyze different research papers and learn more about publication through first-hand experience.
surprised: I was surprised about how much of a global effort this research is. We were working with teams from across the U.S (Johns Hopkins, Harvard, Notre Dame, UT Austin), Thailand, Germany, Japan, Australia, and more in this effort to fight malaria. Hearing from the scientists about the global conferences was especially interesting as I got to learn more about what it is like to work in the research field outside of the wet lab.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway is the importance of organization and precision, especially in the field of research where so many factors are at play in an experiment. With good organization and precision, experiments and overall work endeavors are heavily facilitated and the ease in which work can be looked back upon and referenced is increased. I also learned many different procedures and wet lab skills, which I will be sure to use in my future endeavors with biomedical research.

organization: University of Washington, Department of Atmospheric Sciences
description: Worked with Professor Dale Durran and the Deep Learning Weather Prediction group to build a tool to enable quick conversion between file-formats necessary for model training and inference, and paper publication.
unique_experiences: A uniquely free hand in identifying points of improvement and selecting directions of approach to the problem. Under-the-hood look at group dynamics in small-group research efforts at the university level. Real Impact in producing usable tools, saving anywhere between 1/2 — 3 hours per file conversion.
surprised: The very relaxed and open nature of group meetings was a surprise. I was also surprised by how warmly I was welcomed as a high-schooler and trusted to complete complex projects in a timely fashion.
takeaways: My greatest takeaway from this internship experience is that speedy project development hinges on open dialogue with potential users of the product. A large number of obstacles in my project's work were overcome by talking to users and obtaining a clear understanding of the problem at hand, in turn facilitating development via reducing the generality of the solutio n.

organization: University of Washington Civil and Environmental Engineering Department
location: University of Washington Seattle I nternship Description At the UW's CEE department, I have been a part ofthe PLACE(E) lab, which is led by my mentor, Or. Bethany Gordon. During lab meetings, we worked on graduate students' research projects as well as on developing a group research project for the lab. My personal projects for the lab included helping with literature reviews, creating Instagram posts about research papers by our lab members, and auditing Dr. Gordon's Behavioral Design class at the IJW. Unique experiences Learning about the research process by actMely participating in it. Networking by attending department events and lectures at IJW. Being able to learn about the inner workings of academia and gain experience in a college environment before going to college.

takeaways: Being able to experience the research process at a university firsthand helped me to get a head start for college. Additionally, learning about the impacts that engineers' decisions have on local communities at the PLACE(E) lab and in my mentods class has been a formative experience and in my future as an engineering major I will continue to apply these takeaways to promote equity within the field.

location: South Lake Union Internship description I was a computational biology intern at the Hood Lab, where I analyzed results from the Coaching for Cognition in Alzheimer's (COCOA) trial using computational and statistical tools. I specifically investigated the effect of personalized intervention/treatment on the relationships between blood pressure, blood analytes, and outcomes in COCOA participants using R programming language for data visualization and analysis. I also used Python to organize medical data and extract meaningful information. At the end of the internship, I published my results in a research poster which was presented to scientists and researchers at the annual ISB Retreat in Suncedla, Washington. Unique experiences Presenting (to a group of scientists} my regression plots on mean arterial blood pressure vs. days in COCOA trial for both intervention and control arms Writing Python scripts to perform data manipulation tasks such as merging thousands of columns, filtering by trial arm, sorting by various columns, and converting dates to numeric values Discovering a strong correlation between systolic blood pressure and the homocysteine analyte in one of my longitudinal studies

I specifically investigated the effect of personalized intervention/treatment on the relationships between blood pressure, blood analytes, and outcomes in COCOA participants using R programming language for data visualization and analysis. I also used Python to organize medical data and extract meaningful information. unique_experiences: Presenting (to a group of scientists} my regression plots on mean arterial blood pressure vs. days in COCOA trial for both intervention and control arms Writing Python scripts to perform data manipulation tasks such as merging thousands of columns, filtering by trial arm, sorting by various columns, and converting dates to numeric values Discovering a strong correlation between systolic blood pressure and the homocysteine analyte in one of my longitudinal studies
surprised: I was surprised bv how much time and effort my mentor willingly put in to equip me with the skills, tools, and resources needed to conduct data analysis of the COCOA trial, end-to-end. At the same time, I was given the freedom to explore datasets and attend meetings/discussions to enhance my understanding of Alzheimer's disease.
takeaways: I learned that research in the real world is ambiguous and the journey to discovering something new (or building on existing ideas) doesn't always fol ow a linear path. ae curious, be persistent, and most importantly, enjoy the process! Mentor's name Jared Roach Mentor's email jroach@systemsbiology.org

organization: IJW Bothell Gravitational Astronomy Research Team
description: Gravitational waves were predicted by Albert Einstein as a direct consequence of his theory of general relativity. In this research group, LIWB faculty and students work across the spectrum of gravitational wave astronomy including work in data analysis, astrophysics, and parameter estimation. Ongoing research projects include Bayesian analysis, pulsar search, astrophysical parameter estimation, glitch reconstruction, and source modeling. I focused on the Pulsar Science Collaboratory (PSC). After a 6-week training course, I was able to get access to radio data from the Green Bank telescope and analyze the data for new, undiscovered pulsars.
unique_experiences: Conducting real radio astronomy by analyzing radio data from the Green Bank telescope Learning about PhD and facu ty research projects by attending weekly debriefs Taking courses to understand the code that helps LIGO/LISA work
surprised: I was surprised that projects conducted by PhD students and faculty were accessible and understandable to undergraduate and high students with little to no experience. It helped me understand that effective communication is really important to be a good scientist.
takeaways: This was my first opportunity to conduct "real" astronomy/astrophvsics research outside of a school/hypotheticel setting. My greatest takeaway was learning how important it is to make sure science is accessible to everyone. I look forward to continuing to conduct astronomy/astrophysics research next year in college. organization: UW-Bothell Gravitational Wave Astronomy (GWA) Research Group
location: UW-Bothell (meeting area can vary) + remote (independent or Zoom) Internship description IJW-Bothell's GWA Research Group covers several research endeavors of GWA, the main three research collaborations including LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory), LISA (Laser Interferometer Space Antenna), & NANOGrav (North American Nanohertz Observatory for Gravitational Waves). I worked on reading and taking notes on several recommended sources, learning to start working through physics worksheets involving gravitational wave concepts, how online gravitational wave simulations work and what observations can be made from real data/grading pulsar plots, and more. There is opportunity to connect with a lot of college students and profs, plus access to Discord, Slack, etc, where you can choose different GWA divisions to involve yourself in, as well as opportunities to visit different GWA labs, attend conferences if interested, etc. Very self-paced/self-guided too and many resources offered. Unique experiences Hands-on data analysis — using special software/techniques to analyze direct data from observatories Collaborative research projects — opportunity to assist college students/profs with specific research and even contribute some of your ideas in their papers Technical skill development — developing technical skills in programming and simulation — Python, MATLAB, etc.

I worked on reading and taking notes on several recommended sources, learning to start working through physics worksheets involving gravitational wave concepts, how online gravitational wave simulations work and what observations can be made from real data/grading pulsar plots, and more. surprised: What surprised me the most is how extensive the research in gravitational wave astronomy is — it makes you realize that there are ways, way more unfamiliar concepts in astronomy than the ones that we already have a lot of research on, but this internship gives access to so many resources that can help us learn and make progress. I was also surprised by the very interdisciplinary approach to the research, as not only is there just physics and astronomy, but also cs/machine learning, math, and engineering aspects.
takeaways: My internship at the UW-Bothell GWA Research Group showed me how we can translate classroom subjects into real-world research. By asking questions and being proactive, I learned the importance of curiosity and initiative in a professional setting. I also discovered the value of mentorship/talking to experienced people for help. These things helped me get more out of my time with the internship than I otherwise would have. The self-pacing also motivated me to improve time management and find solutions to my questions.

organization: UW Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
location: Remote Internship description Dr. Anant is a professor who focuses on nanoscale materials and quantum mechanics. He is especially knowledgeable within the areas of physics and electrical engineering. He has many projects that he is currently working on that future interns may be able to help him on, although it is unlikely that one would be able to help with his new research directly, as it is very advanced. Dr. Anant did not know much about and wanted to learn more. During the second part of the year, I took that research and began compiling it into an easily readable document, which has a chance to be published at the end of the year. Overall, Dr. Anant is very smart and kind mentor who is also very accommodating to one's schedule. Unique experiences Self-Driven Research on Thermodynamics, Micro-Electronics, and Physics Writing and Potentially Publishing a Paper Explaining Heat Dissipation in Electronics Working with a UW Professor and Learning the Research Process

surprised: I was surprised both by simply how much information is available online and by how obscure some information is to find. Few people compile information in a useful way, and often the desired info is produced as a side product of a study or locked behind significant paywalls. This makes understanding or even finding the desired information much more difficult than one would assume.
takeaways: One of the major takeaways that I had is that good communication allows for a better functioning workflow and fixes many issues. Another major takeaway is to plan for more time than one might imagine, with a tight and clear plan and a limited scope to help. As I saw with my work, things often take much longer than one might imagine for various reasons, which an insufficient plan certainly augments. These are major lessons that I will certainly use in college. Mentoffs Name : Dr. MP Anant Mentor's email: anantmp@uw.edu 64

organization: University of Washington Mechanical Engineering Department
location: UW MEB/Remote Internship description Professor Vashisth's lab conducts interdisciplinary research on various subjects pertaining to mechanics and material science. The lab has four core areas of research: sustainable advance composites, bio-inspired composites, atomistic simulations, and rapid manufacturing, which is the one I worked in. The project's main ocus was finding different, more efficient ways to use radio frequency heating to form composites out of carbonaceous materials and manipulate their properties. I primarily worked with a senior ME undergrad tudent and spent my time investigating research papers + learning to devise lab-setups, modeling + printing ools for the lab, and shadowing. Unique experiences Learning to model with CAD software (Onshape) + access to 3D printers Shadowing in the lab, learning to create experimental setups, experience with lab practices Sharing and discussing personal research
